Year 1 10% \-20% Year 2 20% \-35% Year 3 30% \-50% Year 4 40% \-60% Year 5 50% \-70% 

*Strums* (second cartoon) limit 20,000 10,000 Ratings estimated likelihood 

6–7 20% 10% 7–8 50% 10% 8–9 10% 10% 9–10 10% 50% 10–11 10% 20% Alternative deal value 3,000,000 2,500,000

***Sample***   
Participants in the Brazilian and Japanese data sets were executives par ticipating in a negotiation-training program in the United States.Participants in the French, Russian, and U.S. data sets were MBA students who negoti ated Cartoon as the first exercise in a course or workshop on negotiation. Participants in the Hong Kong Chinese data set were undergraduate stu dents who negotiated Cartoon as the first negotiation exercise in a course. 

Participants in the Brazilian and one of the classes in the U.S. data set had an average age of 38 years; participants in the Japanese, Russian, French and one class of the U.S. data set averaged 27 years of age; partici pants in the Hong Kong Chinese data set were younger, averaging 23 years. There were no significant differences in cultural values or norms between the two U.S. data sets, so these samples were combined for all data  

analyses.   
The study draws on transcripts for 139 same-culture dyads: 9 French, 45 Russian, 22 Japanese, 16 Hong Kong, 7 Brazilian, and 40 U.S. 

**96** *Adair, Brett, Lempereur, Okumura, Shikhirev, Tinsley, and Lytle Culture and Negotiation Strategy*   
***Procedure***   
After a standard introduction to the negotiation exercise, participants com pleted a cultural value survey and then were given role assignments and confidential information for their roles. Participants were paired into same role dyads for a one-hour preparation session during which researchers  

circulated and were available for questions.   
Then, participants were assigned to buyer–seller pairs and given one and one-half hours to negotiate. Negotiation dyads were assigned so that no two buyers who prepared together were paired with any two sellers who prepared together.The negotiation sessions were audiotaped. 

After the negotiation, participants completed a form describing their agreement as well as questionnaires about their preparation and negotia tion sessions.Then, results were shown to the group and participants were debriefed. 

***Measures***   
The independent variable was culture: U.S., French, Russian, Japanese, Hong Kong Chinese, and Brazilian. Brett et al. (1998) report the between-culture differences on cultural values, norms for negotiation, and power expecta tions in negotiation that were used to develop hypotheses. 

The dependent variables were constructed by coding the transcripts of the negotiations. By coding, we mean categorizing direct information, indirect information,and power strategies represented in negotiators’actual recorded conversations, according to the following criteria: 

*Direct Information-Sharing Strategies* 

•Preferences and priorities about an issue or offer 

• Comparisons and contrasts, marked by a reference to similarities or  differences between the parties 

• Reactions including direct positive, neutral, or negative reactions to  preferences, offers, or proposals 

*Indirect Information-Sharing Strategies* 

•Offers including single-issue, multi-issue, and multi-issue with trade-offs. *Power Strategies* 

• Informational persuasion,consisting of convincing with information and argument 

• Informational persuasion with sanctions, consisting of persuasion with references to BATNA and/or reservation price 

• Legitimacy, including information about one’s own company or  competitors 

*Negotiation Journal January 2004* **97**  
For example, consider the following exchange: 

**Seller:** O.K. As I said before,this program \[Ultra Rangers\] has been extremely successful. You can sell related goods. It’s got a great value.We would like to ask for 7 million. 

**Buyer:** Seven million.That’s not cheap. I would like you to under stand that it’s risky for us. It’s investing in something based on faith. How about Strums? I heard about that.And I wanted to con sider that program. 

In this exchange, the seller’s speaking turn was coded as informational per suasion (“the program has been extremely successful” and “it’s got great value”) and a single-issue offer (“7 million”).The buyer’s speaking turn was coded as a negative reaction (“that’s not cheap”), informational persuasion (“it’s risky for us”), and a question about a single-issue (“how about Strums?”). A summary of the codes with some additional examples appears in Appendix One. 

Hypotheses One through Five were tested by analyzing the relative fre quency of use for each dependent variable in each culture. In other words, controlling for the fact that some dyads negotiated longer than others (dif ferent transcript length) and some people talked more than others (differ ent numbers and lengths of speaking turns),we tested whether each culture group used direct information sharing, indirect information sharing, and power strategies more or less than the other culture groups. 

**Results** 

The results across the six cultures are presented visually in Figures One through Nine. Note that in these figures, the zero-axis indicates perfect pro portionality, that is, no cultural differences. Bars above the zero-axis indicate a strategy occurring with a frequency greater than we would expect if there were no cultural differences. Bars below the zero-axis indicate a strategy occurring with a frequency less than we would expect if there were no cul tural differences. Empirical results of hypotheses tests are presented in the “Notes”section.1 

Hypothesis One predicted that U.S. negotiators would use more direct communication strategies than negotiators from the other five cultures. A loglinear analysis comparing the U.S. to the other five cultures revealed sup port for the hypothesis: relative to the other five cultures, U.S. negotiators were more likely to share information directly with respect to preferences and priorities, comparisons and contrasts, and direct reactions. 

From Figure One, it can be seen that Brazilian negotiators had a greater residual value than U.S. negotiators for statements about preferences and priorities. This means that contrary to our expectations, Brazilian negotia tors were more likely to use this strategy than U.S.negotiators.Our hypothe ses tested the U.S. versus all other cultures lumped together, which explains

our significant finding despite high levels of preferences and priorities in the Brazilian sample. Figures Two and Three clearly illustrate that U.S. nego tiators were more likely than the other five culture groups to make com parisons and contrasts as well as direct reactions. 

Hypothesis Two predicted that Russian and French negotiators would discuss preferences and priorities more than Hong Kong Chinese, Japanese, and Brazilian negotiators. This hypothesis was not supported, and in fact high levels of preferences and priorities in the Brazilian sample led to a sig nificant effect in the opposite direction. Another look at Figure One shows that Russian, French, and Japanese negotiators were the least likely to discuss preferences and priorities. 

Hypothesis Three predicted that Russian, Japanese, and Hong Kong Chinese negotiators were more likely to use indirect communication strate gies than French, Brazilian, and U.S. negotiators. A loglinear analysis com paring Russia, Japan, and Hong Kong to Brazil, France, and the U.S. revealed support for the hypothesis: relative to the other three cultures, Russian, Japanese, and Hong Kong Chinese negotiators were more likely to share information indirectly through offers. As indicated in Figure Four, Russian and Japanese negotiators were extremely likely and U.S. negotiators were extremely unlikely to exchange information indirectly through offers, while Hong Kong Chinese did not use indirect communication as often as expected.

Hypothesis Four predicted that Russian and Japanese negotiators would use power strategies more frequently than the Hong Kong Chinese and Brazil ians.This hypothesis was supported with respect to informational persuasion (Figure Five) and informational persuasion with sanctions (Figure Six),but not for legitimacy,which showed a trend in the opposite direction (Figure Seven). 

Russian and Japanese negotiators were more likely than negotiators from the other four cultures to use power strategies in terms of reference to BATNA or reservation price (informational persuasion with sanctions) and persuasive argument (informational persuasion). Contrary to our pre diction, Russian negotiators were the least likely to discuss company, competitors, and relationships (legitimacy) as a power tactic. 

Hypothesis Five predicted that Hong Kong Chinese and Brazilian nego tiators were more likely to use power strategies than French and U.S. nego tiators.A loglinear analysis comparing Brazil and Hong Kong to France and the U.S. revealed support for the hypothesis with respect to informational persuasion: Brazilian and Hong Kong Chinese negotiators were more likely to use informational persuasion than French and U.S. negotiators (Figure Five). Brazilian and Hong Kong Chinese negotiators also referred to BATNA and reservation price as well as company, competitors, and relationships more frequently than U.S. negotiators but less frequently than French nego tiators, so the hypothesis was not supported with respect to informational persuasion with sanctions or legitimacy.

Exploratory analyses revealed that U.S. negotiators were much more likely and Russian negotiators were much less likely than the other culture groups to ask questions (Figure Eight) and exchange general information about the product (Figure Nine) during the negotiation. 

While we did not hypothesize about differences across cultures on ques tions or general information exchange because these are basic negotiation behaviors enacted to establish a common understanding of what is being negotiated, the fact that U.S. negotiators asked more questions and talked about general information relatively more than the other cultures could be further evidence of direct communication strategies supporting Hypothesis One.These negotiators may have been attempting to make sure that there was an explicit, common understanding of the situation before tackling the problem. 

**Discussion: Negotiating Joint Gains** 

Brett et al. (1998) reported differential joint gains for the six cultures in this sample. By looking at behavioral frequencies of negotiation strategies, this study offers insight into why some cultures maximized joint gains and others did not. 

***High Joint Gains: U.S. and Japan***   
Brett et al. (1998) report that U.S. and Japanese negotiators maximized joint gains. Our analysis of negotiation behaviors reveals that these groups took two different paths to joint gains. U.S. negotiators used a lot of direct

**104** *Adair, Brett, Lempereur, Okumura, Shikhirev, Tinsley, and Lytle Culture and Negotiation Strategy*   
information exchange and asked numerous questions. Presumably, this  open exchange of information allowed negotiators to identify trade-offs and expand the pie. Japanese negotiators frequently used indirect information exchange and persuasion with intermediate amounts of questions and general information exchange. Japanese negotiators inferred the priority information necessary for trade-offs from offers, justifying their offers with persuasive arguments and occasional direct communication. 

***Medium Joint Gains: Brazil and France***   
Brett et al. (1998) report that Brazilian and French negotiators achieved intermediate joint gains. Our analysis of negotiation behaviors helps to understand why French negotiators did not maximize joint gains but leaves some questions regarding the Brazilian negotiators. Brazilian negotiators  

frequently shared information on preferences and priorities, but they did not exchange other forms of direct information and made extremely few offers. It is unclear why information on preferences and priorities was not enough to identify trade-offs and maximize joint gains for Brazilian  

negotiators.   
French negotiators spent their time establishing context. They ex changed general information on the products and they referenced their  own company and competitors.They may have spent too much time clari fying the situation and not enough time identifying issues and priorities. French negotiators displayed extremely low frequencies of offers and  persuasion. 

***Low Joint Gains: Russia and Hong Kong***   
Brett et al. (1998) report that Russian and Hong Kong Chinese negotiators generated the lowest joint gains of the six cultures studied. Our analysis of negotiation behaviors suggests that joint gains suffered for these groups because Hong Kong negotiators did not exchange enough information and Russian negotiators were too focused on power. 

Similar to the Japanese,Russian negotiators exchanged many offers and frequently used informational persuasion and references to BATNA. The  Russians differed from the Japanese because frequencies of other kinds of communication were extremely low. In contrast, the Japanese negotiators displayed intermediate frequencies of direct information exchange: asking questions and discussing general information. It seems that Russian negotiators focused on power and positioning to the exclusion of infor mation necessary to expand the pie. This kind of negotiating may have  been frustrating and confrontational, explaining a tendency to close  prematurely. 

Hong Kong Chinese negotiators used average levels of most behaviors measured. One exception was their frequent use of persuasion, suggesting a positional focus that may have prevented the identification of trade-offs. They also infrequently responded with direct reactions, suggesting that offers may have been proposed without parties making a commitment or 

*Negotiation Journal January 2004* **105**  
moving forward. Because Hong Kong Chinese negotiators did not share much direct or indirect information, there may not have been enough infor mation revealed to identify trade-offs. As with Russia, the Hong Kong Chinese data suggest a rather frustrating, ambiguous atmosphere that may have contributed to premature closure. 

***Summary***   
Pruitt (1981) proposes that maximizing joint gains depends on getting enough information out on the table, through either direct or indirect methods. Our data suggest that negotiators need to exchange information directly (e.g.,U.S.negotiators) or they need to combine indirect information with some other form of direct communication (e.g.,Japanese negotiators). 

The French and Hong Kong Chinese negotiators simply did not exchange enough information. The Russian negotiators exchanged offers but did not balance the offers and a strong power focus with other forms of information exchange.The Brazilian negotiators suggest that direct infor mation on preferences and priorities alone does not provide enough infor mation to maximize joint gains. Perhaps negotiators also need to use direct reactions, like in the U.S., to move negotiations forward. 

**Classifying the Cultures** 

We can infer low/high context communication styles and power prefer ences from our behavioral data on direct and indirect information exchange and power-based influence. These inferences help to classify negotiation norms for the six culture groups. 

***Low Context, Low Power: U.S. and Brazil***   
The U.S. negotiators were clearly low-context and low-power as we origi nally hypothesized. They focused entirely on direct information exchange to the exclusion of indirect information and power-based influence. 

Brazilian negotiators were extremely high in one form of direct infor mation exchange and extremely low in indirect information exchange, sug gesting that they relied primarily on low-context communication.They also did not use extreme amounts of any power behaviors, suggesting they were not particularly focused on power.The data clarifies prior descriptions of Brazil as both low- and high-power, but does contradict prior classifications of Brazil as high-context.The data may portray a Brazilian business culture, where low-context and low-power North American norms are becoming more prevalent. 

***High Context, High Power: Japan and Russia***   
Our behavioral data confirm prior accounts of Japan and Russia as high context and high-power.Why these two cultures had such different negoti ation outcomes is a provocative question for further inquiry.We mentioned that Russian negotiators did not exchange direct or general information. 

**106** *Adair, Brett, Lempereur, Okumura, Shikhirev, Tinsley, and Lytle Culture and Negotiation Strategy*  
They may also have exchanged primarily single-issue offers, which do not convey as much information as multi-issue offers. In combination with the low norms for information exchange reported by Brett et al. (1998), this may have contributed to a much lower level of information and under standing than for the Japanese negotiators. 

***Uncertain: France and Hong Kong***   
Our data does not confirm prior empirical and descriptive accounts of Hong Kong as a high-context, high-power culture.The Hong Kong Chinese nego tiators did not display particularly large frequencies of indirect information exchange or power-based influence. One possible explanation is that the Hong Kong negotiators were undergraduates lacking the work and negoti ation experience of the other participants. 

Consistent with other accounts of France,we found no clear indicators of whether the French negotiators were low- or high-power. Intermediate frequencies of direct information exchange and low frequencies of indirect information exchange suggest that French negotiators were more low- than high-context.But,clear conclusions cannot be drawn from our data.It seems that, as with their negotiation outcome, French negotiators’ reliance on context and power is intermediate. 

***Summary***   
Our data show that, for the most part, negotiation strategies support the tra ditional classification of cultures based on values and norms (Hofstede 1980; Schwartz 1994; Hall 1976). Negotiators from Brazil and the U.S. used pri marily direct information exchange and avoided power-based influence. Negotiators from Japan and Russia used primarily indirect information exchange and power-based influence. Negotiators from France used a mixture of behaviors that confirm descriptions of France as an enigma.Only negotiators from Hong Kong did not use behaviors that were consistent with cultural descriptions.The mixed negotiation outcomes across culture types are consistent with the findings of Brett et al. (1998) that culture type is not directly related to joint gains. 

**Contribution** 

Our findings indicate a direct relationship between information exchange and joint gains in the six cultures. In a negotiation with integrative poten tial, getting information out on the table is critical to identify priorities and trade-offs. Our data suggest that relying on context alone to convey infor mation is not enough. Even high-context negotiators must use some direct information exchange to generate joint gains. 

The data also suggests that exchanging information on preferences and priorities alone is not enough. Other types of essential direct information exchange are comparisons and contrasts to identify trade-offs and direct reactions to keep the negotiation moving forward. 

*Negotiation Journal January 2004* **107**  
We found that power-based strategies were not directly related to joint gains in the six cultures. Japanese negotiators were able to use power-based influence and generate joint gains, but Russian and Hong Kong Chinese negotiators were unable to do so. It seems that power-based influence does not hinder joint gains in a culture with values and norms for power when there is sufficient information exchange. In this case, negotiators success fully enlarge the pie and use power-based influence to position and claim more. If power-based influence is used without sufficient information exchange, negotiators get stuck in positional bargaining and are unable to generate joint gains. 

Our findings suggest that understanding the other parties’ negotiation strategies can help negotiators direct the negotiation process to facilitate the generation of joint gains.One way to understand the other party’s strate gies is to learn how the culture is classified in terms of values and norms and infer the strategies and behaviors likely to be used. 

By understanding how the other party is likely to share information, a negotiator can strategically plan to exchange information to identify prior ities and trade-offs. By understanding whether the other party is likely to use power-based influence, a negotiator can strategically plan how to inte grate or avoid power tactics that may or may not be normative for the other party. 

**Future Directions** 

The next step in this research is to compare how differential use of these negotiation strategies relates to negotiation outcomes in intercultural nego tiation. As opposed to looking at culture as having a main effect on negoti ation strategies and outcome as we have done here, we also would like to look at how culture moderates the outcome effects associated with differ ent strategies in different cultures. For example,Tinsley and Pillutla (1998) show that culture moderates the outcome effects of power scripts in U.S. and Hong Kong negotiations. Because we have not found values, norms, or strategies other than certain forms of information exchange directly related to joint gains in the six cultures, looking at culture as a moderator may help to explain differential joint gains across cultures. 

**Notes** 

The research reported here was supported by a grant from the Dispute Resolution Research Center of Northwestern University. The authors would like to thank Kwok Leung for his valuable com ments on this project. 

**1\.** Empirical results of hypotheses tests:   
Hypothesis One (supported): U.S. negotiators were more likely than negotiators in the other five cultures to share information directly with respect to preferences and priorities \[c2 (1, 138\) \= 5.85, *P* \< 0.01\], comparisons and contrasts \[c2 (1, 138\) \= 69.76, *P* \< 0.01\], and direct reactions  \[c2 (1, 138\) \= 57.28, *P* \< 0.01\]. 

**108** *Adair, Brett, Lempereur, Okumura, Shikhirev, Tinsley, and Lytle Culture and Negotiation Strategy*  
Hypothesis Two (not supported): Russian and French negotiators did not discuss preferences and priorities more than Hong Kong Chinese, Japanese, and Brazilian negotiators. Hypothesis Three (supported): Russian, Japanese, and Hong Kong Chinese negotiators were more likely than U.S.,French,and Brazilian negotiators to share information indirectly through offers   
\[c2 (1, 138\) \= 434.55, *P* \< 0.01\].   
Hypothesis Four (partially supported): Russian and Japanese negotiators were more likely than Hong Kong Chinese and Brazilian negotiators to use informational persuasion with sanctions    
\[c2 (1, 138\) \= 20.15, *P* \< 0.01\] and informational persuasion \[c2 (1, 138\) \= 65.40, *P* \< 0.01\]. Hypothesis Five (partially supported):Brazilian and Hong Kong Chinese negotiators were more   
likely to use informational persuasion than French and U.S. negotiators \[c2 (1, 138\) \= 38.78, *P* \< 0.01\].

Culture and International Marketing: A Sub-Saharan   
African Context 

William K. Darley   
Denise J. Luethge   
Charles Blankson 

Downloaded by \[University of North Texas\] at 10:56 25 October 2013   
**ABSTRACT.** The authors review and extend the culture literature in international marketing by dis cussing some of the cultural underpinnings pertaining to the sub-Saharan African context. With the use of inductive reasoning, the authors offer several key normative suggestions for international mar keting practices. Marketers should focus not only on the end user but also on those who significantly influence the final decision. Sub-Saharan African culture is characterized by long, drawn-out negoti ation processes and by contracts and overall sales targets that likely will be dissimilar to those from countries with a linear view of time. Age is relevant when selecting people for negotiation assignments and hiring sales and managerial staff. Because of the importance of “respect,” messages should focus on an individual’s need for belongingness rather than on the individual striving for achievement. The authors advocate that advertising appeals should focus on the society and on the individual as a member of the society and not on individual self-enhancement. Context and nonverbal communications are important in personal selling. It is better to establish social trust first in negotiations, personal selling, and buyer–seller interaction. 

**KEYWORDS.** Culture, international marketing, sub-Saharan Africa 

***INTRODUCTION*** 

There is consensus in the literature that culture has a fundamental influence on mar keting and business practices, and executives are expected to be more receptive to cul tural differences especially in the interna tional marketplace (Johnson, Elliot, & Grier, 2010; Kalliny, Saran, Ghanem, & Fisher, 2011; 

Kaynak, Kara, & Apil, 2011). Unfortunately, repeated cultural clashes abound, and strategic harmony among partners from different cultures has proved to be illusory (Strutton & Pelton, 1997). Clearly, international marketing execu tives’ cultural competencies must be mastered in the international marketplace (Cateora, Gilly, & Graham, 2011; Cavusgil, Knight, & Riesen berger, 2012). 

William K. Darley, PhD, is affiliated with the Department of Marketing, Faculty of Economics and Management, King Abdulaziz University, Jeddah, Saudi Arabia. Denise J. Luethge, PhD, is affiliated with the Department of Management, Haile/U.S. Bank College of Business, University of Northern Kentucky, Highland Heights, Kentucky, USA, and Institute for Technology, Enterprise and Competitiveness, Doshisha Business School, Doshisha University, Kyoto, Japan. Charles Blankson, PhD, is affiliated with the Department of Marketing and Logistics, College of Business, University of North Texas, Denton, Texas, USA. 

Address correspondence to Charles Blankson, Department of Marketing and Logistics, College of Business, University of North Texas, 1167 Union Circle \#311396, Denton, TX 76203-1396, USA. charles.blankson@unt.edu 

188   
***Darley, Luethge, and Blankson 189*** 

In an age of growing interdependence where   
Western, Chinese, and Indian companies alike   
are rapidly moving to create more business op   
portunities in sub-Saharan Africa (Churchwell,   
2003), a greater sensitivity to African culture   
should prove beneficial to the bottom-line of   
companies. Hence, a more refined understand   
ing of African cultural realities should facilitate   
marketing practices in the African marketplace   
(Johnson et al., 2010). Given the underlying dif   
ferences among cultures throughout the world,   
understanding and appreciating the similarities   
and differences of cultures in the context of in   
ternational marketing become necessary (Grif   
Downloaded by \[University of North Texas\] at 10:56 25 October 2013   
fith, Cavusgil, & Xu, 2008; Kaynak et al., 2011;   
Yaprak, 2008). Huff and Smith (2008, p. 179\) as   
sert that “as markets become increasingly global,   
an understanding of the world’s cultures is im   
perative.”   
The international business and marketing lit   
erature still lacks sustained studies delving into   
international marketing deliberations in Africa   
and the subsequent role of culture (Johnson   
et al., 2010). Because of the dearth of knowl   
edge of African culture, a greater representa   
tion of African culture in the international mar   
keting literature would add to the richness and   
relevance of marketing practices in the global   
marketplace (Babarinde, 2009; Soares, Farhang   
mehr, & Shoham, 2007; Yang, Wang, & Su,   
2006; Yasin, 1996).   
Thus, the aim here is to discuss the cultural   
underpinnings of Africa that will provide an un   
derstanding of consumption context. Following   
the suggestions of marketing scholars, including   
Paul, Roy, and Mukhopadhyay (2006), Yaprak   
(2008), Laroche (2007), Huff and Smith (2008),   
and Soares et al. (2007), first, the objectives are   
to review Hofstede (1980, 1983, 1991), House,   
Hanges, Javidan, Dorfman, and Gupta (2004),   
and Hall (1989) as a backdrop to reveal a syn   
optic view of the African culture. To that end,   
this review adheres to Huff and Smith’s (2008,   
p. 182\) observation that “little research seems to   
be drawing from sources such as House, Hanges,   
Javidan, Dorfman, & Gupta’s (2004) GLOBE   
study” in cultural studies. Huff and Smith note   
that cultural researchers would benefit by draw   
ing from other sources in addition to the popu   
lar dimensions of Hofstede (1980). Second, in 

line with Soares et al., we summarize insights from the cultural dimensions and then discuss their implications for international marketing practices. Third, following Strutton and Pelton (1997), on the basis of literature review, partici pant observations, and personal experiences, we suggest normative guidelines covering informa tion sources, brand preferences, advertising ap peals, sales management, personal selling, and negotiations. 

This conceptual article thus contributes to the marketing literature by (a) reacting to re quests for continuous studies examining the impact of culture on marketing strategies (see Kaynak et al., 2011; Laroche, 2007; Marketing Science Institute \[MSI\], 1998–2000); (b) re sponding to repeated calls for more research into the African marketplace (Ibeh & Debrah, 2011; Johnson et al., 2010; Kuada & Buatsi, 2005; Osei & Gbadamosi, 2011; Yang et al., 2006); and (c) addressing recent calls for more con ceptual and theoretical articles in the develop ment of knowledge in the marketing discipline (MacInnis, 2011). Analyzing 30 years of articles from major marketing journals, Yadav (2010) concludes that conceptual articles, the backbone of the development of knowledge in marketing, are declining, despite repeated calls for concep tual articles in the marketing discipline. 

Obviously, the adoption of marketing strate gies never occurs in cultural vacuums. Instead, strategies unfold within social contexts that en code values, beliefs, and acceptable patterns of behavior (Cayla & Arnold, 2008; Kaynak et al., 2011). The ability to address culture during the execution of marketing practices (e.g., branding, advertising, selling, pricing, positioning, cus tomer service) more effectively in Africa, par ticularly given the contrast between African and Western cultures, is an important research task. This task is fueling interests from academicians, practitioners, and policy-makers (Ibeh & De brah, 2011; Knowledge@Wharton, 2010; Kuada & Buatsi, 2005). 

Sub-Saharan Africa remains largely under researched and lags behind other regions in terms of global business and marketing discourse (Yang et al., 2006). This is not surprising, how ever, in that the literature is replete with publi cations that associate sub-Saharan Africa with   
***190 JOURNAL OF GLOBAL MARKETING*** 

poverty, underdevelopment, corruption, doom,   
and pestilence (Brown, 2000; Lepp, 2008; Osei   
& Gbadamosi, 2011). Notwithstanding the neg   
ative perceptions, there are exporting, franchis   
ing, and licensing opportunities, as well as joint   
venture and direct investment opportunities in   
agriculture, tourism, retailing, real estate, con   
struction, manufacturing, and extractive indus   
tries, that are attracting long-sighted foreign in   
vestors from China, India, and some Western   
countries to several African countries (Kasan   
ganayi, 2009; Osei & Gbadamosi) that should at   
tract multinational firms. Also, future prospects   
for the tourism industry are good as evidenced   
Downloaded by \[University of North Texas\] at 10:56 25 October 2013   
in research undertaken by Oyewole (2006). 

***A SYNOPTIC VIEW OF SUB-SAHARAN***   
***AFRICA*** 

In line with the extant literature, in this ar   
ticle, the term “Africa” is used interchange   
ably with sub-Saharan Africa and represents   
black Africans south of the Sahara (see Gyekye,   
2003; Ibeh & Debrah, 2011; Osei & Gbadamosi,   
2011). Thus, for the purposes of this article, the   
Arab countries of North Africa that are more   
often identified with the Middle East (House   
et al., 2004; Mellahi, Demirbag, & Riddle,   
2011; Quelch & Austin, 1993\) are excluded (see   
Kalliny, Saran, Ghanem, & Fisher, 2011).   
Kalliny, Saran, Ghanem, and Fisher (2011,   
p. 42\) note that the Arab world consists of 12   
Middle Eastern countries (Bahrain, Iraq, Jor   
dan, Kuwait, Lebanon, Oman, Palestine \[the   
Gaza Strip and West Bank\], Qatar, Saudi Ara   
bia, Syria, U.A.E., and Yemen) and 10 African   
countries (Algeria, Egypt, Eritrea, Djibouti,   
Libya, Mauritania, Morocco, Somalia, Sudan,   
and Tunisia). The northern part of Africa is   
much different culturally and politically than the   
middle and southern parts of Africa (i.e., black   
Africa, the focus of this article). Because culture   
determines business models and approaches in   
international markets (Gao, 2013), a contribu   
tion of this research is the exposition of the na   
ture of host culture that underpins multinational   
and international companies’ business models.   
Multinational and international companies are 

always in search of well-suited business models in foreign markets (Gao, 2013). 

Sub-Saharan Africa has an estimated 30% of the world’s mineral reserves, including 40% of the gold, 60% of the cobalt, and 90% of the platinum reserves. The projection is that 25% of U.S. oil imports will come from Africa by 2015 (Harsch, 1999). Yet, Africa remains the least-researched continent in emerging markets (Harsch, 1999; Yang et al., 2006). 

The World Bank reports that Africa’s steady economic growth, progress on Millennium De velopment Goals, and very favorable returns on investment give Africa “an unprecedented opportunity for transformation and sustained growth.” The World Bank concludes that “Africa is on the brink of an economic takeoff, much like China was 30 years ago, and India 20 years ago” (World Bank, 2010, p. 1). Also, Africa repre sents one of the few regions of the world still offering opportunities for outsized investment gains. Africa’s profitability is one of the best kept secrets in the world’s economy (Knowl edge@Wharton, 2010). 

The IMF forecasts that African economy will outpace its Asian counterpart (*Economist*, 2011\) and that Africa is by far the fastest-growing continent in terms of population. Sub-Saharan Africa’s current population of 856 million is a little more than Europe’s and a fifth of Asia’s. However, according to the *Economist*, by 2050 it could be three times that of Europe, and by 2100, might even be three-quarters of the size of Asia. 

In addition, political, economic, and legal policies toward foreign investment are now sim ilar to those in most emerging countries. This has resulted in foreign direct investment flows from $3.1 billion in 1988–1992 to $6 billion in 1993–1997. This is projected to rise to $40.8 bil lion in 2011 and $150 billion in 2015 (Ohuocha, 2011). Hence, the substantial political and eco nomic liberalization now under way in many African countries prompts a closer look at the business and marketing opportunities (Knowl edge@Wharton, 2010; Kuada & Buatsi, 2005). 

African culture differs from other cultures in the way Africans construct meanings, negoti ate social contexts, and make sense of their en vironment (Ahiauzu, 1986). Understandably, to propose a monolithic African culture may be   
***Darley, Luethge, and Blankson 191*** 

misleading because of strong national differ   
ences (Gannon, 2004). Mufune (2003) notes that   
even within African national states, a myriad   
of cultures and subcultures exists. Nonetheless,   
there are certain indigenous trends of thought,   
cultural influences, and value orientations that   
are shared by the majority of people in sub   
Sahara Africa (Edoho, 2001; Owusu-Frimpong,   
2008). Also, several African scholars argue   
for cultural relatedness across Africa (Gyekye,   
2003; Mbiti, 1990). The common modal charac   
teristics and value systems include a hierarchical   
social structure, the importance of kinship, pri   
macy of the group, time orientation (Safavi &   
Downloaded by \[University of North Texas\] at 10:56 25 October 2013   
Tweddell, 1990), and the driving norms of hu   
man interdependence, virtue of symbiosis, and   
reciprocity (Mangaliso, 2001). Despite the grad   
ual shift away from the traditional high collec   
tivism in urban areas because of economic hard   
ships and the influence of Western business and   
marketing practices (Kuada & Buatsi, 2005), the   
authors’ observation of the vast majority of sub   
Saharan Africans who live in small towns and   
rural communities reveals a rather slow pace of   
change in culture taking place. Cavusgil, Knight,   
and Riesenberger (2008, pp. 50–51) write that   
cultural values change at a “glacial pace” and   
that, despite convergence of economies and con   
sumption patterns, people usually hold tradi   
tional attitudes, values, and beliefs rooted in   
the history and core culture of the country in   
which they live. The present study concentrates   
on the traditional, core values, and attitudes un   
derpinning sub-Saharan African culture as prac   
ticed by the vast majority of the people. Thus,   
the discussion does not consider the changing   
nature of culture in cities/urban areas in sub   
Saharan Africa, although many urban residents   
possess many of these traditional values and be   
liefs (Owusu-Frimpong, 2008). 

***TAXONOMIES OF CULTURAL***   
***PATTERNS*** 

Hofstede’s (1991) taxonomy, Hall’s (1989)   
high- and low-context communication, and   
House et al.’s (2004) time orientation serve   
as the basis of African culture context. Hof   
stede’s (1980) taxonomy (i.e., power distance, 

individualism–collectivism, uncertainty avoid ance, and masculinity–femininity) is popular in marketing and allied literature. It (a) proves to be stable and meaningful in explaining cultural differences (Lustig & Koester, 1996; Pomerantz, 2004), (b) is accepted widely and serve as cri teria by which to interpret cultural differences (Cavusgil et al., 2008, 2012), and (c) is used widely to study cultural dynamics in interna tional marketing contexts (Hewett, Money, & Sharma, 2006; Paul, Roy, & Mukhopadhyay, 2006; Teng & Laroche, 2006; Soares et al., 2007). Time orientation is also important in cul tural explication because it affects decision mak ing, negotiations, and how people interact (see, for example, Kaynak et al., 2011). Moreover, Hall’s (1989) work on communication style has gained renewed attention because of the explo sion of business interaction between East Asia and the rest of the world (Cavusgil et al., 2008, 2012). 

***Cultural Dimensions*** 

*Power Distance* 

Cultures differ in the extent to which they view status inequality as good or bad and they describe how a society deals with the inequal ities in power that exist among people (Soares et al., 2007). Authority is inherent in one’s po sition within a hierarchy in high power distance cultures. There are strong dependency relation ships between parents and children, bosses and subordinates and a significant social distance be tween superior and subordinate. For low power distance cultures the opposite is true and the gaps between the powerful and weak are reduced. 

According to Hofstede (1991), most sub Saharan African cultures exhibit high power dis tance (Kiggundu, 1988; Munene, 1996). Respect for elders is an important guiding principle for behavior. Nowhere is power more evident than in the most senior member of the group. The elderly are seen as (a) the true repositories of wisdom and knowledge, (b) examples for the youth to emulate (Moemeka, 1996), and (c) the forbearers or gate-keepers of society. Individu als treat elders with deference, respect, and dig nity (Nnoruka, 1999). The respect for hierarchies   
***192 JOURNAL OF GLOBAL MARKETING*** 

fosters respect for authority and seniority, en   
hances the enforcement of rules and regulations,   
and facilitates the establishment of discipline. 

*Individualism–Collectivism* 

Whereas individualism describes the impor   
tance of the individual and the virtues of   
self-reliance and personal independence, col   
lectivism emphasizes the interrelationships of   
persons and the importance of relationships in   
connecting people (Blackwell, Miniard, & En   
gel, 2006).   
According to Hofstede (1991), sub-Saharan   
Downloaded by \[University of North Texas\] at 10:56 25 October 2013   
African cultures exhibit high collectivism be   
cause of the prevalence of the extended fam   
ily social fabric that characterizes most societies   
in Africa (see also Kiggundu, 1988; Munene,   
1996). Africans gravitate toward people since a   
man is what he is because he lives in the company   
of others (Anyanwa, 1983; Mphahlele, 1962).   
This outlook is reflected by an often-repeated   
Southern African phrase, “*Umuntu ngu muntu*   
*nga bantu*” (i.e., a person is a person because   
of other people; Mangaliso, 2001; Mufune,   
2003). This collectiveness fosters group dynam   
ics, facilitates the building of team spirit, and   
minimizes conflicts as well as creating loyalty,   
generating trust, and fostering a friendly work   
environment (Nnadozie, 1998). 

*Uncertainty Avoidance* 

Uncertainty avoidance is the extent to which   
cultures focus on ways to reduce uncertainty   
and create stability (Hofstede, 1991; Thomas,   
2002\) and concerns how a society reacts to the   
uncertainties and ambiguities inherent in life.   
Uncertainty avoidance cultures prefer structure   
and consistent routine, whereas uncertainty ac   
ceptance cultures exhibit positive responses to   
change and new opportunities. While uncer   
tainty avoidance societies prefer predictability   
and stability; in contrast, uncertainty acceptance   
societies accept uncertainty and have greater tol   
erance for unclear or indefinite situations (Hof   
stede, 1980, 1991; Pomerantz, 2004).   
In Hofstede’s (1983) survey, the multicoun   
try regions of East Africa (i.e., Kenya, Tan   
zania, and Ethiopia) and West Africa (i.e., 

Nigeria, Ghana, and Sierra Leone) scored at the intermediate position on the uncertainty avoid ance dimension. Nigeria and Zimbabwe were included in Hofstede (1991) in the “cautious” group (Munene, 1996). In a study involving Zim babwe, Zambia, Namibia, and Nigeria, House et al. (2004) place these African countries in the mid-level scores on their uncertainty avoid ance scale. Thus, the evidence strongly suggests African cultures are in the moderate category of the uncertainty avoidance. 

*Masculinity–Femininity* 

Masculinity–femininity refers to a society’s orientation based on traditional male and female values (Hofstede, 1991\) and describes the ex tent to which “traditional” male orientations of assertiveness, competiveness, materialism, am bition, and achievement are emphasized over “traditional” female orientations of nurturance, quality of life, relationships, and interpersonal harmony (Soares et al., 2007). A masculine orientation also includes respect for achieve ment and the acquisition of money and mate rial possessions, whereas feminine orientation includes concern for the environment, champi oning the underdog, and caring for the less for tunate (Blackwell, Miniard, & Engel, 2006). 

The high affiliation orientation within African culture strongly suggests that African societies exhibit some degree of femininity (Kiggundu, 1988; Nnadozie, 1998\) and, in fact, some re searchers note that African cultures/societies tend toward the feminine side (Pomerantz, 2004). The two African countries of Nigeria and Zimbabwe included in Hofstede (1991) fall in the “feminine” category. 

*High and Low Context* 

Hall’s (1989) taxonomy organizes cultures by the amount of information implied by the con text of the communication itself. High-context cultures prefer messages where the meaning is implied or is presumed to be part of the indi vidual’s internalized beliefs, values, and norms and where meanings are internalized and em phasis is placed on nonverbal cues or codes (Pomerantz, 2004). High-context cultures view   
***Darley, Luethge, and Blankson 193*** 

communication as a means to promote smooth,   
harmonious relationships, whereas low-context   
cultures rely on elaborate verbal explanations,   
putting much emphasis on spoken words (Kay   
nak et al., 2011).   
Reactions in low-context cultures are direct   
and explicit, whereas reactions are reserved   
and indirect in high-context cultures (Lustig   
& Koester, 1996). People in high-context cul   
tures refrain from critiquing each other openly   
because of their unwillingness to separate the   
actor from the actions (Sawadogo, 1994). Sub   
Saharan Africa is usually placed in the high   
context category.   
Downloaded by \[University of North Texas\] at 10:56 25 October 2013   
The key communication aspects in African   
culture influence concern who is talking, who is   
listening, and the particular circumstances of the   
situation (Pomerantz, 2004). 

*Time Orientation* 

Time orientation covers the temporal aspect   
of human life and whether time is directed at   
the past, present, or future (House et al., 2004;   
Kaynak et al., 2011; Kluckhohn & Strodtbeck,   
1961). People make decisions with respect to   
events in the past, events in the present, or events   
in the future (Thomas, 2002). The term also en   
capsulates monochronic and polychronic time   
(Hall, 1989), as well as socialized time (Onwu   
biko, 1991).   
Cultures with a linear time orientation have a   
highly organized view of time (Lustig & Koester,   
1996). Punctuality is expected and people view   
time as a commodity. With this orientation to   
ward time, time can be spent, wasted, lost, or   
managed. According to Hall (1989), cultures   
may also be monochronic or polychronic with   
respect to their time orientation. Cultures that   
have a monochronic orientation prefer to do one   
thing at a time, with high importance placed on   
tasks and schedules (Pomerantz, 2004). Poly   
chronic cultures, however, tend to do multiple   
things at the same time, with schedules being   
used as general guidelines rather than dead   
lines, and the involvement with people being   
more important than schedules. To the African,   
“time is simply a composition of events which   
have occurred, those which are taking place now   
and those which are inevitable or immediately 

to occur” (Mbiti, 1990, p. 16). Even the term “deadline” infers completion of the task, a no tion that is absurd to Africans, as schedules and time should not dictate life or one’s actions. Most of Africa falls into the category of poly chronic time that recognizes the complexities of life and the elasticity of time (Pomerantz, 2004). 

Time within the African culture is also so cialized. *Socialized time* is the use of time that does not sacrifice social duties and human re lations on clock-time punctuality alter. Time is programmed into sociocultural norms of human behavior and interpersonal relationships (Onwu biko, 1991). Thus, time is most important when one shares with family and social relations. Us ing time in social contexts takes precedence over the use of time in other contexts. 

***Normative Suggestions for International Marketing Practices*** 

Culture relates to purchase and consumption decisions; affects product selection, structure of the consumption, and individual decision making (Johnson et al., 2010); and influ ences advertising and interpersonal interactions (Bagozzi, Rosa, Celly, & Coronel, 1998; John son et al.). For example, the literature documents the influence of culture in *buyer-seller inter actions* (Simintiras & Thomas, 1998\) and that different cultures have different selling styles since customers’ expectations and behaviors dif fer significantly from culture to culture (Hon eycutt & Ford, 1996). Using inductive reason ing, this section explores implications of cul ture with international marketing—information sources, brand preference, advertising appeals, communication, personal selling, sales manage ment, and negotiations. The discussion serves as the basis for normative suggestions and provides managerial relevance (Soares et al., 2007\) (Ta ble 1). This is consistent with Strutton and Pel ton’s (1997) exposition on cultural dimensions and key normative suggestions. 

*Power Distance* 

In high-power distance cultures, inequalities are normal and expected. As a result, one would

Negotiations should appreciate the extended family. There is no need to rush in negotiation; rather, emphasis should be placed on relationships. Contracts and sales targets will not occur at the same rate as in cultures with linear view of time (e.g., the West). 

High/low context To what extent does the   
culture rely on spoken   
and written language for   
Downloaded by \[University of North Texas\] at 10:56 25 October 2013   
meaning?   
High context Attention should be paid to nonverbal communications in 

personal selling contexts. Indirect   
communication such as the use of   
proverbs, metaphors is favored   
over direct communication.   
Messages may be subtle, use less

expect to see differences in the marketing mix depending on the status and family relationships of the consumer. The African cultural character istics of high-power distance and hierarchy sug gest higher use of personal *information sources*. The high-power distance suggests that harmony will be a valued evaluative criterion. Word-of mouth and recommendations of a family mem ber or an elder in the household about a product or brand choice will be more important than in formation found in a commercial source such as in an advertisement. In addition, a personal *information source* from a respected individual will be viewed as a legitimizing source. Parents, elders, and family members are consulted for in put in the buying decision and, in some cases, such input will be offered unsolicited. 

This difference in status should be apparent not only in *advertising appeals* but also in a more formal and distant relationship of the *sales staff* with their supervisor, compared with what may be seen in Western cultures. With regard to advertising appeals, status symbols and privi leges may be able to be associated with a *brand*. 

Status, power, skills, and wealth all go together (Hofstede, 1991), and to the extent those can be associated with a particular *brand,* this can be a strong communication message. 

The *advertising appeals* should focus on the society to which the individual belongs and on the individual as a member of that society rather than on individual self-enhancement. Because of the importance of “respect,” messages should focus on an individual’s need for belonging ness rather than on the individual striving for achievement. More emphasis should be placed on endorsements and testimonials (Fletcher & Melewar, 2001). 

The high power distance and hierarchical na ture of African societies suggest paying atten tion to the role of the elderly and respect in the society. Age and gender are grounds for defer ence (Richmond & Gestrin, 1998). Age earns the highest respect. Thus, enhancing one’s age and trying to look older may not be out of place. Richmond and Gestrin (1998, p. 160\) advise that “if a foreign firm has a choice, it should send an older representative to Africa.” Thus, the age   
***196 JOURNAL OF GLOBAL MARKETING*** 

factor is relevant when selecting people for im   
portant assignments and when selecting and hir   
ing *sales and managerial staff* in Africa.   
Managers are expected to act the part in terms   
of dress and manners, to be formal and adhere to   
the “boss-subordinate” role. In addition, because   
power and status serve as motivators, managers   
will be obeyed as authorities. Thus, participative   
management is less likely to be effective be   
cause the boss is supposed to have the answers   
(Carnevale & Leung, 2003). The power distance   
suggests that in disputes using high status, third   
party individuals will facilitate the *negotiations*   
(Carnevale & Leung, 2003).   
Downloaded by \[University of North Texas\] at 10:56 25 October 2013   
*Individualism–Collectivism* 

African culture values relationships, particu   
larly family relationships. This means that older   
members of the family may have significant   
influence in the purchase decision, including   
whether a product is purchased and which *brand*   
is purchased. Because of the collectivistic nature   
of the culture and the high likelihood of social   
risk concerns, consumers may try to stay with   
*brands* recommended by family and reference   
groups. Thus, marketers should focus not only   
on the end user but also on those who signifi   
cantly influence the final decision.   
*Advertising appeals* should show respect and   
relate to both older individuals as well as the   
target market. Age differences between sales   
representatives and their clients or between ne   
gotiators may have an effect on the outcome   
of the relationship. Younger individuals show   
deference to older individuals, thus having an   
impact on the contracts, terms, or price that is   
*negotiated.*   
African culture focuses on maintaining har   
mony and group cohesiveness. This impacts   
marketing particularly in the areas of product   
choice and advertising appeals. Clearly, *adver*   
*tising appeals* should avoid contentious issues,   
foster group cohesiveness, and promote cultural   
values such as sharing and loyalty. Loyalty and   
the collective interest are also vital considera   
tions for sales and marketing managers, as group   
harmony is maintained when managers are seen   
as caring protectors and benefactors for their em 

ployees (Blackwell, Miniard, & Engel, 2006). *Advertisement appeals* that use humor “to break the ice” and focus on socialization and rela tionship building are more effective (Nnadozie, 1998). 

Because African societies are collectivistic, *persuasive appeals* that emphasize social norms and roles may be more effective in chang ing behavioral intentions than appeals focus ing on individual preferences and benefits (Han & Shavitt, 1994). In addition, the collectivis tic societies of Africa are more likely to use psychological appeals and symbolic association strategies suggestive of human emotions than are individualistic societies (Albers-Miller & Gelb, 1996). The collectivistic nature of the society suggests that *advertising appeals* that emphasize social norms and roles are more effective. 

When building relationships, trust is an es sential ingredient. This is vital for *sales rep resentatives* and their clients or for marketing managers who may be negotiating purchasing contracts. Since the development of relation ships between people in Africa takes time to mature, so, too, will the relationships between customers and new products. To that end, the awareness and persuasive stages of new product advertising message may be protracted, as time to develop a strong, loyal relationship with the customer may not happen quickly. The relational aspect of African culture calls for a different ap proach to negotiations. Richmond and Gestrin (1998) report that Africans see negotiation as a social and personal interaction—a “win-win” situation from which both parties should come away satisfied. 

*Uncertainty Avoidance* 

Cultures that are moderate in uncertainty avoidance tend to be less willing to try new products and slower to accept those products. Uncertainty orientation should influence *brand preference*. Whereas brand loyalty is likely to be encouraged in cultures that avoid uncertainty, brand switching is less likely to occur. Brand loy alty, preference for established brands, and brand name consciousness are likely to be high because   
***Darley, Luethge, and Blankson 197*** 

of uncertainty avoidance. The uncertainty avoid   
ance orientation negatively influences the desire   
for change and innovation. Thus, those firms that   
get into the market first are likely to gain a com   
petitive advantage (i.e., the pioneering effect) as   
they build loyalty. African cultures tend to have   
less tolerance for ambiguity, indicating that an   
*advertising message* might be more effective if   
it is simple and focuses on the decision process   
(Hofstede, 1983).   
This characteristic has an impact on sales rep   
resentatives and negotiators. With regard to con   
tract negotiators, Africans consider haggling on   
price as part of *negotiation*. Western cultures   
Downloaded by \[University of North Texas\] at 10:56 25 October 2013   
with low ambiguity tolerance prefer explicit   
rules and regulations (Hofstede, 1991). There   
fore, in an American African *joint venture*, while   
American partners prefer explicit, detailed con   
tractual documents, Africans will be comfort   
able with implicit, broadly termed agreements,   
since they are accustomed to some aspects of   
overlapping social and personal relationship in   
tertwining with negotiations (Darley & Blank   
son, 2008). 

*Masculinity–Femininity* 

African cultures are feminine, with a focus   
on relationships and quality of life rather than   
on assertiveness and materialism. *Advertising*   
*appeals* that promote relationships and quality   
of life issues should be effective. Conversely,   
African cultures exhibit ego effacement, where   
people are less assertive and tend to underrate   
the quality of their own performance (Hofst   
ede, 1983). This is an appropriate *advertising*   
*appeal* for offerings seeking to portray the im   
portance of modesty, humility, and relationships.   
The latter may be appealing for charity and non   
government organizations. The concept of ego   
effacement will impact the actions and deliber   
ations of the marketing team, sales force, sales   
managers, and negotiation teams, as staff may   
be unwilling to acknowledge their own perfor   
mance, especially if it appears to be superior to   
others in the group. Finally, the feminine nature   
of African culture suggests that the soft-sell is   
almost always better than the hard-sell (Rich   
mond & Gestrin, 2003); otherwise, negotiation   
may be counterproductive if the opposing team 

exerts undue masculine egos (i.e., assertiveness and single-mindedness). 

*High and Low Context* 

One basic difference between high and low context cultures is the degree of the reliance on nonverbal communications. This means that members of high-context cultures (e.g., Africans) will not express their opinions openly and explicitly, fearing it is a sign of overambi tious behavior, rudeness, or arrogance. However, members of *low context cultures* (e.g., Ameri cans) encourage openness and directness with little attention to hidden contexts (Lin & Miller, 2003). As a result, in situations where individu als from both low- and high-context cultures are interacting, miscommunication can occur as one person can be very direct (e.g., American) while the other is indirect (e.g., African). *Advertising appeals* that show direct comparisons of prod ucts are likely to be seen as less than tactful and, hence, inappropriate. 

As Africans generally refrain from critiquing openly or from giving negative feedback, it is better to use a “triangular model of obtaining feedback.” This involves engaging an interme diary to help channel feedback comments to the seller or marketer. The triangular model works best when the intermediaries are persons who are trusted by both parties and chosen on the basis of gender and age (Sawadogo, 1994). Commu nication may take longer and may have to go through third party channels, particularly with negative feedback, to save someone from los ing face (Richmond & Gestrin, 1998). Mes sages are effective if they are left somewhat open-ended to let the listener draw conclusions. *Advertising appeals* that show direct compar isons of products are likely to be seen as less tactful 

In addition, in buyer–seller interactions and personal selling situations, particular attention ought to be paid to the African *context* and nonverbal communications. Also, in the train ing of employees and sales force, one should consider the backgrounds of the participants in a manager–subordinate or salesperson–consumer interaction. As noted earlier, African cultures tend to favor indirect communication over direct   
***198 JOURNAL OF GLOBAL MARKETING*** 

communication. This has a tremendous impact   
on managing a *sales force* and marketing staff.   
Communication takes longer and messages are   
effective if they are left somewhat open-ended to   
let the listener draw conclusions (Richmond &   
Gestrin, 1998). While there may be some chance   
that an incorrect conclusion will be drawn by   
the audience, satisfaction with the overall mes   
sage may be higher due to the indirect approach.   
Hence, *Advertising appeals* that show direct   
comparisons of products are likely to be seen   
as less tactful or even boastful and, therefore,   
inappropriate.   
In high-context cultures (e.g., African cul   
Downloaded by \[University of North Texas\] at 10:56 25 October 2013   
tures), it is better to establish social trust first in   
*negotiations, personal selling,* and *buyer–seller*   
*interaction*. Personal relations and goodwill are   
valued and *negotiations* are slow and ritualis   
tic (Cavusgil, Knight, & Riesenberger, 2008,   
2012). 

*Time Orientation* 

African culture falls in the polychronic time   
orientation. This has an impact on *sales man*   
*agement* and *negotiations*, particularly when the   
two orientations collide. When it comes to in   
ternational marketing, certain marketing and   
sales management processes that allow peo   
ple the opportunity to respond to issues re   
lating to the extended family responsibilities   
and juggling many things at a time are wel   
come (Kaynak et al., 2011). To the African,   
efficiency without the concomitant ability to   
maintain relationships is not effective (Gyekye,   
2003).   
Frequent but reasonable interruptions, late or   
missed meetings, and conversations wandering   
far from the actual topic may seem to some (e.g.,   
Westerners) as inefficient, while to Africans this   
may not be seen as unreasonable but rather as   
appropriately taking care of responsibilities and   
relationships in the extended family (Onwubiko,   
1991). Perceptions about time suggest no need   
to rush a deal in a *negotiation*, which is consis   
tent with building relationships in high-context   
cultures (Kaynak et al., 2011). Hurrying through   
business deals will lead to suspicions of cheating   
and foul play (Richmond & Gestrin, 1998).   
***CONCLUSIONS, CONTRIBUTIONS, LIMITATIONS, AND FUTURE RESEARCH DIRECTIONS*** 

As the world becomes smaller, examination of consumer cultures worldwide evolves into an interesting academic and practical exercise (Huff & Smith, 2008). The changing global marketplace and how cultural characteristics of people affect international marketing prac tices are arguably two of the critical issues fac ing marketing managers today (Cleveland & Laroche, 2007; Huff & Smith, 2008; Kaynak et al., 2011; Laroche, 2007; Strutton & Pelton, 1997). The present review complements Soares et al.’s (2007) examination of the relationship be tween culture and international marketing, Kay nak et al.’s (2011) investigation of time orien tation and attitudes toward advertising in in ternational context (i.e., Georgia), and Kalliny, Saran, Ghanem, and Fisher’s (2011) examina tion of cultural differences in television com mercials in the Arab world and the United States and places the discussion of culture in the sub Saharan African context, a continent that has been greatly overlooked in global business dis course (Yang et al., 2006). 

Contributions of this article are in the form of rare impressions with regard to the implications of various aspects of sub-Saharan African cul ture on international marketing decisions (Gao, 2013). Specifically, sub-Saharan African culture (a) exhibits high-power distance, (b) is collec tivistic in social orientation, (c) exhibits un certainty avoidance, and (d) demonstrates pas sive goal orientation or feminine characteristics. In addition, the culture reflects embeddedness, hierarchy, and harmony and is high context. Any and all knowledge of sub-Saharan African culture is therefore valuable in that as knowl edge grows, so will multinational negotiators’ and executives’ accumulation of personal and nonpersonal connections to the culture (Gao, 2013; Johnson et al., 2010; Strutton & Pelton, 1997). 

As well, the discussion contributes to the in ternational marketing literature by responding to MSI (1998–2000) priority topics that specif ically stated the need to study “how different cultures respond to different marketing stimuli.”   
***Darley, Luethge, and Blankson 199*** 

Moreover, the review reacts to calls for future re   
search to study how differences in culture impact   
information sources, brand preference (Dash,   
Bruning & Acharya, 2009), advertising appeals,   
personal selling (Roman & Ruiz, 2003), and   
international negotiations (Lin & Miller, 2003;   
Simintiras & Thomas, 1998). In addition, the   
discussion pays heed to Laroche’s (2007) and   
Huff and Smith’s (2008) suggestions for more   
studies in culture and international marketing.   
This article will be of interest to interna   
tional marketing managers in that without dili   
gent appreciation of host culture, as in the case of   
Home Depot’s failure in China (see Gao, 2013),   
Downloaded by \[University of North Texas\] at 10:56 25 October 2013   
success in international markets will elude an   
organization’s foreign marketing deliberations.   
While admittedly doing business in Africa is not   
easy (Babarinde, 2009, p. 319), particularly for   
Western firms, creative thinking about how to   
surmount challenges in the African cultural en   
vironment, due diligence about its potential busi   
ness and investment opportunities, and creativity   
in managing the bureaucracy are bound to find   
the untapped and undertapped business environ   
ment to be a reservoir of profitable business (see   
Oyewole, 2006).   
Because multinational and international com   
panies seek fitting business models (Gao, 2013),   
the exposition of the nature of sub-Saharan   
African culture will be appreciated by interna   
tional marketing managers. International mar   
keters interested in sub-Saharan Africa may ap   
preciate these cultural implications by selecting   
target audiences with care, as the nonuser of a   
product (e.g., older relative) may have signifi   
cant influence over or actually make the final   
product choice decision (see Table 1). Moreover,   
since brand loyalty is strong given the African   
cultural characteristics of uncertainty avoidance   
and harmony, firms should pursue long-term po   
sitioning policies that will enhance competitive   
advantages as brand switching is rare (Owusu   
Frimpong, 2008). Advertising appeals that focus   
on social relationships and traditional values are   
more appropriate than messages that focus on   
benefits or technical characteristics (Table 1).   
Despite the diligence given to the theoretical   
development, the discussion, and the choice of   
the context, we acknowledge the limitations of   
conceptual studies without empirical examina 

tion. Nonetheless, the discussion has advanced several ideas that offer opportunities for future research. Researchers could empirically test var ious advertising appeals, examine brand pref erence/loyalty, and examine the impact of cer tain attributes on brand preference/loyalty in the African setting. Future studies that examine a wide range of ethno-African cultures, within a country or across countries, would be infor mative. In an age of information, communi cation, and technology, there is the need for research that examines the changing African cul tural characteristics in urban areas (Simon, Mc Gregor, & Nsiah-Gyabaah, 2004\) and the sub tle ways these impact international marketing strategies (Laroche, 2007). Future studies may divide the continent into regions and then dis cuss the distinctive differences based on culture. The latter may have major impact on marketing practices. Finally, future researchers need to em pirically determine how African countries differ from similarly collectivistic Asian cultures.
