[[KB-FONTE: Adair, Brett & Okumura — Negotiation Behavior When Cultures Collide: The United States and Japan (Journal of Applied Psychology, 2001, Vol. 86, No. 3) · framework cross-culturale di processo negoziale (strategie dirette vs. indirette di condivisione delle informazioni, confronto USA-Giappone, sequenza e reciprocità delle tattiche) ]]

Journal of Applied Psychology 2001, Vol. 86, No. 3, 371–385   
Copyright 2001 by the American Psychological Association, Inc. 0021-9010/01/$5.00 DOI: 10.1037/0021-9010.86.3.371 

Negotiation Behavior When Cultures Collide: The United States and Japan 

Wendi L. Adair Northwestern University 

Jeanne M. Brett Northwestern University   
Tetsushi Okumura Shiga University 

This study compared the negotiation behaviors of Japanese **and** U.S. managers in intra- and intercultural settings. Transcripts from an integrative bargaining task were coded and analyzed with logistic and linear regression. U.S. negotiators exchanged information directly and avoided influence when negotiating intra- **and** interculturally. Japanese negotiators exchanged information indirectly and used influence when negotiating intraculturally but adapted their behaviors when negotiating interculturally. Culturally nor- mative negotiation behaviors partially account for the lower joint gains generated by intercultural, relative to intracultural**,** dyads. The behavioral data inform motivational and skill-based explanations for elusive joint gains when cultures clash. 

Because there is substantial empirical evidence of cultural dif- ferences in negotiation behaviors (see Wilson, Cai, Campbell, Donohue, & Drake, 1995, for a review in negotiation context**;** Weldon & Jehn, 1995, for a review in conflict management con- text), it would seem reasonable that when negotiators are from behaviorally distinct cultures, one or both may have to adapt to reach a successful agreement. Weiss (1994), for example, suggests **that** the negotiator who is most familiar with the other's negotia- tion culture should do the most adaptation. Adler (1997) cautions that although cultural adaptation does not guarantee a positive outcome, it increases the chances of one. In this study, we inves- tigated whether a failure in adaptation explains why the agree- ments negotiated by a group of U.S. and Japanese intercultural negotiators were significantly less integrative than those reached by groups of intracultural U.S. and Japanese negotiators. We used previously unpublished negotiation behavior data from a subset of participants in the Brett and Okumura (1998) study on cultural values, negotiation schemas, and joint gains. 

Both Graham (1985) and Brett and Okumura (1998) reported that intercultural negotiations between U.S. and Japanese negoti- ators were less successful than intracultural negotiations. How- ever, neither study focused on negotiators' behaviors, and neither tested whether adaptation failed to occur. This study takes a step 

Wendi L. Adair and Jeanne M. Brett, Kellogg Graduate School of Management, Northwestern University; Tetsushi Okumura. Faculty of Economics, Shiga University, Shiga, Japan. 

This study was supported by a grant from the Dispute Resolution Research Center, Northwestern University. 

We thank Ranjay Gulati for his valuable help with the logistic regression. 

Correspondence concerning this article should be addressed to Wendi L. Adair, who is now at the Johnson School of Management. Cornell Uni- **versity**, 437 Sage Hall, Ithaca, New York 14853\. Electronic mail may be sent to wla5@cornell.edu. 

371   
toward understanding intercultural adaptation by investigating the less successful intercultural negotiations reported by Brett and Okumura (1998). 

When intercultural negotiators adapt, it means they are using negotiation behaviors that are more normative in the other culture and less normative in their own culture. Thus, to study adaptation, we need a baseline profile of negotiation behaviors characteristic of Japanese and U.S. intracultural negotiators. Although there is substantial descriptive information about differences between U.S. and Japanese negotiation styles (e.g., March, 1990; Yoshimura, 1997), there is little empirical data profiling characteristic Japanese negotiation behaviors beyond Graham's research showing that Japanese negotiators ask more questions but reveal less informa- tion than U.S. negotiators and rarely use the word no in negotia- tions (Graham**,** 1993; Graham & Sano, 1989\). Thus, another con- tribution of this study is that it empirically develops profiles of negotiation behaviors characteristic of Japanese and U.S. intracul- tural negotiations. 

Studying cultural adaptation in the context of U.S. and Japanese negotiations is particularly useful because the U.S. and Japan are major trading partners**.** They are, however, culturally quite differ- ent (Graham & Sano, 1989; Hofstede, 1980; Schwartz, 1994), and stories of unsuccessful intercultural negotiations between U.S. and Japanese companies are common (Graham & Sano, 1989). Under- standing intercultural adaptation in the context of U.S.-Japanese negotiations has important implications for future economic trans- actions between nationals of these two major economic powers. 

We propose that culture, defined as a society's characteristic profile with respect to values, norms, behaviors, and institutions (Lytle, Brett, Barsness, Tinsley, **&** Janssens, 1995\), is one impor- tant factor influencing negotiation behavior. Given the well- documented differences between the Japanese and U.S. cultures (Graham & Sano, 1989; Hofstede, 1980; Schwartz, 1994), we suspected that U.S. and Japanese negotiators would have distin- guishable profiles of negotiation behavior. We anticipated that   
372   
ADAIR, OKUMURA, AND BRETT 

when U.S. and Japanese negotiators meet, their culturally norma- tive behaviors would be incompatible. We hypothesized conditions leading to adaptation, particularly on the part of the Japanese. We also hypothesized conditions indicative of process losses associ- ated with trying to adapt in the face of cultural differences. 

Culturally Normative Negotiation Behaviors 

We used two criteria for developing hypotheses about culturally normative behaviors that would distinguish U.S. and Japanese negotiators. First, we were interested in behaviors that prior re- search indicates are correlated with integrative negotiation out- comes (high joint gains). Second, we were interested in behaviors that prior research suggests will vary between the U.S. and Japa- nese cultures. 

Negotiation behaviors that Pruitt (1981) and others (Olekalns, Smith, & Walsh, 1996; Tutzauer & Roloff, 1988; Weingart, Thompson, Bazerman, & Carroll, 1990\) have found to be corre- lated with joint gains include exchanging information directly by sharing preferences and priorities, exchanging information indi- rectly by engaging in heuristic trial and error search, and eschew- ing distributive tactics. Research on and theorizing about the characteristics of the U.S. and Japanese cultures suggests that Japanese and U.S. negotiators may exchange information differ- ently and that Japanese negotiators may not eschew distributive tactics to the same extent as U.S. negotiators. 

*Information Exchange* 

Both U.S. and Japanese negotiators are concerned about infor- mation in negotiations (Brett & Okumura, 1998; Yoshimura, 1997); however, cultural differences suggest that they may have distinct ways of exchanging information (Brett & Okumura, 1998\). Japan is a high-context culture, and the United States is a low- context culture (Hall, 1976; Okabe, 1983). In low-context cultures, people communicate directly; information is contained in explicit verbal or written messages (Hall, 1976). In high-context cultures, people communicate allusively and rely on context (e.g., personal experiences, environmental factors), to convey information implic- itly (Hall, 1976). We expect that these different communication styles will be reflected in how negotiators exchange information. Negotiators from high-context cultures infer meaning from what is not said, whereas negotiators from low-context cultures focus on sending and receiving accurate messages or articulating with words (Harris & Moran, 1991). For example, as cited in Brett & Okumura (1998), Yoshimura (1997) noted that U.S. negotiators are more likely to disclose information, but Japanese negotiators are more likely to hide information. Graham (1993) reported that U.S. dyads reveal slightly more information than Japanese dyads; Graham and Sano (1989), that Japanese negotiators ask more questions but reveal less information than U.S. negotiators; and Graham (1993), that Japanese negotiators rarely use a direct "no" in negotiation. March (1990) also commented that Japanese nego- tiators are reluctant to turn down a proposal explicitly. 

Brett and Okumura (1998) found indirect evidence for different communication behaviors in negotiation: U.S. negotiators did not understand Japanese negotiators' priorities when negotiating inter- culturally. This finding is highly likely if in fact U.S. negotiators were communicating directly and Japanese negotiators were com-   
municating indirectly. Japanese negotiators would presumably un- derstand direct U.S. communication because people from high- context cultures are skilled in both direct and indirect communication **(**Hall, 1976). But low-context U.S. negotiators would presumably **have** trouble understanding the Japanese nego- tiators' priorities because people from low-context cultures are not typically skilled in the subtle inference necessary to communicate indirectly **(**Hall, 1976\). 

We hypothesized that U.S. intracultural negotiators would be more likely than Japanese intracultural negotiators to exchange information directly because of cultural differences in communi- cation styles. Direct forms of information exchange include ex- plicit statements about priorities, for example what is more and what is less important, or statements comparing or contrasting the needs or interests of the two parties (**Pruitt**, 1981). Direct reactions to the other party's proposals are another example of explicit**,** low-context information exchange. 

Hypothesis 1: U.S. intracultural negotiators are more likely to ex- change information directly than Japanese intracultural negotiators. 

We also hypothesized that Japanese intracultural negotiators would be more likely than U.S. intracultural negotiators to ex- change information indirectly because of cultural differences in communication styles. The measure of indirect information ex- change proposed by Pruitt (1981) is heuristic trial and **error search** via offers **and** counteroffers, Gathering information about priorities from offers requires drawing inferences. For example, if I make an offer **and** you make a counteroffer, I can then **draw** an inference about your priorities from the changes you made in my offer. Information about priorities is conveyed implicitly and indirectly through multiple offers over time. 

*Hypothesis* 2*:* Japanese intracultural negotiators are more likely to exchange information indirectly than U.S. intracultural negotiators. 

*Influence* 

Power behaviors in negotiation are those strategies of influence (French & Raven, 1959; Lewicki, Saunders, & Minton, 1997\) by which a negotiator tries to satisfy individual goals **(**Deutsch, 1973**)**. sway the other party (Thompson, 1997; Ury, Brett, & Goldberg, 1988), and gain advantage over the other party (Lewicki et al., 1997\). Research on power-based influence in negotiations refers to pressure tactics (Carnevale, Pruitt, & Seilheimer, 1981), conten- tious behavior (Ben\-**Yoav** & Pruitt, 1984; Brett, Shapiro, & Lytle, 1998; Pruitt. 1983; Shapiro & Bies, 1994), and a distributive approach (Lewis & Fry, 1977; Pruitt & Lewis, 1975\) that include threats, **persuasion**, status, and positioning. 

The cultural value of hierarchy versus egalitarianism is corre- lated with norms for using distributive tactics in negotiation (Brett, Adair, **et** al., 1998). Brett and Okumura (1998) report that hierar- chical Japanese negotiators were more likely than egalitarian U.S. negotiators to endorse a power schema and to report having spent considerable time discussing power when preparing for the nego- tiation. For example, during the planning session, Japanese nego- tiators were more likely than U.S. negotiators to discuss how powerful their company was and how advantageous their negoti- ating position was (ie., positioning and status). Japanese negoti- ators were also more likely than U.S. negotiators to think that it is   
NEGOTIATION BEHAVIOR WHEN CULTURES COLLIDE 

appropriate during negotiation to make the first offer (positioning) or appeal to sympathy (persuasion). These differences suggest **that** Japanese negotiators are more likely than U.S. negotiators to use power and influence behaviors during a negotiation. 

*Hypothesis 3:* Japanese intracultural negotiators are more likely to use power or influence tactics than U.S. intracultural negotiators. 

Culture Clash 

If U.S. and Japanese managers negotiate differently within their own cultures, what happens when they negotiate with each other in **an** intercultural setting? Does one or the other party try to adapt? Do cross-cultural negotiators tend to satisfice when they confront a negotiating style that is different from their own? Are there process losses associated with negotiating **cross**\-culturally? We know that the joint gains of the intercultural negotiators **in** our data set were lower than the joint gains of the Japanese and the U.S. intracultural negotiators **(**Brett & Okumura, 1998). In this section, we develop hypotheses to explore whether the lower joint gains in intercultural negotiations can be explained by a failure to adapt successfully. We also test whether culturally normative behaviors mediate the relationship between type of negotiating dyad (intra- **vs.** intercultural) and joint **gains**. 

*Did Adaptation Occur?* 

Bettenhausen and Murnighan (1985) propose that group mem- bers come to the table with scripts based on past experiences. When they encounter behavior **that** clashes **with** their scripts, **they** may negotiate group norms. A group member may assert her own script or may observe and adapt to the script being enacted by other group members. We expected that the Japanese would be more likely to adapt to the U.S. negotiators' normative behaviors than would the U.S. negotiators to the Japanese negotiators' nor- mative behaviors. There are situational and cultural **reasons** for this prediction. First, the Japanese participants in the intercultural sample were working for Japanese companies **in** the United States, **and** the data were collected in the United States. Weiss (1994) suggested that the party most familiar with the other's culture should adapt. Second, the Japanese culture values mutual under- standing and adapting to one's environment (Miyanaga, 1991). Japanese managers might also adapt as an expression of what Miyanaga (1991) calls “passive individualism.” According to this reasoning, Japanese negotiators may adapt to their counterparts to **break** away from the traditional Japanese collective orientation and develop an individual, personal relationship. 

*Hypothesis 4*: Japanese intercultural negotiators are more likely than U.S. intercultural negotiators to adapt the other party's culturally normative behaviors. 

Specifically, we expected Japanese intercultural negotiators **to** use more direct information sharing and less indirect information sharing and influence than Japanese intracultural negotiators. High levels of direct information exchange and low levels of indirect information exchange and influence are the normative behaviors we expect for U.S. negotiators. 

Because the Japanese intercultural negotiators lived and worked for Japanese companies in the United States, the data would not   
373 

allow us to determine whether adaptation occurred because of situational **(**Weiss' explanation) or cultural (Miyanaga's explana- tion) factors. However, if we found that U.S. intercultural negoti- ators adapted more than Japanese intercultural negotiators, we would have important evidence that contradicts both strategic and cultural predictions for U.S.-Japanese intercultural negotiations. Furthermore, unsuccessful adaptation would offer a powerful ex- planation for why intercultural negotiators did poorly relative to intracultural negotiators. 

*Were Negotiators Motivated to Adapt Successfully?* 

The theorizing about culturally responsive adaptation fails to recognize the difficulties of doing so. There are both motivational and skill-related obstacles to successful adaptation. One motiva- tional obstacle is ethnocentrism**, or** the belief that in comparison to other cultures, your culture has the best approach **(**Adler, 1997\). Another is the fact that intercultural negotiation is, by definition**,** an out\-group interaction, with uncertainty and incomplete infor- mation that make conformity unlikely (Turner, 1991). Some case- specific factors, for instance, the perceived relative power of the parties, might also affect motivation to adapt. 

Evidence of satisficing, not using the time allowed for negoti- ation and simply interacting less**,** would allow us to focus on a pure motivational explanation for unsuccessful adaptation. If intercul- tural negotiators' joint gains are limited by satisficing, they should on average be doing less negotiating than the intracultural nego- tiators, whose agreements are significantly more integrative. 

*Hypothesis* 5*:* Intercultural negotiators will do less negotiating than intracultural negotiators. 

*Did Negotiators Have the Skills to Adapt Successfully?* 

In trying to adopt the other party's behaviors, negotiators may not know the appropriate behaviors or be skilled in using them. Evidence of effort to improve understanding allows us to focus on a skill\-based explanation for a failure of adaptation and integration. Brett and Okumura (1998) found that especially the U.S. intercul- tural negotiators had difficulty reporting the other parties' priori- ties in the postnegotiation questionnaire. If the intercultural nego- tiators were having trouble understanding each other's priorities, it seems likely that they would be asking each other more questions and making more statements of a clarifying nature than intracul- tural negotiators. 

*Hypothesis* 6: Intercultural negotiators are likely to use more clarify- ing questions and statements **than** intracultural negotiators. 

Another behavioral indicator of adaptation difficulty is the amount of discussion about negotiation procedure. If intercultural negotiators were having difficulties bridging the **gap** between their different negotiation scripts, they may have discussed negotiation procedure to manage the interaction more effectively. For exam- ple, if I am uncertain whether the other party understands my approach, I can try to clarify by indicating what I would like to discuss now, where I would like to go from here, **my** expectations for the length of negotiations, negotiation outcome, reciprocal concessions, and so on. If intercultural negotiators were having difficulty adapting, these types of procedural comments should be   
374   
ADAIR, OKUMURA, AND BRETT 

more common in transcripts of their interactions than in intracul- tural negotiation transcripts. 

*Hypothesis 7:* Intercultural negotiators are more likely than intracul- tural negotiators to discuss negotiation procedures. 

*Negotiation Behaviors and Joint* Gains 

We know from research in the United States that the three behaviors measured in this study, direct information sharing, in- direct information sharing, and influence, have implications for joint gains in U.S. intracultural negotiations (Olekalns et al., 1996; Pruitt, 1981; Tutzauer & Roloff, 1988; Weingart et al., 1990). We do not know how these behaviors are related to joint gains for Japanese intracultural and U.S.\-Japanese intercultural negotiators. Our argument for culturally normative negotiation behaviors sug- gests that culturally normative behaviors will be the most effective behaviors for generating joint gains in intracultural negotiations. 

Our study hypothesized that U.S. negotiators are more likely to expect and enact direct information\-sharing behaviors, whereas Japanese negotiators are more likely to expect and enact indirect information-sharing and power behaviors. Because negotiators should interact and communicate most effectively when they are using culturally normative behaviors, these behaviors in particular should allow negotiators to exchange information and identify integrative solutions that generate joint gains. Because direct in- formation exchange is normative for U.S. negotiators, we expected that it would help distinguish the joint gains realized by U.S. intracultural and intercultural negotiators. Indirect information ex- change and influence are not culturally normative behaviors in the United States, so we did not expect these behaviors to help explain different joint gains realized by U.S. intracultural and intercultural negotiators. Indirect information exchange and influence are the culturally normative behaviors we expected would help distinguish joint gains realized by Japanese intracultural and intercultural negotiators. Because direct information exchange is not the cul- turally preferred strategy for Japanese negotiators, we would not expect it to be a factor accounting for differential joint gains generated by Japanese intracultural and intercultural negotiators. 

*Hypothesis* 8: Culturally normative behaviors are more likely to account for different joint gains generated by intra- and intercultural negotiators than are behaviors that are not culturally normative. 

*The Study*   
Method   
one distributive issue: price of each episode. There is the potential for a trade\-off between financing and runs (the number of times each episode may be shown in the 5\-*year* period), as it is more important for the seller to have payment up front and for the buyer to have a greater number of runs. There is also a second cartoon**,** Strums, that will be available in the future and represents a compatible issue **with** the potential to add value for both parties. Finally, there is an opportunity for **a** contingent contract if the parties realize the potential to bet on their different rating expectations. Negotiators received confidential information on their positions and alter- native deals as indicated in the appropriate column in Table 1. 

There were four versions of the simulation: a Japanese buyer and a Japanese seller, a U.S. buyer and a U.S. seller, a Japanese buyer and a U.S. seller, and a Japanese seller and a U.S. buyer. For the Japanese intracultural data collection, the text was translated from the U.S. version with only minor changes in context, for example**,** a Chicago independent television station versus a Tokyo independent television station. Bilingual speakers translated and back\-translated all role instructions and simulation materials. Japanese participants in the intercultural sample received their role instruc- tions in Japanese and English. In the Japanese version, all numbers in Table 1 were converted into yen using the approximate exchange **rate** ($1 to 100 yen) at the time of data collection. 

*Sample* 

The data consist of three samples: U.S. intracultural, Japanese intracul- tural, and U.S.-Japanese intercultural. The U.S. intracultural data (40 dyads) were collected in the United States at two different times, approx- imately 1 year apart. In the first U.S. intracultural data collection, the participants were full\-time graduate business students on the first day of a course on negotiation. They averaged 28 years old and had 5 years of work experience. In the second U.S. intracultural data collection, participants were part-time executive business students on the first day of a course on negotiation. They averaged 37 years old and had over 10 years of work

The behavioral data used in this study came from **a** subset of the participants in the Brett and Okumura (1998) study: those whose audio recordings of their negotiation sessions were audible for transcription. There were no significant differences in outcome of those negotiators with audible and those with inaudible tapes within each culture group. 

*Simulation* 

The negotiation simulation, Cartoon, involves the sale of syndicated rights to a children's television cartoon. The context of the case was adapted slightly from the Working Women case (Tenbrunsel & Bazerman**,** 2000\). The seller is a major film production company that is prepared to sell a fixed 5-year, 100-episode contract. The buyer is an independent television station in a large metropolitan area. The parties must negotiate   
**8-9** 

9-10 

10-11   
NEGOTIATION BEHAVIOR WHEN CULTURES COLLIDE 

experience. There were no significant differences between the U.S. intra- cultural samples on any measure, 

The Japanese intracultural sample consisted of 22 dyads. The partici- pants did the negotiation simulation as the first exercise in a management training course on negotiation in Japan. Their average age was 27 years, and their average work experience was *5* years. 

The U.S.-Japanese intercultural sample consisted of 26 U.S. and 26 Japanese managers who were invited to participate in a 1-day executive program on intercultural negotiation. Potential participants for the U.S. sample were identified from the alumni of a graduate program in business administration who resided in the metropolitan area. Potential participants for the Japanese sample were identified from the population of Japanese companies with offices **in** the metropolitan area. Potential participants were sent a letter of invitation to participate in a daylong program on U.S.- Japanese cross\-cultural negotiation. For the Japanese participants, letters of invitation were sent to senior managers, who were asked to send middle managers who conduct the majority of their daily business in English. Japanese participants were told that the program would be conducted in English. 

The intercultural data were collected at two times**,** exactly 1 year apart, and there were no significant differences between the groups on any measure. The U.S. managers were 38 years old on average and had average work experience of 15 years. The average age of the Japanese managers was 36, and they had 13 years' work experience on average. 

A method check that the samples had values characteristic of their culture (Brett, Tinsley, Janssens, Barsness, & Lytle, 1997\) is reported in Brett and Okumura (1998). They used two measures of cultural values: hierarchy and individualism (Schwartz, 1994). We reran the sampling check analysis on this subset of negotiators who recorded their negotiation to make sure that the subsample had all the characteristics of **the** sample reported in Brett and Okumura (1998). The Japanese participants valued hierarchy more than the U.S. participants (U.S.; *M* 21.15, *SD* 6.44: Japanese: *M* 23.77, *SD* 6.88), *F(*1, 165\) 6.24, *p* \= .01. The U.S. participants valued individualism more than did the Japanese participants: (U.S.: M 39.30, *SD*   
4.78; Japanese: M 36.06, SD 5.86), *F*(1, 167\) \= 15.48, p \= .01. There were no significant differences on these same cultural values between U.S. negotiators in the intra- and intercultural data sets or between the Japanese negotiators in the intra- and intercultural data sets. 

We also reran Brett and Okumura's (1998) joint\-gains analysis to confirm that the joint gains of the intercultural negotiators whose behavior we would be studying were significantly lower than the joint gains of the U.S. and Japanese intracultural negotiators. There were no significant differences in joint gains between U.S. and Japanese dyads in **an** intra- cultural setting (U.S. ***M*** $4.38 million, SD $0.773 million; **Japanese**: *M* \= $4.11 million, *SD* \= $0.80 million), F(1, 58\) \= 1.65, *p* .20. There were significant differences in joint gains between U.S. and Japanese intracultural and intercultural dyads (intracultural: M $4.29 million, *SD* $0.79 million; intercultural: M $3.54 million, *SD* $0.94 million), *F*(1, 83\) 14.14, p \= .01\* 

*Procedure* 

A standard introduction to the negotiation exercise was used each time data were collected. Participants gathered in a large classroom, **where** they completed a cultural value survey. Participants were briefly introduced to the negotiation simulation and were given role assignments and confiden- tial information. Participants were paired into same\-role, same-culture dyads and assigned breakout rooms for a 1\-hr preparation session. After the preparation session, participants returned to the main classroom, where they had an opportunity to ask questions and were assigned to buyer-**seller** pairs. Negotiators always played the role of a party from their own culture. Negotiation dyads were assigned so that no two buyers who prepared together were paired with two sellers who prepared together. Participants   
375 

were provided **with a** cassette recorder to audiotape their negotiation session. Each dyad was assigned a breakout room and given 1.5 hr to negotiate. Researchers were available during the 1.5-hr period to answer questions. After the negotiation simulation, participants once again gath- ered in the main classroom, where they reported results **and** were debriefed as a group in conjunction with a lecture on cross-cultural negotiation. 

*Measures* 

*Independent* variables. For Hypotheses 1-4, 6, and 7, the independent variables were dummy variables capturing culture and type of negotiating dyad: U.S. intracultural, U.S. intercultural, and Japanese intercultural. The category Japanese intracultural was **used** as the reference category. For Hypothesis 5, the independent **variable** was a categorical variable coded I for U.S. intracultural dyads, 2 for Japanese intracultural dyads, and 3 for intercultural dyads. For Hypothesis 8, the independent variables were type of negotiating dyad (U.S. intracultural, Japanese intracultural, or U.S.- Japanese intercultural) and the proportion of each negotiation behavior in each transcript. The proportion of each negotiation behavior was opera- tionalized by computing the relative frequency of each negotiation behav- ior within each transcript divided by the dyad's total number of speaking turns. This percentage was then transformed to stretch the tails of the distribution using the following transformation offered by Tukey (1977): 

1.15 **logo** (\#1 − *ƒ)*. 

where *ƒ* represents **the** percentage score. 

*Dependent* variables. For Hypotheses 1-4, 6, and 7, the dependent variables were constructed from coded negotiation transcripts. **We** based our code on those used in prior research **on** transactional negotiations (Donohue, 1981; Pruitt, 1981; Putnam & Jones, 1982; Weingart, Bennett, & Brett, 1993; Weingart et al., 1990; see the Appendix)**.** We aggregated items from the code to operationalize the five negotiation behaviors ad- **dressed** in the hypotheses of this study: direct information exchange, indirect information exchange, influence, clarification, and procedural 

comments. 

Direct information exchange includes information on priorities, compar- isons and contrasts between the parties**,** and direct reactions to offers **and** proposals. Indirect information exchange consists of offers and counterof- fers (single and multi-issue). Influence tactics include legitimacy (refer- ences to the status of one's company, competitors, and relationship to the other party); informational persuasion (positive and negative substantia- tion); information with sanctions (threats such as references to the best alternative to a negotiated agreement \[BATNA; Fisher, Ury, & Patton**,** 1991\] or reservation price that convey a party's alternatives or power to walk away from the table); and sympathy, or stressing one's personal stake in the negotiation. Clarification includes any type of clarification or con- firmatory question **or** statement, including requests for repetition. Proce- 

We did not have all of the hierarchy items from the cultural value survey for 5 of the 44 Japanese intracultural participants, 2 of the 80 U.S. intracultural participants, and 2 of the 26 U.S. intercultural participants. 

2 We did not have all of **the** individualism items from the cultural value survey for 4 of the 44 Japanese intracultural participants, 2 of the 80 U.S. intracultural participants, and I of the 26 U.S. intercultural participants. 

3   
3 One of the 40 U.S. intracultural dyads and 1 of the 22 Japanese intracultural dyads did not reach a negotiated agreement. These dyads **were** excluded from the ANOVA analysis but they were included in the process analyses. 

**4**   
\* One of the 40 U.S. intracultural dyads**,** I of the 22 Japanese intracul- tural dyads**,** and 1 of the U.S.**\-**Japanese intercultural dyads did not reach a negotiated agreement. These dyads were excluded from the ANOVA analysis, but they were included in the process analyses.   
376   
ADAIR, OKUMURA, AND **BRETT** 

dural comments include statements about procedures to be used, reciproc- ity, expected negotiation outcome, limits of case information, or breaks. 

Negotiation tapes were transcribed and, in the case of the Japanese tapes, translated and transcribed. All Japanese tapes were translated and tran- scribed by the same person. A person fluent in the pertinent language or languages monitored transcriptions against the tapes. 

*Four* coders were rigorously trained to content\-code transcripts using the speaking turn as the unit of analysis. Coders worked independently coding a negotiation transcript and then met to establish consensus for all codes assigned. There were five such practice rounds before the coders completed a series of three transcripts that were coded for interrater reliability using the formula for Cohen's kappa offered by Bakeman and Gottman (1997). At this point, we gave coders a series of unique transcripts. At 6-month intervals, we gave each coder the same transcript to code, and we reeval- uated reliability. Across four reliability checks, interrater reliability was an average of 62\. Bakeman and Gottman (1997) note that Cohen's **kappa** of .60 to 75 is "good.” 

A speaking turn was coded to capture up to five thought units so that additional content would not be lost in longer speaking turns. There were a total of 15,369 primary; 3,529 secondary; 2,698 third; 2,559 fourth; and 2,542 fifth thought units. 

Code categories were then collapsed to represent the five dependent variables: direct information exchange, indirect information exchange, influence, clarification, and procedural comments. Five dummy variables **were** constructed and coded 1 for each speaking turn in which the depen- dent variable appeared and (0) for each speaking turn in which the dependent variable did not appear. For example, direct information exchange was coded I for any speaking turn when there **was** a preferences and priorities, mutuality, or direct-reactions code in Thought Units 1-5. 

The dependent variable for Hypothesis 5 was the total number of speaking turns per dyad. For Hypothesis 8, the dependent measure was joint gains**,** calculated as the sum of the **buyer's** and seller's gains over and above **their** BATNAs. 

*Control variables*. For Hypotheses 1-4, 6, and 7, a dichotomous variable (yes/no) indicating whether there were multiple codes in a given speaking turn was included to control for interdependence within **a** speak- ing turn. To control for role and age effects, we constructed a dichotomous variable for role (1 if buyer, 2 if seller) and a continuous control variable for age. To control for intracultural variance on cultural values, individual scores on individualism and hierarchy were also included as continuous control variables. To control for interdependence within individuals and between dyad members, we used a continuous variable: total number of speaking turns in each transcript. The total number of speaking turns in a transcript is highly correlated (r \= .99) with the number of utterances per individual, so it can serve to control for both types of interdependency. 

Because the content of a speaking turn is related to what happened previously, we also created the following control variables: **(a**) a continu- ous variable (time), calculated as the speaking-turn number divided by the total number of speaking turns in a transcript to note the place in a particular transcript when an utterance occurred, (b) dichotomous variables to control for reciprocity (lag D) and consistency (lag 2), and (c) a continuous variable measuring the prior frequency of a code category within a particular transcript. 

*Analyses* 

The unit of analysis was the speaking turn, which contained from one to five codes. We used logistic regression to **test** Hypotheses 1-4**, 6,** and 7 because of the highly dependent nature of speaking turns within a **dialogue**. Logistic regression allows us to control for the interdependent nature of speaking turns (reciprocity and consistency **within** a transcript, **overall use** of a code within a transcript, time within a transcript, number of speaking turns within a transcript), multiple **codes** within **a** single speaking turn, possible role effects, as well as sample variance in age and cultural values.   
Logistic regression was used to **assess** the effects of **the** independent variables on the likelihood of each dependent variable within **a** speaking turn (Aldrich & Nelson, 1984). The general specification of the logit model is the following equation: 

log \[P(*M1* \= 1)/1 − P***(M***; I)\] \= A, \+ **BI**(*X)*, 

**where** P***(**M* 1\) is the probability that dependent **variable** *i* was observed and *X*, is the vector of independent **variables (**Aldrich **& Nelson**, **1984**). 

Logistic regression reports raw beta **coefficients** that indicate a change in the logarithmic odds of one versus another category of a dichotomous dependent variable given **a** one-unit change in the independent variable (Demaris, 1992). **Because** the coefficients refer to log odds, we cannot directly interpret their magnitude, but we can interpret the direction of their effect (Demaris, 1992\). For example, a significant positive or negative coefficient indicates that the predictor variable has a positive or negative impact on the determination of **the** dependent variable**,** respectively. 

The log likelihood ratio and model *y2* are indicators of overall model significance. **Similar** to a global *F* test in linear regression, the \-2 log likelihood ratio contrasts two nested logistic regression equations, where **one** equation is a restricted version of the other. On the basis of the log likelihood ratio, a test statistic is computed with a chi**\-**square distribution. **A** significant chi-square indicates that at least one beta coefficient in the full equation is **not** equal to zero (Demaris, 1992*).* 

Hypothesis 5, comparing the length of negotiation transcripts between intra- and intercultural negotiators, was tested with analysis of variance (ANOVA) on an aggregated, dyad\-level file. 

Hypothesis 8, investigating whether culturally normative behaviors help explain different **joint** gains **for** intra- and intercultural groups, was tested with a series of linear regression analyses with the dyad **as** the unit of analysis. Because U.S. and Japanese negotiators **have** different culturally normative behaviors**,** we conducted two sets of analyses comparing how behaviors relate to outcomes: one for U.S. intracultural versus U.S.- Japanese intercultural negotiators and another for Japanese intracultural versus U.S.--Japanese intercultural negotiators**.** For each set of analyses, we regressed joint gains on each negotiation behavior and the type of negoti- ation **(**intra- or intercultural). Because of our small sample size, we ana- lyzed separately how each behavior accounted for joint **gains** relative to the intra- and intercultural negotiation groups. 

**Results** 

Table 2 presents the logistic **regression estimates**. The columns represent the dependent variables: direct information exchange, indirect information exchange, influence, clarification, and proce- dural comments**. Rows** 1-3 are the primary independent variables representing type of negotiator. Japanese intracultural negotiator was defined as the reference category, for which there **are** no beta coefficients to report. **Rows** 4-13 are the control variables. Rows 14-15 are measures **of** the model's overall fit. 

In support of Hypothesis 1, intracultural U.S. negotiators were more likely to share information directly than intracultural Japa- nese negotiators **(Table** 2, Row 1, Column 1, ẞ 41). **In** support of Hypothesis 2, intracultural U.S. negotiators were less **likely than** intracultural Japanese negotiators to share information indirectly **(Table** 2, Row 1, Column 2, B \-.49). **In** support of Hypothe- **sis 3**, intracultural U.S. negotiators were less **likely** than intracul- tural Japanese negotiators to use influence (**Table** 2, Row 1, Column 3, B **\-.41**). Because Japanese intracultural negotiators were the reference category, we can also infer the characteristic profile of Japanese negotiators from **these** beta weights. Japanese intracultural negotiators used less direct information exchange, more indirect information exchange, and more influence than U.S. intracultural negotiators.

The **results** in Table **2** support Hypothesis 4 that in an intercul- tural setting, the Japanese negotiators adapted to U.S. normative negotiation behaviors, but the U.S. negotiators did not adapt to Japanese culturally normative behaviors. Japanese intercultural negotiators were more likely to share information directly (Ta- ble 2, Row 3, Column 1, ß 41\), **less** likely to share information indirectly (Table 2, Row 3, Column 2, B \- .39), and less likely to **use** influence (Table 2, Row 3, Column 3, B **\-.49**) than Japanese intracultural negotiators. These findings suggest that in an intercultural setting**,** Japanese negotiators were moving away from Japanese normative behaviors toward U.S. normative behaviors. 

Table 3 presents the results of exploratory data analyses running the same logistic regression model with U.S. intracultural negoti- ators as the reference category. The nonsignificant beta coeffi- cients for direct and indirect information exchange in Row 3   
indicate that Japanese intercultural negotiators were as likely as U.S. intracultural negotiators to use direct and indirect information\-sharing behaviors. These findings show **that** Japanese intercultural negotiators fully adapted to U.S. normative negotia- tion behaviors. **We** also found **that** Japanese intercultural negoti- ators were even less likely to use power-based influence than U.S. intracultural negotiators **(Table** 3, Row 3, Column 3, ß \~.08). These results suggest **that in** adapting U.S. normative behaviors regarding influence**,** the Japanese intercultural negotiators overshot the U.S. norm. 

The **results** of the logistic regressions in Table 2 show further support for Hypothesis 4, that unlike Japanese intercultural nego- tiators, the U.S. intercultural negotiators continued to use primarily their culturally normative behaviors**.** The data in Row *2* show that similar to U.S. intracultural negotiators, U.S. intercultural negoti- ators were more likely to share information directly (Table 2\.

Row 2, Column 1, B .31), less likely to share information indirectly (Table 2, Row 2, Column 2, B   
\-.35), and less likely to use influence (Table 2, Row 2, Column 3, p \-.35) than Japanese intracultural negotiators. 

The exploratory data analyses presented in Table 3 also support the finding that U.S. intercultural negotiators did not adapt their culturally normative behaviors. Nonsignificant beta coefficients for all three dependent variables in Table 3, Row 1 show that U.S. intercultural negotiators used direct information exchange, indirect information exchange, and influence tactics with the same relative frequency as U.S. intracultural negotiators. 

Hypothesis 5 proposed that intercultural negotiators might not have been motivated to adapt. The ANOVA comparing transcript length across types of dyads was significant, *F(*2, 85\) \= 29.06, *p* .01. However, post hoc Bonferroni tests did not support Hypoth- esis 5, that intercultural dyads would use fewer speaking turns than intracultural dyads. Instead, U.S. intracultural dyads ***(M*** 213.28, SD 75.77) and the intercultural dyads (M 197.27, *SD* \= 81.72) had a greater number of speaking turns than Japanese intracultural dyads (*M* 77.95, *SD*   
28.18). 

Hypothesis 6 proposed that intercultural negotiators might lack the skills to adapt. The results in Table 2 support Hypothesis 6. Both U.S. intercultural negotiators (Table 2, Row 2, Column 4, B \= 40) and Japanese intercultural negotiators (Table 2, Row 3, Column 4, *B* .30) were more likely than Japanese intracultural negotiators to make clarifying statements. Likewise, the explor- atory analyses presented in Table 3 show that U.S. intercultural negotiators (Table 3, Row 1, Column 4, B .17) were more likely *than* U.S. intracultural negotiators to make clarifying statements. The results also indicate that Japanese intracultural negotiators (Table 3, Row 2, Column 4, B \-.23) were less likely than U.S. intracultural negotiators to make clarifying remarks. These results indicate **a** continuum in which intercultural dyads used the greatest amount of clarification, U.S. intracultural negotiators used **inter-** mediate levels of clarification, and Japanese intracultural negoti- ators used the least clarification. Low relative frequencies of clarification are also indirect evidence of high-context communi- cation by the Japanese intracultural negotiators. 

The results in Tables 2 and 3 do not support Hypothesis 7, that intercultural negotiators would be more likely than intracultural negotiators to discuss negotiation procedures. The only significant   
difference was in a direction opposite of our prediction: Japanese intercultural negotiators made fewer procedural remarks than U.S. intracultural negotiators (Table 3, Row 3, Column 5, ß \--.20). Table ***2*** also presents the beta coefficients for control variables. The results indicate that holding other factors constant, role had a significant negative effect on direct information exchange (Ta- ble 2, Row 4, Column 1; buyers were more likely to share infor- mation directly) and a significant positive effect on influence and procedural comments (Table 2, Row 4, Column 3; sellers were more likely to use influence and refer to procedure). There was a significant negative effect for age on direct information exchange (Table 2, Row 5, Column 1; older participants were less likely to exchange information directly). 

There was a significant positive effect for individualism on indirect information exchange (Table 2, Row 6, Column 2). Be- cause this finding is contrary to prior research that suggests indi- vidualism would be negatively associated with *the* use of indirect communication (Cohen, 1991; Hall, 1976; Singelis & Brown**,** 1995; Ting**\-**Toomey, 1985), we did **an** exploratory analysis**,** adding interaction terms into the equation. The U.S. Intercultural by Individualism interaction term was significant (ẞ \= .03, *SE* \= .01; p \< .01), indicating that only in the U.S. intercultural group was individualism significantly related to indirect information sharing. U.S. intercultural negotiators who valued individualism more were more likely to use indirect information exchange than were those who valued individualism less. Hierarchy had a significant positive effect on clarification (Table 2, Row 7*,* Column 4), indicating that hierarchical negotiators were more likely to make clarifying 

statements**.** 

The control variable for transcript length was negatively related to all the dependent variables, indicating that the conversation was varied in longer negotiations **and** not focused on one or another of our dependent variables. The control variable for location of a speaking turn within a transcript was significant and negative, indicating that holding other factors constant, the dependent vari- ables of direct information **exchange**, influence, clarification**,** and procedural comments were used more frequently toward the be- ginning of the negotiation. 

The control variable for multiple codes within a speaking turn was positively related to indirect information exchange, influence, and procedural statements but negatively related to clarification.

These **results** suggest that when a speaking turn contained multiple codes, one of those codes was likely to be indirect information exchange, influence, or a procedural comment. Speaking turns containing more than one code were unlikely to contain a clarify- ing comment. 

Finally, there were significant positive effects for reciprocity on all dependent variables except direct information exchange. This indicates that across all dyads, when one party used indirect information sharing, influence, clarification, or procedural re- marks, the other party was likely to respond with similar behavior. There was a positive effect for consistency on all dependent variables. The consistency effect means that when one party used a particular behavior, that party was likely to use it again in his subsequent speaking turn. Prior use was positively related to all dependent variables. These effects capture the dynamics of the negotiating process and indicate that a pattern of repetition char- acterized these negotiations. 

*Elasticity* 

Table 4 presents elasticities for the primary independent vari- ables and the five negotiation behaviors for which we hypothe- sized and found differences. Elasticities report the percentage change in the probability of a hypothesized event (an instance of the dependent variable) for a one**\-**unit change in an explanatory variable. This allows us to interpret the net effect of an indepen- dent variable on a dependent variable's probability, holding all other variables in the equation constant. The elasticities are com- puted with a simple transformation: *b,(*p;) (1 *P*), where *b,* is the independent variable's beta coefficient from the logistic regression and *p*, is the proportion of all cases in which the dependent variable was observed **(**Bohrnstedt & Knoke, 1982). This transformation   
offers an easier interpretation than the raw beta coefficients that represent a change in log odds. For example, the beta coefficient reported in Table 3 for U.S. intracultural negotiators predicting direct information exchange was 41, indicating that for a U.S. intracultural negotiator, the log odds of direct information ex- change increased by 41 over the log odds of the same behavior for a Japanese intracultural negotiator (the reference category). As indicated in Table 4, the probability transformation for this rela- tionship is 11.48%, meaning that U.S. intracultural negotiators were 11.48% more likely to share information directly than Japa- nese intracultural negotiators. 

*Negotiation* Behaviors and *Joint Gains* in *Intra- Versus Intercultural Negotiation* 

To test the hypotheses that culturally normative behaviors would account for different joint gains in intra- and intercultural dyads, we ran two sets of analyses. First, we compared U.S. intracultural negotiators and U.S.-Japanese negotiators (N 64 dyads), which *we* call the U.S. contrast data set. Then we com- pared Japanese intracultural negotiators and U.S.-Japanese inter- cultural negotiators (*N* 46 dyads), which we call the Japanese contrast data set. Within each data set, we looked at correlations between cultural group membership, the three process variables under investigation, and joint gains at the dyad level of analysis **(**Table 5). For the U.S. data, we found that cultural group mem- bership was negatively correlated with joint gains (r \-.45, p s .01), indicating that U.S. intracultural negotiators generated greater joint gains than U.S.-Japanese intercultural negotiators. In the Japanese contrast data set, we found that cultural group member- ship was also negatively correlated with joint gains **(r**:

.01), indicating that Japanese intracultural negotiators also gener- ated greater joint gains than did intercultural negotiators. 

In the U.S. contrast data set, direct information sharing was positively related to joint gains (r 21, *p* \= .05), and indirect 

information sharing was negatively related to joint gains (r \-23, p.05). Influence was not significantly related to joint gains. In the Japanese contrast data set, direct information ex- change was not related to joint gains. Indirect information **ex-** change (*r* .19, p.10) and influence (r .25*, p* \= .05) were both positively related to joint gains. Although the correlations were generally small, reflecting substantial within**\-**group variance on the negotiation process variables, they did support our **expec-** tations that culturally normative behaviors would be positively related to the generation of joint gains. 

We then used regression analyses. Because our unit of analysis is the dyad and our group sizes are quite small, we tested each intervening variable separately. In the U.S. contrast data set, direct information accounted for 4% of the variance in joint gains, *F*(1, 62\) 2.81, p.10, and cultural group membership accounted for 20% of the variance in joint gains, *F*(1, 62\) 15.29, p \< .01. When we added direct information to the group membership equation, the predicted variance in joint gains increased slightly to 21%, *F*(2, 61) 8.33, p \= .01. This indicates that 3% of the variance in outcome accounted for by direct information was variance that direct information held in common with group mem- bership. Thus, direct information partially mediated the relation- ship between cultural group membership and joint gains. In other words, a small part, though not all, of the differences in joint gains of U.S. intracultural negotiators and U.S. and Japanese intercul- tural negotiators can be explained by their differential use of direct information. 

We used the Japanese contrast data set to test whether indirect information accounted for group membership effects on joint gains. Indirect information accounted for 4% of the variance in joint gains **(**Model *F* not significant). Cultural group membership in this data set accounted for 10% of the variance in joint gains, *F(*1, 44\) \= 4.79, *p* \= .05. When we added indirect information to the group membership equation, the predicted variance in joint gains remained at 10%, *F*(2, 43\) 2.35, p.10. This indicates that the 4% of the variance in outcome accounted for by indirect information was variance that indirect information held in com- mon with group membership. Thus, indirect information partially mediated the relationship between cultural group membership and joint gains. In other words, a small part, though not all, of the differences in joint gains of U.S. intracultural negotiators and U.S. and Japanese intercultural negotiators can be explained by their differential use of indirect information. 

We used the Japanese contrast data set to test whether influence accounted for group membership effects on joint gains. Influence accounted for 6% of the variance in joint gains, *F*(1, 44\)   
4.79, 

*p* \= .05. Cultural\-group membership in this data set accounted for 10% of the variance in joint gains, *F*(1, 44\) \= 4.79, p \< .05. **When** we added influence to the group membership equation, the pre- dicted variance in joint gains increased slightly, to 11%, *F(***2**, 43\)   
2.55, p.10. This indicates that 5% of the variance in outcome accounted for by influence was variance that influence held in common with group membership. Thus, influence partially mediated the relationship between cultural group membership and joint gains. In other words, a small part, though not all, of the   
differences in joint gains of U.S. intracultural negotiators and U.S. and Japanese intercultural negotiators can be explained by their differential **use** of influence. 

Discussion 

The results show significant behavioral differences between U.S. and Japanese intracultural negotiators. They show that Japa- nese intercultural negotiators adapted their behavior to U.S. norms and that intercultural negotiators, especially the Japanese, spent more effort clarifying than did either group of intracultural nego- tiators. Despite these efforts, intercultural negotiations generated significantly less joint gains than intracultural negotiations. These results have both theoretical **and** practical implications. They also suggest avenues for future research. 

*Culturally Normative Negotiation Behaviors* 

U.S. and Japanese negotiators emphasized different behaviors in negotiation. The U.S. negotiators relied on direct information to **learn** about each other's preferences and priorities and to integrate this information to generate joint gains. They were comfortable sharing information about priorities, comparing and contrasting their preferences **with** those of the other party, and giving specific feedback to **offers** and proposals. The Japanese **negotiators** relied on indirect information, inferring each other's preferences and priorities from multiple **offers** and counteroffers over time. **Rela-** tive to the U.S. intracultural negotiators, the Japanese intracultural negotiators made significantly more **offers**. 

**These** results **are** consistent with prior **research** identifying both direct and indirect information exchange as negotiation behaviors correlated with integrative agreements (Pruitt, 1981\)**.** They extend prior research on negotiation process in three ways. **First,** the study identifies culture as a factor associated with the relative frequency **with** which negotiators **use** direct and indirect information\-sharing negotiation behaviors**.** Second, the relative use of direct and indi- rect information-sharing negotiation behaviors by U.S. and Japa- **nese** negotiators is consistent with the low- and high-context communication patterns characteristic of the U.S. and Japanese cultures **(Hall**, 1976). Third, the findings show that when used in a culturally consistent way, both direct and indirect information sharing can generate joint gains. 

Brett et al.'s (1998) multiculture comparative study of cultural values**,** norms, and joint gains suggests a possible boundary **on** the results of this study. Among six cultures studied, they found that negotiators from Japan and the United **States** had the strongest norms for information sharing in negotiations and negotiated the highest joint gains. In this study, Hong Kong and **Russia** were classified as high-context cultures and France and Brazil as low- context cultures. Negotiators from these four cultures had weaker information-sharing norms than the U.S. or Japanese negotiators and realized low to moderate joint gains. Thus, facility **in** direct or indirect communications may not lead to joint gains if parties do not also have a norm for information sharing. 

There are two practical implications **of** our findings: The first is that **if** negotiators are seeking an integrative agreement, it is important to focus on sharing as well **as** seeking information. The second is **that** negotiators need an information **exchange** engine and that either direct or indirect information sharing will do.   
NEGOTIATION BEHAVIOR WHEN CULTURES COLLIDE 

The U.S. and Japanese negotiators' behavior differed in another significant way. The Japanese negotiators were more likely to use influence, and U.S. negotiators eschewed power tactics. This be- havioral difference confirmed **our** predictions based on hierarchi- cal and egalitarian cultural differences. The results were also consistent with Brett and Okumura's (1998) findings that the Japanese negotiators thought that using distributive tactics in ne- gotiation was more acceptable than did the U.S. negotiators. The U.S. negotiators' relatively low use of influence behaviors was consistent with Pruitt's (1981) findings that integrative negotiators share information and eschew power tactics. However, the Japa- nese negotiators' behavior was inconsistent with Pruitt's findings. The Japanese were able to negotiate high joint gains not only while sharing information indirectly but also **while** engaging in distrib- utive negotiation behavior, including legitimacy; informational persuasion; information with sanctions, such as threats; and sympathy. 

The Japanese negotiators avoided the trap of a distributive agreement that commonly affects U.S. negotiators when they engage in contentious, distributive tactics (Brett et al., 1998; Pruitt, 1981; Putnam & Jones, 1982; Weingart et al., 1990\). Why they were able to do so is a question for future research. It is possible that in hierarchical cultures, where the **use** of influence tactics in negotiation is normative and information sharing is indirect in the form of offers, the use of distributive tactics may motivate search. Pruitt (1983) hinted that distributive tactics may motivate search when pointing out that contentious tactics that point out the neg- ative consequences of failure to negotiate may encourage a reluc- tant negotiator to come to the table. Pruitt and Gleason (1978) also suggested that mutual threat capacity may encourage coordination if there is a minimal tendency to use it. However, neither of the contexts Pruitt refers to is similar to the context in which the Japanese negotiators in our study used distributive tactics and still negotiated integrative agreements. We think that in the Japanese culture, when both parties come to the table feeling powerful, the need to resolve power differences may motivate search. Graham and Sano (1989) noted that it is normative for Japanese negotiators to use power tactics, to stand firm, and to try to sway the other party. March (1990) suggests that the fear of loss of face and conviction that their position is right motivates Japanese negotia- tors to "push, push, push" (p. 140\). 

The data on the Japanese negotiators indicate that it is possible to negotiate integrative agreements even when making use of distributive tactics, so long as search for alternative outcomes also continues. The Japanese can do this. The question remains whether U.S. negotiators and negotiators from other cultures can success- fully combine integrative negotiation behaviors (information ex- change) with distributive tactics. 

*Culture Clash* 

Our results show that culturally normative negotiation behaviors partially explain why intracultural negotiators generated greater joint gains than intercultural negotiators. Negotiators are most comfortable and effective when they use behaviors **that** are cus- tomary and natural for them. In an intracultural setting, culturally normative negotiation behaviors allow effective communication and sufficient motivation to identify integrative solutions that generate joint gains. In intercultural negotiations, ineffective and   
381 

frustrated communication may be a primary consequence of clash- ing normative behaviors that hinders the generation of joint gains. One way that intercultural negotiators respond to facilitate their interaction when normative negotiation behaviors clash is behav- ioral adaptation. 

Our results suggest that there is more to successful intercultural negotiation than adaptation to another culture's behavioral pattern. The Japanese intercultural negotiators adapted to the behaviors exhibited by the U.S. intracultural negotiators. Intercultural U.S. negotiators, like U.S. intracultural negotiators, shared information directly and avoided influence tactics. Japanese intercultural ne- gotiators increased direct information sharing and decreased indi- rect information sharing relative to the base rate in Japanese intracultural negotiation. Their rates of direct and indirect infor- mation sharing were not different from the base rates of U.S. intracultural negotiators. These results confirmed our prediction that for situational and cultural reasons, the Japanese negotiators would be more likely to adapt. However, the results do not explain why**,** given the adaptation of the Japanese negotiators, intercultural negotiations were less integrative than intracultural negotiations. 

Our results suggest that one possible explanation is that inter- cultural negotiators lacked sufficient skills to adapt successfully. We found that intercultural negotiators were using significantly more clarifying statements than intracultural negotiators. This shows that despite behavioral adaptation, intercultural negotiators had trouble understanding the interests of the other party. By making repetitive, confirmatory, or clarifying statements, intercul- tural negotiators tried to gain a better understanding of the other party's interests and priorities. We also found that hierarchical negotiators were more likely to make clarifying statements. Be- cause we know that Japanese intracultural negotiators made fewer clarifying statements than the other groups, these results suggest that it was primarily the Japanese intercultural negotiators who were focused on sending and receiving clear messages. This ex- plains why Brett and Okumura (1998) found that Japanese inter- cultural negotiators understood the priorities of the U.S. negotia- tors but that U.S. intercultural negotiators did not understand the priorities of the Japanese negotiators. 

Japanese intercultural negotiators also made fewer procedural statements than other negotiators. It seems that in the intercultural dyads, the U.S. negotiators tried to discuss procedures, but the Japanese negotiators did not respond. The intercultural negotiators may have improved understanding and outcomes if both parties had focused on sending and receiving accurate information and on procedures to develop a common understanding of each party's expectations and approach. 

Another possible explanation for low joint gains is that inter- cultural negotiators lacked motivation to generate integrative so- lutions. We do not think that intercultural negotiators closed pre- maturely out of frustration because we did not find direct evidence of intercultural negotiators closing prematurely or satisficing based on the number of speaking turns in the transcripts. Also, Brett and Okumura (1998) reported that intercultural negotiators were more happy and satisfied after negotiation than were intracultural negotiators. 

Our results are consistent with an in-group-out-group explana- tion for low motivation to generate joint gains in the intercultural dyads. The literature on individualism-collectivism and in-group- out-group behavior suggests that collectivist cultures will negotiate   
382   
ADAIR, OKUMURA, AND BRETT 

cooperatively with an in-group but competitively with an out- group (Triandis, 1989). In the intercultural setting, collectivist Japanese negotiators were negotiating with an out-group: individ- ualist U.S. negotiators. Perhaps Japanese intercultural negotiators were more interested in claiming distributive gains than in nego- tiating joint gains with the U.S. negotiators. Recall that Brett and Okumura (1998) reported that the Japanese intercultural negotia- tors had relatively accurate postnegotiation understanding of the U.S. intercultural negotiators' preferences but that the U.S. nego- tiators did not have comparable understanding of the Japanese intercultural negotiators' preferences. Thus, it was only the Japa- nese negotiators who had sufficient understanding to construct integrative agreements in the intercultural negotiations, and they did not do so. This clearly suggests to us a motivational decrement. On the other hand, it must be noted that had the Japanese inter- cultural negotiators engaged in culturally normative influence be- haviors, they may have found themselves in a conflict spiral with U.S. negotiators, for whom such behavior is not normative, and reached an equally poor integrative agreement. 

*Limitations and Future Directions* 

One limitation of this study is that we only measured indirect information sharing in terms of Pruitt's (1981) heuristic trial and error exchange of offers. Another form of indirect communication discussed in the literature on high\-context communication that might be relevant to indirect information sharing **in** negotiation is conveying information through storytelling (March, 1990). Be- cause the data used in this study were collected in a simulation, contextual information was limited, and storytelling did not occur. However, storytelling should not be overlooked in operationalizing indirect information sharing in nousimulated negotiations. 

This study identifies culturally normative negotiation behaviors and shows the difficulty of negotiating interculturally when these behaviors clash. We leave for future research the task of discov- ering why functionally equivalent but conceptually different ne- gotiation behaviors characterize different cultures. We anticipate that the different approaches to negotiation that we identified in this study are just one part of an integrated approach to interper- sonal communication that varies by culture. Another avenue for future research is highlighted by the fact that our behavioral measures accounted for relatively little variance in the joint gains generated by intra- versus intercultural negotiators. We studied frequency of behaviors. Future research might consider reciprocity or other patterns of behaviors to understand why joint gains are not comparable for intra- and intercultural negotiators. 

Our Japanese intercultural negotiators were senior Japanese managers who did not have U.S.-style MBA training. Presumably, the adaptation process would have been even cleaner if the Japa- nese negotiators had been trained in the United States, where direct styles of communication and interaction are reinforced. Future research should examine whether similar training could lead to more similar interaction styles, perhaps reducing the in-group- out-group effect and increasing joint gains for intercultural dyads. 

*Conclusion* 

U.S. and Japanese negotiators favored different behaviors when negotiating intraculturally. When placed in an intercultural con-   
text, Japanese negotiators adopted behaviors normative to the U.S. negotiators. However, despite the Japanese negotiators' adapta- tion, the intercultural agreements were significantly less integra- tive than the intracultural ones. The Japanese intercultural negoti- ators had the information to develop integrative agreements, but they failed to do so. The U.S. intercultural negotiators did not have the information to develop integrative agreements**,** though this was not for lack of the Japanese negotiators**'** direct information-sharing and clarifying behavior. 

These results suggest that one party's adaptation to the other party's culturally normative negotiation behaviors is not sufficient to generate integrative agreements. Instead, negotiators need to have an effective means of information sharing and be motivated to search for agreements that are highly acceptable to both parties. In U.S.-Japanese negotiations, this means that negotiators from both culnires should become facile with direct and indirect infor- mation sharing. When messages are unclear or protocol is uncer- tain, negotiators should turn to clarifying and procedural state- ments to develop a common understanding and approach. Negotiators from both cultures should also be aware of what appears to be motivating the other group and use that behavior to motivate search, as opposed to entering into a distributive contest.
