And like me I bet you like crisp, clean water with no chemical aftertaste, like Mother Nature made it.” “Well, yes, but . . .” 

Who is this guy with a fake smile in his voice, you wonder, who thinks he can trick you into buying something you don’t want? You feel your muscles tighten, your voice go defensive, and your heart rate accelerate. 

You feel like his prey, and you are\!   
The last thing you want to do is say “Yes,” even when it’s the only way to answer, “Do you drink water?” Compromise and concession, even to the truth, feels like defeat. And “No,” well, “No” feels like salvation, like an oasis. You’re tempted to use “No” when it’s blatantly untrue, just to hear its sweet sound. “*No*, I do *not* need water, carbon filtered or otherwise. *I’m a camel\!*” 

Now let’s think about this selling technique. It’s designed to get to “Yes” at all costs, as if “No” were death. And for many of us it is. We have all these negative connotations with “No.” We talk about the rejection of “No,” about the fear of hearing it. “No” is the ultimate negative word.  
But at the end of the day, “Yes” is often a meaningless answer that hides deeper objections (and “Maybe” is even worse). Pushing hard for “Yes” doesn’t get a negotiator any closer to a win; it just angers the other side. 

So if “Yes” can be so damn uncomfortable, and “No” such a relief, why have we fetishized one and demonized the other? We have it backward. For good negotiators, “No” is pure gold. That negative provides a great opportunity for you and the other party to clarify what you really want by eliminating what you don’t want. “No” is a safe choice that maintains the status quo; it provides a temporary oasis of control. 

At some point in their development, all negotiators have to come to grips with “No.” When you come to realize the real psychological dynamic behind it, you’ll love the word. It’s not just that you lose your fear of it, but that you come to learn what it does for you and how you can build deals out of it. 

“Yes” and “Maybe” are often worthless. But “No” always alters the conversation. 

**“NO” STARTS THE NEGOTIATION** 

My fascination with “No” in all its beautiful nuance began with a conversation I had a few months before my negotiation career began. 

I started my career with the Bureau as a member of the FBI SWAT team in the Pittsburgh Division but after nearly two years I was transferred to New York, where the FBI attached me to the Joint Terrorism Task Force (JTTF). It was an amazing post: We spent our days and nights tracking suspected terrorists, investigating their cells, and assessing whether or how they might strike. We were untying knots of human anger in the midst of America’s biggest city, making life-and-death decisions on who was dangerous and who was just blowing hot air. The work fascinated me. 

Ever since my first days with the Bureau, I had been obsessed with crisis response. The immediacy of the task enthralled me. The stakes were high. Lives hung in the balance.  
The emotional terrain was complex, changing, and often conflicting. To successfully gain a hostage’s safe release, a negotiator had to penetrate the hostage-taker’s motives, state of mind, intelligence, and emotional strengths and weaknesses. The negotiator played the role of bully, conciliator, enforcer, savior, confessor, instigator, and peacemaker—and that’s just a few of the parts. 

I thought I was cut out for every one of them.   
A few weeks after I got to Manhattan, I showed up at the desk of Amy Bonderow, who ran the FBI’s Crisis Negotiation Team in New York. I didn’t know beans about negotiating, so I went for the direct approach. 

“I want be a hostage negotiator,” I said.   
“Everyone does—got any training?” she asked.   
“No,” I said.   
“Any credentials?”   
“Nope.” I answered.   
“Any experience?” she asked.   
“No,” I answered.   
“Do you have a degree in psychology, sociology, *anything* at all related to negotiation?” 

“No.”   
“Looks like you answered your own question,” she said. “No. Now go away.” 

“Go away?” I protested. “Really?”   
“Yep. As in, ‘Leave me alone.’ Everybody wants to be a hostage negotiator, and you have no résumé, experience, or skills. So what would you say in my position? You got it: ‘No.’” 

I paused in front of her, thinking, This is not how my negotiating career ends. I had stared down terrorists; I wasn’t going to just leave. “Come on,” I said. “There has to be *something* I can do.” Amy shook her head and gave one of those ironic laughs that mean the person doesn’t think you’ve got a snowball’s chance in hell. 

“I’ll tell you what. Yes, there is something you can do: Volunteer at a suicide hotline. Then come talk to me. No guarantees, got it?” she said. “Now, seriously, *go away*.”  
My conversation with Amy kicked off my awareness of the complex and hidden subtleties of conversation, the power of certain words, the seemingly unintelligible emotional truths that so often underlie intelligible exchanges. 

A trap into which many fall is to take what other people say literally. I started to see that while people played the game of conversation, it was in the game beneath the game, where few played, that all the leverage lived. 

In our chat, I saw how the word “No”—so apparently clear and direct—really wasn’t so simple. Over the years, I’ve thought back repeatedly to that conversation, replaying how Amy so quickly turned me down, again and again. But her “No’s” were just the gateway to “Yes.” They gave her—and me—time to pivot, adjust, and reexamine, and actually created the environment for the one “Yes” that mattered. 

While assigned to the JTTF, I worked with an NYPD lieutenant named Martin. He had a hard shell, and whenever asked for anything he responded with a terse negative. After I’d gotten to know him a bit, I asked him why. “Chris,” he said, proudly, “a lieutenant’s job is to say, ‘No.’” 

At first, I thought that sort of automated response signaled a failure of imagination. But then I realized I did the same thing with my teenage son, and that after I’d said “No” to him, I often found that I was open to hearing what he had to say. 

That’s because having protected myself, I could relax and more easily consider the possibilities. 

“No” is the *start* of the negotiation, not the *end* of it. We’ve been conditioned to fear the word “No.” But it is a statement of perception far more often than of fact. It seldom means, “I have considered all the facts and made a rational choice.” Instead, “No” is often a decision, frequently temporary, to maintain the status quo. Change is scary, and “No” provides a little protection from that scariness. 

Jim Camp, in his excellent book, *Start with NO*,1 counsels the reader to give their adversary (his word for counterpart) permission to say “No” from the outset of a negotiation. He calls it “the right to veto.” He observes that people will fight to the death to preserve their right to say “No,” so give them that right and the negotiating  
environment becomes more constructive and collaborative almost immediately. 

When I read Camp’s book, I realized this was something we’d known as hostage negotiators for years. We’d learned that the quickest way to get a hostage-taker out was to take the time to talk them out, as opposed to “demanding” their surrender. Demanding their surrender, “telling” them to come out, always ended up creating a much longer standoff and occasionally, actually contributed to death. 

It comes down to the deep and universal human need for autonomy. People need to feel in control. When you preserve a person’s autonomy by clearly giving them permission to say “No” to your ideas, the emotions calm, the effectiveness of the decisions go up, and the other party can really look at your proposal. They’re allowed to hold it in their hands, to turn it around. And it gives you time to elaborate or pivot in order to convince your counterpart that the change you’re proposing is more advantageous than the status quo. 

Great negotiators seek “No” because they know that’s often when the real negotiation begins. 

Politely saying “No” to your opponent (we’ll go into this in more depth in Chapter 9), calmly hearing “No,” and just letting the other side know that they are welcome to say “No” has a positive impact on any negotiation. In fact, your invitation for the other side to say “No” has an amazing power to bring down barriers and allow for beneficial communication. 

This means you have to train yourself to hear “No” as something other than rejection, and respond accordingly. When someone tells you “No,” you need to rethink the word in one of its alternative—and much more real—meanings: 

■ I am not yet ready to agree; 

■ You are making me feel uncomfortable; 

■ I do not understand;  
■ I don’t think I can afford it; 

■ I want something else; 

■ I need more information; or 

■ I want to talk it over with someone else. 

Then, after pausing, ask solution-based questions or simply label their effect: 

“What about this doesn’t work for you?”   
“What would you need to make it work?”   
“It seems like there’s something here that bothers you.” People have a need to say, “No.” So don’t just hope to hear it at some point; get them to say it early. 

**PERSUADE IN THEIR WORLD** 

I’d like to present you with a guy named Joe Businessman as he readies himself for a negotiation. You’ve met him before. He’s the prepared type, with all his *Getting to Yes* strategies written out and memorized. And he’s *more* than ready to unleash them on the guy across the table. Joe pauses to look at his expensive suit in the mirror, fantasizing about the impressive things he’ll say and the fancy charts and graphs that’ll back up those things and leave his counterpart—his *opponent*—vanquished and in defeat. He is Russell Crowe in *Gladiator*. He is The Man. 

Now allow me to let you in on a secret: None of that preparation will mean a damn thing. His negotiation style is all me, me, me, ego, ego, ego. And when the people on the other side of the table pick up those signals, they’re going to decide that it’s best to politely, even furtively, ignore this Superman . . . by saying “Yes”\! 

“Huh?” you say.   
Sure, the word they’ll say right off is “Yes,” but that word is only a tool to get this blowhard to go away. They’ll weasel out later, claiming changing conditions, budget issues, the weather. For now, they just want to be released because Joe isn’t convincing them of anything; he’s only convincing himself.  
I’ll let you in on a secret. There are actually three kinds of “Yes”: Counterfeit, Confirmation, and Commitment. 

A counterfeit “yes” is one in which your counterpart plans on saying “no” but either feels “yes” is an easier escape route or just wants to disingenuously keep the conversation going to obtain more information or some other kind of edge. A confirmation “yes” is generally innocent, a reflexive response to a black-or-white question; it’s sometimes used to lay a trap but mostly it’s just simple affirmation with no promise of action. And a commitment “yes” is the real deal; it’s a true agreement that leads to action, a “yes” at the table that ends with a signature on the contract. The commitment “yes” is what you want, but the three types sound almost the same so you have to learn how to recognize which one is being used. 

Human beings the world over are so used to being pursued for the commitment “yes” as a condition to find out more that they have become masters at giving the counterfeit “yes.” That’s what the people facing Joe Businessman are doing, dangling the counterfeit “yes” so they can hear more. 

Whether you call it “buy-in” or “engagement” or something else, good negotiators know that their job isn’t to put on a great performance but to gently guide their counterpart to discover their goal as his own. 

Let me tell you, I learned that the hard way.   
Two months after talking with Amy, I started answering phones for HelpLine, the crisis hotline founded by Norman Vincent Peale. The basic rule was that you couldn’t be with anybody on the phone for more than twenty minutes. If you did your job, it wasn’t going to take you longer than that to get them to a better place. We had a thick book of organizations we referred them to for help. It was a paramedic approach: patch them up and send them on their way. But people in crisis only accounted for about 40 percent of the calls we got. The majority of the calls came from frequent callers. These are highly dysfunctional people, energy vampires whom no one else would listen to anymore. 

We kept a list of frequent callers and when you got one, the first thing you had to do was check to see if the person had called that day, because they were only allowed one call a day. They knew it,  
too. A lot of times, they’d say, “Yeah, I’m Eddie. I haven’t called yet today. Go ahead and check the list. You got to talk to me.” Since I was there primarily to learn a skill, I loved the frequent callers. They were a problem, and I loved trying to figure them out. I felt I had some talent at it. I felt like a superstar. 

When it came time for my performance review, they assigned me a shift supervisor named Jim Snyder. Jim was a hotline veteran and a sweetheart; the only problem was he always wanted to joke around. Jim understood that volunteer burnout was the biggest problem at a hotline, so he dedicated his time to making work fun. I became good friends with Jim. 

For my review, Jim waited until I got a call and went into the monitoring room where the supervisors could listen to our calls. The call was from one of my frequent clients, a cabbie with a fear of going outside and plenty of time to tell me about it. This energy vampire (his name was Daryl) launched into his shtick about how he was going to lose his house and with it his will to live if he couldn’t work. 

“Seriously, when was the last time someone tried to hurt you on the streets?” I asked. 

“Well, I mean, it’s been a long time,” Daryl said.   
“Like . . . ?”   
“I can’t really remember a date, Chris. Maybe a year, I guess.” “So it’s safe to say that the outside world hasn’t been *too* hard on you, right?” 

“Yes,” Daryl said. “I suppose so.”   
We went back and forth like this for a while, as I made him admit that most of us had little to fear in the world. I was feeling good about my new skills, about listening to Daryl and then “CareFronting” him, which was the slightly goofy name we gave to assertively—but caringly—responding to frequent callers. 

It was all flowing, and our rapport was great. I even got Daryl to laugh a few times. By the time I was done with him, he couldn’t give me one reason not to step outside. 

“Thank you, Chris,” Daryl said just before he hung up. “Thanks for doing such a great job.”  
Before I went to see Jim, I leaned back in my chair and basked in that compliment. How often do you get that from a man in pain, I thought. Then I sprung up and strode toward the monitoring room, so proud I was practically buffing my nails on my shirt and patting my own back. 

Jim motioned me to the chair in front of him and gave me his biggest smile. I must have returned it with twice the wattage. “Well, Chris,” he said, still smiling. “That was one of the worst calls I ever heard.” 

I stared at him, gape-jawed.   
“Jim, did you hear Daryl congratulate me?” I asked. “I talked him down, man. I killed it.” 

Jim smiled—I hated that smile right then—and nodded. “That’s one of the signs, because they should be congratulating themselves when they get off the line,” he said. “They don’t need to be congratulating you. That tells me you did too much. If they think you did it—if you were the guy who killed it—how is he going to help himself? I don’t want to be harsh, but you were horrible.” As I listened to what Jim said, I felt that acid stomach rush you get when you are forced to accept that the guy dumping on you is completely right. Daryl’s response had been a kind of “yes,” but it had been anything but a true commitment “yes.” He’d made no promise to action. His “yes” had been designed to make me feel good enough to leave him alone. Daryl may not have known it, but his “yes” was as counterfeit as they came. 

You see, that whole call had been about me and my ego and not the caller. But the only way to get these callers to take action was to have them own the conversation, to believe that they were coming to these conclusions, to these necessary next steps, and that the voice at the other end was simply a medium for those realizations. 

Using all your skills to create rapport, agreement, and connection with a counterpart is useful, but ultimately that connection is useless unless the other person feels that they are equally as responsible, if not *solely* responsible, for creating the connection and the new ideas they have. 

I nodded slowly, the fight drained out of me.   
“One of the worst calls?” I said to Jim. “That’s right.”  
I worked hard at reorienting myself from that point on. I asked so many questions and read so much about it that soon they had me teaching two classes for new volunteers at HelpLine: the opening class, on active listening; and the one on CareFrontation. 

Got it, you say. It’s not about me. We need to persuade from their perspective, not ours. But how? 

By starting with their most basic wants.   
In every negotiation, in every agreement, the result comes from someone else’s decision. And sadly, if we believe that we can control or manage others’ decisions with compromise and logic, we’re leaving millions on the table. But while we can’t control others’ decisions, we can influence them by inhabiting their world and seeing and hearing exactly what they want. 

Though the intensity may differ from person to person, you can be sure that everyone you meet is driven by two primal urges: the need to feel safe and secure, and the need to feel in control. If you satisfy those drives, you’re in the door. 

As we saw with my chat with Daryl, you’re not going to logically *convince* them that they’re safe, secure, or in control. Primal needs are urgent and illogical, so arguing them into a corner is just going to push your counterpart to flee with a counterfeit “Yes.” 

And being “nice” in the form of feigned sympathy is often equally as unsuccessful. We live in an age that celebrates niceness under various names. We are exhorted to be nice and to respect people’s feelings at all times and in every situation. 

But nice alone in the context of negotiation can backfire. Nice, employed as a ruse, is disingenuous and manipulative. Who hasn’t received the short end of the stick in dealings with a “nice” salesman who took you for a ride? If you rush in with plastic niceness, your bland smile is going to dredge up all that baggage. 

Instead of getting inside with logic or feigned smiles, then, we get there by asking for “No.” It’s the word that gives the speaker feelings of safety and control. “No” starts conversations and creates safe havens to get to the final “Yes” of commitment. An early “Yes” is often just a cheap, counterfeit dodge.  
About five months after she’d told me to “go away,” I stopped by Amy Bonderow’s office and told her that I’d volunteered at HelpLine. “You did?” she asked, smiling with surprise. “I tell everybody to do that. And nobody ever does.” 

It turned out that Amy had started her negotiating career by volunteering at the same place. She started naming people who were now mutual friends of ours. We laughed about Jim. 

In a sudden shift, Amy stopped speaking and stared at me. I shifted in my shoes as she gave me the Pause. Then she smiled. “You get the next position.” 

At that time, there were five other people aiming for the same slot, people who had psychology degrees, experience, and credentials. But I was on the road to the next hostage negotiation training course at the FBI Academy in Quantico, Virginia, ahead of everybody else. My career as a negotiator had officially begun. 

**“NO” IS PROTECTION** 

Think back to the telemarketer at the beginning of this chapter. The obvious reply to his question—“Do you enjoy a nice glass of water?”—is “Yes.” But all you want to do is scream, “No\!” After a question like that you just know the rest of the phone call is going to be painful. 

That, in a nutshell, distills the inherent contradictions in the values we give “Yes” and “No.” Whenever we negotiate, there’s no doubt we want to finish with a “Yes.” But we mistakenly conflate the positive value of that final “Yes” with a positive value of “Yes” in general. And because we see “No” as the opposite of “Yes,” we then assume that “No” is always a bad thing. 

Nothing could be further from the truth. Saying “No” gives the speaker the feeling of safety, security, and control. You use a question that prompts a “No” answer, and your counterpart feels that by turning you down he has proved that he’s in the driver’s seat. Good negotiators welcome—even invite—a solid “No” to start, as a sign that the other party is engaged and thinking. 

Gun for a “Yes” straight off the bat, though, and your counterpart gets defensive, wary, and skittish. That’s why I tell my students that
*Computational Intelligence,* Volume 29, Number 4, 2013 

**A TWO-STAGE WIN–WIN MULTIATTRIBUTE NEGOTIATION MODEL: OPTIMIZATION AND THEN CONCESSION** 

LI PAN,1 XUDONG LUO,2 XIANGXU MENG,1 CHUNYAN MIAO,3 MINGHUA HE,4 AND XINGCHEN GUO5 

1*School of Computer Science and Technology, Shandong University, Jinan, China* 

2*Institute of Logic and Cognition, Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou, China* 

3*School of Computer Engineering, Nanyang Technological University, Singapore* 

4*School of Engineering and Applied Science, Aston University Birmingham, UK* 

5*School of Computer Science, University of Birmingham, Birmingham, UK* 

Many automated negotiation models have been developed to solve the conflict in many distributed computa tional systems. However, the problem of finding win – win outcome in multiattribute negotiation has not been tackled well. To address this issue, based on an evolutionary method of multiobjective optimization, this paper presents a negotiation model that can find win – win solutions of multiple attributes, but needs not to reveal negotiating agents’ private utility functions to their opponents or a third-party mediator. Moreover, we also equip our agents with a general type of utility functions of interdependent multiattributes, which captures human intuitions well. In addition, we also develop a novel time-dependent concession strategy model, which can help both sides find a final agreement among a set of win – win ones. Finally, lots of experiments confirm that our negotiation model outperforms the existing models developed recently. And the experiments also show our model is stable and efficient in finding fair win – win outcomes, which is seldom solved in the existing models. 

Received 25 May 2011; Revised 9 February 2012; Accepted 10 March 2012; Published online 3 July 2012 

*Key words:* automated negotiation, multiattribute decision making, agent, e-business, evolutionary computing. 

**1\. INTRODUCTION** 

Negotiation is a process by which a group of agents communicate with one another to try and come to a mutually agreement on some matter (Lomuscio, Wooldridge, and Jennings 2001). Negotiation systems play an important role in distributed computational systems, such as semantic web (Du 2009), e-commerce (He, Jennings, and Leung 2003; Huang et al. 2010), grid computing (Sim 2010), and so on. In such complex distributed systems, multiple stakeholders are interconnected with one another but no centralized control exists, and so when conflict happens, negotiation is a reasonable way to get them working together (Luo et al. 2012). 

Because multiattribute negotiation is very common in the real world, many systems and models have been developed. Most of them can only deal with independent multiple attributes over the class of linearly additive utility functions (Hindriks, Jonker, and Tykhonov 2009). However, many real-world negotiation problems involve interdependent multiple attributes and thus the utility functions are usually nonlinear. To remove this limitation, in this paper, we present a type of nonlinear utility functions. It captures human intuitions well and can be applied to many real-world scenarios in fields of e-commerce, grid computing, cloud computing, and so on. 

One of key issues in multiattribute negotiation is to find a win–win agreement that cannot make one party’s benefit better off without worsening those of the others. A negotiation system should be able to find win–win solutions because none wants to leave mutual gain on the table. Also, multiple diverse solutions are desired because the more options the easier 

Address correspondence to Xudong Luo, Institute of Logic and Cognition, Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou, China; e-mail: luoxd3@mail.sysu.edu.cn 

 C 2012 Wiley Periodicals, Inc.  
 14678640, 2013, 4, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1467-8640.2012.00434.x by University Cattolica, Piacenza, Wiley Online Library on \[25/05/2026\]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License  
578 COMPUTATIONAL INTELLIGENCE 

to reach an agreement. A good win–win negotiation model should be efficient and have a symmetric bargaining mechanism. That is, it does not treat any party differently on the basis of inappropriate criteria, such as which party goes first in the negotiation. A good negotiation model also should be a one with which the participants are not asked to reveal much of their private information. Thus, the users of negotiating agents can trust the negotiation model and let the agents act on behalf of them. Although there has been much previous work on automated negotiation, not many models of win–win negotiation work well. 

In this paper, we propose a win–win negotiation model based on a fast and elitist multiobjective genetic algorithm NSGA-II (Deb et al. 2002). Basically, we set an agent’s and its opponent’s utility functions as two objectives. However, NSGA-II is centralized and thus it can work only in the situation where two utility functions are public knowledge to both sides in negotiation. So, we cannot employ NSGA-II straightforward in negotiation because both sides would not like to let the other side or a third party know their private utility functions. Thus, we extend the NSGA-II to a distributed one. Further in our model, to reach a final agreement from a set of win–win solutions found by the distributed NSGA-II, we also propose a novel time-dependent concession model that can model various concession strategies. Finally, our experiments show that our win–win negotiation model works well for several linear and nonlinear utility functions and outperforms some existing models. 

The rest of the paper is organized as follows. Section 2 presents a type of nonlinear utility functions of multiple attributes. Section 3 extends NSGA-II to a distributed one and applies it to find a Pareto-front. Section 4 develops novel time-dependent concession strategies to reach a final agreement on the Pareto-front. Section 5 conducts a wide range of experiments to evaluate our negotiation model against the main existing automated negotiation systems. Section 6 discusses the related work. Finally, Section 7 summarizes this paper and points out some future work. 

**2\. UTILITY FUNCTIONS** 

This section presents a type of general utility functions for buyer and seller agents in negotiating a multiattribute trading object. 

2.1. A General Pair of Utility Functions 

Now we first describe a general negotiation scenario for a buyer and a seller agents who are negotiating on a multiattribute object. Suppose the vector of negotiated attributes is (*A*1*,..., An*) and (*a*1*,..., an*) represents a vector of values where *ai* is the value of attribute *Ai* . We use \[*Ai, Ai*\] to represent the acceptable domain of attribute *Ai* . Suppose *Ai* is a negative attribute to the buyer agent, which means the smaller value of *Ai* , the happier the buyer agent. For example, suppose a buyer and a seller agents are negotiating on a price of domain \[50*,* 100\]. The attribute “price” is a negative attribute to the buyer agent and a positive attribute to the seller agent. Suppose for every attribute *Ai* , a value in the middle \[*Ai, Ai*\], which is denoted as *Ai* and calculated as *Ai* \= (*Ai* \+ *Ai*)*/*2, is a neutral value to both the buyer agent and the seller agent, which means they both have neutral attitudes on this attribute value *Ai* . Taking the “price” attribute above as an example, both the buyer agent and the seller agent have neutral attitudes on this price of 75\. A price higher than 75 is good for the seller agent but bad for the buyer agent, and vice versa. Because a negotiating agent may have different preferences on different attributes, we use weight *wi* ∈ \[0*,* 1\] to represent the importance an agent regards attribute *Ai* .   
Now we suppose all the attributes are negative attributes for the buyer agent and present a pair of utility functions for the buyer and seller agents in the scenario described above

**3\. PARETO-FRONT NEGOTIATION** 

Our negotiation model consists of two major stages: (i) negotiation for a set of win–win solutions based on a fast and elitist multiobjective optimization algorithm NSGA-II (Deb

et al. 2002), and (ii) bargaining on the win–win set to reach a final agreement. This section will focus on the first stage (and the second stage will be elaborated in the next section). 

3.1. The Basics of NSGA-II 

NSGA-II is a nondominated sorting-based multiobjective evolutionary algorithm (Deb et al. 2002). In a multiobjective problem, solution *x*1 is partially better than solution *x*2 if *x*2 is not better than *x*1 in any objective function and *x*1 is strictly better than *x*2 in at least one objective function. If *x*1 is partially better than *x*2, *x*1 is said to *dominate x*2. The optimal solutions to a multiobjective problem are nondominated solutions, also known as *Pareto optimal* solutions. By plotting the sets of Pareto-optimal solutions in the criteria space, we can get *Pareto-front*. In the absence of any further information, one of these Pareto-optimal solutions cannot be said to be better than the other. This demands a user to find as many Pareto-optimal solutions as possible. NSGA-II can find a good spread of solutions with good convergence near the true Pareto-optimal front in most problems. 

In NSGA-II, its selection operator but its crossover and mutation operators is specially designed. Before the selection is performed, the population is ranked based on nondomi nation. By using the crowding comparison procedure used in the tournament selection and the population phase, the nondominated solutions maintains the diversity. And its main procedures are: 

(i) *A fast nondomination sorting procedure*. It sorts out all solutions into different non dominated fronts *F*0*, F*1*,...*. Solutions on the same front cannot dominate each other, and the solutions on a lower front dominate the solutions on a higher front. 

(ii) *A fast crowded distance estimation procedure*. It is used to further rank the solutions on the same dominated front by using measure *crowding distance*. A solution with a smaller value of this distance measure, in some sense, is more crowded than other solutions. 

(iii) *A crowded comparison operator*. Assuming every solution *Xi* in a population has the two attributes of nondomination rank (*i*rank) and crowding distance (*i*distance), the   
 14678640, 2013, 4, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1467-8640.2012.00434.x by University Cattolica, Piacenza, Wiley Online Library on \[25/05/2026\]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License  
586 COMPUTATIONAL INTELLIGENCE 

crowded-comparison operator (≺), which guides the selection process at the various stages of NSGA-II toward a uniformly spread-out Pareto-optimal front, is defined as 

*Xi* ≺ *Xj* ⇔ (*i*rank *\< j*rank) ∨ ((*i*rank \= *j*rank) ∧ (*i*distance *\> j*distance)) 

That is, if two solutions are with different nondomination ranks, the one with the lower (better) rank is preferred; otherwise the one located in a less crowded region is preferred. 

(iv) *Main loop*. Based on the above three points, now we can describe the main process of NSGA-II. Supposing the population size is *N*, initially a random parent population *P*0 of size *N* is created. Then the population is sorted based on the nondomination and each solution is assigned a fitness (or rank) equal to its nondomination level. Next, the usual binary tournament selection, recombination and mutation operators are used to create an offspring population *C*0 of size *N*. The procedure that the *t*th generation evolves is as follows. First, a combined population *Rt* \= *Pt* ∪ *Ct* (which combines parent and offspring populations) is formed. The population *Rt* is of size 2*N*. Then, the population *Rt* is sorted according to nondomination and then each solution is grouped to corresponding nondominated front *Fi* . *F* \={*F*1*, F*2*,...*} is the set of all nondominated fronts of *Rt* . Then the solutions from *Rt* will be included to the new parent population front by front until the parent population is filled. If the size of *F*1 is smaller than *N*, all the solutions on *F*1 are chosen for the new population *Pt*\+1. The remaining members of the population are chosen from subsequent nondominated fronts in the order of their ranking. Thus, the solutions from set *F*2 are chosen next, followed by solutions from set *F*3, and so on. This procedure is continued until no more sets can be accommodated entirely. Then the solutions in set *Fi* are sorted using the calculated crowding-distance in descending order. And then the best solutions needed are chosen to fill all population slots. New population *Pt*\+1 of size *N* is now used for selection, crossover, and mutation to create a new offspring population *Ct*\+1. It is important to note that a binary tournament selection operator is used here but the selection criterion is now based on crowded-comparison operator ≺. Because this operator requires both the rank and crowded distance of each solution in the population, these quantities are calculated while forming the population *Pt*\+1. The step-by-step evolution procedure is then repeated generation after generation until exit criteria are met. 

3.2. Negotiation by Using Distributed NSGA-II 

*3.2.1. Distributed NSGA-II.* Now, we will develop a distributed NSGA-II. In our au tomated negotiation problem, a win–win outcome is a Pareto-optimal solution that cannot benefit one party more without benefiting the other less. Each agent’s benefit is calculated by its own utility function (i.e., its own satisfaction degree on a solution). Thus, the negotiation problem can be viewed as an optimization problem of two parties’ utility functions as ob jectives and thus it seems NSGA-II could be used to solve the bilateral negotiation problem. However, because a negotiating agent would not like to let other sides know its own private information, such as its negotiation strategy and its utility function. Thus, we need to extend the original NSGA-II model to a distributed one. 

Specifically, our distributed NSGA-II works in this way: each agent has an NSGA-II engine to evolve its sets of Pareto-optimal offers by taking its own utility function as one objective function and its opponent’s utility function as another objective function but only knowing the opponent’s evaluation on its offers according to the opponent’s private utility function. This is based on the assumption that the two agents are semi-competitive (Luo   
![][image5] 14678640, 2013, 4, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1467-8640.2012.00434.x by University Cattolica, Piacenza, Wiley Online Library on \[25/05/2026\]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License  
TWO\-STAGE NEGOTIATION:OPTIMIZATION AND CONCESSION 587 

FIGURE 5\. The message flow and the evolutionary processes of agents *b* and *s*. 

et al. 2003). That is, there are both competition and cooperation existing between them. Thus, an agent answers its utility value on offers proposed by its opponent; but it would not unnecessarily reveal its entire utility function. 

*3.2.2. Offer Encoding.* Let offer *o* be a vector of (*a*1*,..., an*) where *ai* is the value of attribute *Ai* . In each negotiating agent, a population of chromosomes is used to represent a subset of candidate offers, with each chromosome representing an offer. Genes of a chro mosome represent the attribute values of a candidate offer. In the evolutionary process, the genetic operators, such as crossover and mutation are applied on these genes. A population of chromosomes, which represents a set of candidate offers, can be represented as{*o*1*,..., oN* }, where *N* is the population size. 

*3.2.3. Negotiation Process.* Now we discuss how the buyer and seller agents use the distributed NSGA-II to find the Pareto-fronts. As shown in Figure 5, each agent conducts an evolutionary process on its own side independently. Initially, each agent creates a random parent population *P*0 \= {*o*1*,..., oN* }, which are denoted as *Pb,*0 for agent *b* and *Ps,*0 for agent *s*. Agent *b* first calculates its own utility value of each offer *oi* in *Pb,*0 and obtains *ub* (*oi*). Then, agent *b* sends all those offers in *Pb,*0 to agent *s*, and asks agent *s* for its utility values of these offers. Receiving these offers, agent *s* calculates its utility values of them, denoting *us*(*oi*) for offer *oi* and sends these values back to agent *b*. Now, for every offer   
![][image6] 14678640, 2013, 4, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1467-8640.2012.00434.x by University Cattolica, Piacenza, Wiley Online Library on \[25/05/2026\]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License  
588 COMPUTATIONAL INTELLIGENCE 

FIGURE 6\. The distributed NSGA-II algorithm to obtain win –win offers. 

*oi* of population *Pb,*0, agent *b* knows its opponent’s utility value *us*(*oi*), and its own utility value *ub* (*oi*). Based on these values, agent *b* computes the fitness values of all the candidate offers in *Pb,*0. Then these offers are sorted based on the nondomination and the usual binary tournament selection, recombination, and mutation operators are used to create an offspring population *Cb,*0 of size *N*, as presented in the previous subsection. A similar procedure also happens at the side of agent *s* to get an offspring population *Cs,*0.   
The details of the distributed NSGA-II is shown in Figure 6\. After generating the offspring population *Cb,*0, agent *b* asks agent *s* again for its utility values of the offers in *Cb,*0. Then, the evolution procedure is repeated generation after generation until a halt criterion is met (as shown in lines 4-11 in Figure 6). In each generation, agent *b* calculates its own utility values of candidate offers of the new reproduced population and also asks agent *s* for its utility values of them. The binary tournament selection based on the crowded-comparison operator ≺, crossover, and mutation are the same as those in NSGA-II. Similar procedures happens at the side of agent *s*. After evolving certain generations, a set of offers, which converges to Pareto-optimal front while maintaining diversity, can be obtained by each agent, say *Pb* for agent *b* and *Ps* for agent *s*. In this distributed NSGA-II, the diversity among nondominated solutions is introduced by using the crowding comparison procedure, which is used in the tournament selection and the population reproduction phase. 

Figure 7 shows an example of initial sets of offers and the win–win outcome sets of the negotiation between agents *b* and *s*, which are obtained by using the proposed distributed NSGA-II. It can be seen that many offers among the initial, randomly generated sets of offers are far from the Pareto-fronts, in the both sides of the buyer and the seller. However, after 50 generations of evolution at both sides, the offers are locating on the Pareto-fronts. Now, each agent obtains a set of win–win offers and it can continue negotiating with its opponent along its own Pareto-front to reach a final agreement. Each agent only knows its own Pareto-front and the two agents do not exchange them after the NSGA-II evolution process. Because all the solutions obtained by NSGA-II are supposed to be convergent to Pareto-fronts, as claimed in (Deb et al. 2002), the set of win–win offers obtained by either the buyer agent or the seller agent is of the same size as the population size set in NSGA-II. 

**4\. BARGAINING ON THE PARETO-FRONT** 

This section will discuss the second stage of our negotiation model. More specifically, we will propose a novel time-dependent concession strategy model first, and then an algorithm   
![][image7] 14678640, 2013, 4, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1467-8640.2012.00434.x by University Cattolica, Piacenza, Wiley Online Library on \[25/05/2026\]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License  
TWO\-STAGE NEGOTIATION:OPTIMIZATION AND CONCESSION 589 

FIGURE 7\. The randomly generated initial set of offers and the resulting offer set evolved after 50 generations of agents *b* and *s* and the negotiation outcome. 

of reaching a final agreement, especially a fair one, from the win–win set found in the first stage of our model. 

4.1. Monotonic and Multistep Bargaining along the Pareto-Front 

First, we recall the basic conception of negotiation protocols and strategies. A negotiation protocol is a set of rules that specify how the agents should interact during the course of a negotiation, while a negotiation strategy determines, according to the negotiation history, which offers should be made or whether or not they accept their opponents’ offers at a certain time of the negotiation process (Jennings et al. 2001; Endriss 2006). A majority of the protocols are of multistep interactions, but there are still a few one-step or one-shot protocols (Rosenschein and Zlotkin 1994). Multistep protocols are usually supposed to allow more efficient outcomes but take more time to complete than the one-shot ones. In the rest of this section, we present a concession strategy model that can be used with a multistep monotonic concession protocol first and then a one-step protocol.   
 14678640, 2013, 4, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1467-8640.2012.00434.x by University Cattolica, Piacenza, Wiley Online Library on \[25/05/2026\]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License  
590 COMPUTATIONAL INTELLIGENCE 

Now, we turn to discuss how buyer agent *b* and seller agent *s* negotiate following a multistep monotonic concession protocol. Let *ua,r* be the reservation utility of agent *a* (*a* ∈{*b,s*}), and *a*− be agent *a*’s opponent agent. The agents *a* and *a*− simultaneously propose offers at times T \={0*,* 1*,...,* min{*Ta, Ta*− }}, where *Ta* and *Ta*− are the negotiation deadlines for agents *a* and *a*−, respectively. Let *ot,b*→*s* denote the offer of agent *b* to agent *s* at time *t*. When agent *s* receives an offer from agent *b* at time *t* (i.e., *ot,b*→*s*), it rates the offer using its utility function *us* and gets *us* (*ot,b*→*s*). If *us* (*ot,b*→*s*) is greater than or equal to the value of the current proposal of agent *s* (i.e., *us* (*ot,b*→*s*) ≥ *us* (*ot,s*→*b*)), then agent *s* accepts the offer at time *t* and the negotiation ends successfully with an agreement. Agent *b* will do a similar evaluation on agent *s*’s offer. When either agent *a* makes a decision to accept its opponent’s offer, an agreement is reached. If both agents accept their opponents’ offer, they can take either offer as the agreement randomly. If neither agent accepts its opponent’s offer, they will either come to next time round *t*  and propose a new offer to each other or quit if the negotiation deadline is reached. Because an agent can always get profit from an agreement on which its utility is above its reservation utility, it prefers to reach an agreement rather than fail to reach any agreement under such conditions. Thus, when coming to the last time round (deadline) of agent *a*, agent *a* will check whether the opponent’s offer is greater than its reservation utility *ua,r*. If it is, agent *a* will accept the offer and the negotiation will end successfully with this offer (i.e., as the final agreement); otherwise, the negotiation process will end with a failure. Thus, the action, **A***a*, that agent *a* takes at time *t*, in response to the offer *ot*−1*,a*−→*a*, is 

**A***a*(*t, ot*−1*,a*−→*a*) \= ⎧⎪⎨   
Quit if *t \> Ta* and *ua*(*ot*−1*,a*−→*a*) *\< ur,a,* 

Accept if (*ua*(*ot*−1*,a*−→*a*) ≥ *ua*(*ot*−1*,a*→*a*− )) ∨ (*t \> Ta* ∧ *ua*(*ot*−1*,a*−→*a*) ≥ *ur,a*)*,* ⎪⎩   
Propose *ot,a*→*a*− otherwise*.* (11) To maximize its own profit, an agent can make an initial offer, denoted as *oa,*0, which   
guarantees it has the greatest utility and then in the following negotiation round it makes concessions according its own strategy. In fact, this is typical for monotonic concession protocols (Endriss 2006). 

If agent *a* decides to proceed into the subsequent round according to (11), it has two options: (*i*) concede from current offer *ot*−1*,a*→*a*− to a new offer *ot,a*→*a*− that might be more preferable to its opponent, with *ua*(*ot,a*→*a*− ) *\< ua*(*ot*−1*,a*→*a*− ); and (*ii*) refuse to concede and stick to current offer *ot*−1*,a*→*a*− . The concession model (which we will discuss in next section) can help an agent to decide whether to stick to current offer or make a concession to generate a new offer. A concession model can help the agent to further decide the desired utility value of the new offer (i.e., *ua*(*ot,a*→*a*− )).   
In this paper, we propose a novel time-dependent concession function, denoted as *R*(*t*), to compute the rate of concession in a specific round during the negotiation process. That is, 

*ua*(*ot,a*→*a*− ) \= *ua*(*ot*−1*,a*→*a*− ) − *R*(*t*) ×  *ua,t*−1*,* (12) where 

 *ua,t* \=

4.2. Various Kinds of Time-Dependent Concession Rate 

Now we detail our novel time-dependent concession strategy. 

*4.2.1. Increasing Concession Rate.* This type of function is used to model that an agent wants to speed up its concession rate during the negotiation process. Let the negotiation deadline be *T* . Then the increasing concession rate function can be given by 

*R*(*t*) \= *a* \+ *b* ×   
 

 *t T* 

*λ*   
*,* (14) 

where *a* ∈ \[0*,* 1\]*, b* ∈ (0*,* 1 − *a*\]*,λ* ∈ (0*,* \+∞). Thus, *R*(*t*) ∈ \[*a, b*\].   
When using the above formula, the user needs to specify his own negotiation deadline *T* , his initial concession rate *a* and his largest amount of increased concession rate *b*, and also his rate, *λ*, of increasing concession rate. These parameters decide the specific shape of the curve of concessions. Figure 8 shows some specific examples of monotonic rate increasing functions that are derived from (14). From the figure, we can see that: (*i*) a larger value of factor *λ* means the agent uses a smaller rate to speed up concession, but in the late stage it increases its concession rate more quickly; (*ii*) a smaller value of factor *λ* means the agent uses a larger rate to speed up the concession rate at the beginning, but in the late stage it increases its concession rate more slowly; and (*iii*) the agent increases its concession rate linearly when *λ* is 1\. 

*4.2.2. Decreasing Concession Rate.* This type of function is used to model that an agent wants to decrease the concession rate during the negotiation process. Let the negotiation deadline be *T* . Then the decreasing concession rate function can be given by 

*R*(*t*) \= *a* − *b* ×   
 

 *t T* 

*λ*   
*,* (15) 

where *a* ∈ \[0*,* 1\]*, b* ∈ (0*, a*\]*,λ* ∈ (0*,* \+∞). Thus, *R*(*t*) ∈ \[*a* − *b, a*\].

FIGURE 9\. Decreasing concession rate. 

When using formula (15), the user needs to specify his own negotiation deadline *T* , initial concession rate *a*, largest amount of decreased concession rate *b*, and also his rate, *λ*, of decreasing concession rate. These parameters determine the specific shape of the curve of concessions as shown in Figure 9\. From the figure, we can see that: (*i*) a larger value of factor *λ* means the agent uses a smaller rate to decrease the concession rate first, but in the late stage it decreases its concession rate more quickly; (*ii*) a smaller value of factor *λ* means the agent uses a larger rate to decrease the concession rate at the beginning, but in the late stage it decreases its rate of concession more slowly; and (*iii*) the agent increases its concession rate linearly when the value of *λ* is 1\. 

*4.2.3. Constant Concession Rate.* This type of function is used to model that an agent wants to keep a concession rate unchanged during the negotiation process. It can be given by 

*R*(*t*) \= *c,* (16) 

where *c* ∈ \[0*,* 1\] is a constant. 

*4.2.4. A General Model for Hybrid Concession Rate.* In previous subsubsections, we have presented three basic types of concession functions. However, agents may want to adopt a hybrid concession strategy. For example, an agent may want to increase its concession rate in the early stage of the negotiation process, then keep a constant concession rate in the middle stage, and at last increase its concession rate till the end of the negotiation. To model this situation, now we give a general function as follows:

In the case of *c* ∈ \[0*,* 1\]*, a* ∈ \[0*, c*\]*, b* ∈ \[−*c,* 0\]*, t*1 ∈ \[0*, T* \]*, t*2 ∈ \[0*, T* \]*,* and *t*1 ≤ *t*2, we call it *mountain strategy*; and in the case of *c* ∈ \[0*,* 1\]*, a* ∈ \[*c,* 1\]*, b* ∈ \[0*,* 1 − *c*\]*, t*1 ∈ \[0*, T* \]*, t*2 ∈ \[0*, T* \]*,* and *t*1 ≤ *t*2, we call it *valley strategy*. The *mountain strategy* means that the negotiating agent first increases its concession rate from *a* to *c* from the beginning to round *t*1, then keeps the rate at *c* till round *t*2, and finally decreases from *c* to *c* \+ *b* till the negotiation ends. The *valley strategy* means that the negotiating agent first decreases its concession rate from *a* to *c* from the beginning to round *t*1, then keeps at

In this case, the agent has a concession rate of 0 during the time between *t*1 and *t*2. That means the agent does not make any concession but sticks to its current offer during this stage. 

This general mixed rate of concession function can be used to model various concession rates, including the three basic ones in previous subsubsections. In fact, from formula (17), we can see (*i*) when *t*1 \= *t*2 \= *T, a* ∈ \[0*,* 1\)*, c* ∈ (*a,* 1\], it becomes increasing concession strategy (14); (*ii*) when *t*1 \= *t*2 \= *T, a* ∈ \[0*,* 1\)*, c* ∈ (0*, a*), it becomes decreasing concession strategy (15) (here \[*c, a*\] is the range for concession rate); (*iii*) when *t*1 \= 0*, t*2 \= *T, c* ∈ \[0*,* 1), it becomes constant concession strategy (16); and (*iv*)–(*iv*) these six variants are shown in Table 1\. 

4.3. Negotiate along the Pareto Fronts 

Our negotiation algorithm for a final agreement from the win–win set is shown in Figure 12 (there the Pareto-optimal set of offers, found by using the distributed NSGA-II, is denoted as *P*). Firstly, as specified in Subsection 4.1, each agent takes the offer with the largest utility value for itself as the initial offer to its opponent (line 1 in Figure 12). As the negotiation process goes on, the agents concede according to their own negotiation strategies. At any round time *t* (lines 3–14 in Figure 12), after receiving offer *ot,a*−→*a* from its opponent *a*−, agent *a* checks whether its utility value of the opponent’s offer is higher than that of its own offer in current round. If yes, it accepts the opponent’s offer; otherwise it rejects the opponent’s offer. If any side accepts its opponent’s offer, the negotiation succeeds with this offer as an agreement; otherwise, they enter into the next round or one agent withdraws from the negotiation as its negotiation deadline passes (and thus the negotiation fails). If agent *a* enters into the next negotiation round, it first computes its utility value of current offer, say *u*, according to function (12) (line 12 in Figure 12). Then, it looks up its Pareto-optimal set *P* for a solution 

*x* \= arg max *x*∈*P,ua* (*x*)\=*u*   
{*ua*− (*x*)} (19) 

and then offers it to its opponent. During the whole negotiation process, either agent has the right to accept or reject its opponent’s offer, and they make their offers according to their own negotiation strategies, without treating any agent differently on the basis of inappropriate criteria such as which agent starts the negotiation first. Thus, the presented bargaining mechanism is a symmetric one. 

4.4. One-Shot Negotiation for Fair Win–Win Outcomes 

In some situations, both buyer and seller sides in negotiation want to reach a fair agreement. So, here we will discuss how to enable both agents to divide the utility equally between them. Formally, a fair win–win agreement, denoting as *A*win−win is an agreement in the win–win set and satisfies: (*i*) for agent *a*, *ua*(*A*win−win)   *ua*− (*A*win−win), and (*ii*) there does not exist an offer, denoted as *o*, such that *ua*(*o*)   *ua*− (*o*) and *ua*(*o*) *\> ua*(*A*win−win) and *ua*− (*o*) *\> ua*− (*A*win−win) simultaneously, where   is *approximately* equal relation that meets |*ua*(*A*win−win) − *ua*− (*A*win−win)|≤  . That is, a fair outcome is the one on which two agents have an approximate utility value. Here,   can be viewed as the *tolerance* degree. As an agent’s utility value on an offer is in interval \[0*,* 1\], for example, we can set the value of   to 0.1 in the experiments in this paper.   
 14678640, 2013, 4, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1467-8640.2012.00434.x by University Cattolica, Piacenza, Wiley Online Library on \[25/05/2026\]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License  
TWO\-STAGE NEGOTIATION:OPTIMIZATION AND CONCESSION 597 

FIGURE 12\. The negotiation process along Pareto-front. 

Now, we turn to discuss how agents reach a fair win–win agreement using one-shot negotiation protocol. Suppose the sets of win–win outcome found by agents *b* and *s* (using the distributed NSGA-II as developed in previous section) are *Pb* and *Ps*, respectively. If both agents agree to find a fair outcome in one-step, each agent will look up some candidate approximate fair offers in its own set of win–win outcomes and then propose them to its opponent. Let the set of candidate fair offers proposed by agent *b* be denoted as *Ob, f* \= {*ob,*1*, ob,*2*,..., ob,m*} and the set proposed by agent *s* be denoted as *Os, f* \= {*os,*1*, os,*2*,..., os,n*}. After exchanging the candidate fair offers, the two agents compute the pair of two offers (*ob , os* ) that have the shortest Euclidean distance, one of which is taken from the set *Ob, f* and the other is from *Os, f* , i.e., 

(*ob , os* ) \= arg min *ob,i*∈*Ob, f ,os,j*∈*Os, f*   
      |*A*|   
*k*\=1 


*ab,i,k* − *as,j,k Vk* − *V k*   
 2 

*,* (20) 

where *A* is the attribute set, *Vk* and *Vk* are, respectively, the upper and lower bounds of the values on the *k*th attribute, i.e., *ab,i,k* is the value of the *k*th attribute of offer *ob,i* , and *as,j,k* is the value of the *k*th attribute of offer *os,j* . Thus, both agents can get a pair of fair offers (*ob , os* ). Then they can compute the offer in the middle of these two offers as the fair agreement. 

4.5. Negotiation Example 

This subsection will illustrate the negotiation process in our accommodation scenario. Assume buyer agent *b* and seller agent*s* take formulas (7) and (10) with *w*price \= *w*period \= 1 as their utility functions, respectively. And they use our distributed NSGA-II to obtain the win–win solution sets *Pb* and *Ps* as shown in Figures 13 and 14\. Then agents *b* and *s* need to negotiate further to reach a final agreement from *Pb* and *Ps*. Assume their reservation utility values are 0.3, and their negotiation deadlines are 20 rounds. Seller’s offer   
Negotiation outcome 

50 60 70 80 90 100   
price 

FIGURE 15\. Offers made by agent *b* and *s* in different time *t* in variable space. 

Then, agent *b* looks up its win–win solution set *Pb* for a potential offer. By formula (19), agent *b* gets an offer of (price*,* period) \= (55*.*8*,* 380\) on which its own utility value is 0.875, and then proposes it to agent *s*. Similarly, agent *s* makes a new offer of (price*,* period) \= (85*.*5*,* 528). Now, still no agent would like to accept its opponent’s offer and thus they continue their negotiation. In round 10, agent *s* finds out that its utility value on agent *b*’s offer (price*,* period) \= (75*.*5*,* 409\) is 0.570 and is greater than its utility value, 0.564, of its own offer (price*,* period) \= (77*.*4*,* 377). Thus, agent *b* decides to accept agent *s*’s offer, and thus they come to agreement at (price*,* period) \= (75*.*5*,* 409). The negotiation process ends up successfully at time round 10\. Figures 13 and 14 show the Pareto-optimal solution set (found in Pareto-front negotiation stage), their offers exchanged and the final agreement in utility space, respectively. Figure 15 shows the information in the space of *price* and *period*. From these three figures, we can see that agents negotiate along the Pareto-fronts throughout the whole negotiation process, which guarantees the final agreement is the Pareto-optimal solution. 

If agents *b* and *s* want a fair win–win agreement, firstly they look up in their own win–win outcomes sets *Pb* and *Ps* for their own fair outcomes, respectively, and then offer to each other, as shown in Figure 16\. By exchanging fair offers with each other, by formula (20), both agents find out that their fair offers converge mostly at agent *b*’s offer of (price*,* period) \= (73*.*1*,* 416\) and agent *s*’s offer of (price*,* period) \= (72*.*8*,* 416). Then they will reach an agreement in the middle of these two offers: the price is 72*.*8\+73*.*1 

the period is 416\+416   
~~2~~ \= 416\. 

**5\. EXPERIMENTAL ANALYSIS**   
~~2~~ \= 73*.*0 and 

This section carries out a series of experiments to investigate the performance of our win–win negotiation model based on our distributed NSGA-II. More specifically, three types of empirical information are sought. The first aims to find a proper set of parameters so that

Buyer’s fair offer Seller’s fair offer Negotiation outcome 

50 60 70 80 90 100   
buyer utility 

FIGURE 16\. Fair offers made by agent *b* and *s* and negotiation outcome. 

the NSGA-II can find Pareto-optimal offers with a good diversity for general utility functions. The second aims to check whether our win–win negotiation model can find fair win–win solutions directly and can reach win–win agreements under different customized negotiation strategies. The third is to test whether our model can outperform the main existing systems of multiattribute negotiation. 

5.1. Parameters of NSGA-II 

Like other genetic algorithms, the performance of NSGA-II is affected by the settings of its parameters of *population size, distribution indexes for real-coded crossover and mutation operators, probabilities of cross-over and mutation,* and *maximum of generations*. In fact, the more solutions can be found with the larger population size and the larger maximum of generations by the algorithm. However, increasing the population size can also bring up more computational burden. Thus, we need to balance between the spread of solutions and the computing complexity in finding a proper size of population and a smaller maximum of generations while maintaining a good spread of solutions. Also, proper crossover and mutation probabilities need to be set. To find a proper set of parameters, we first let the buyer agent *b* use utility function (7) and seller agent *s* use utility function (10), and then test whether a good spread of Pareto-optimal offers can be found at each side with a certain setting of parameters. We do 20 experiments for each combination of parameters of crossover probability (0.5, 0.6, 0.7, 0.8, or 0.9), mutation probability (0.1, 0.2, 0.3, or 0.4), population size (50, 100, 200, or 300), generation (10, 50, 100, 200, 300, 400, or 500), and distribution indexes for real-coded crossover and mutation operators (1, 10, 20, 30, 40, or 50), respectively. Through the experiments, we have found a proper set of parameters as shown in Table 3\. 

Now, we test whether our distributed NSGA-II can obtain Pareto-optimal solutions with a good spread for our utility functions. Without loss of generality, besides the utility functions proposed in this paper, we will also test how our distributed NSGA-II works with other two types of utility functions, which are often used in existing negotiation models. Our test result shows that our algorithm works well with the three types of utility functions,

See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

TABLE 3\. Parameter Settings for NSGA-II. 

Parameter name Value Parameter name Value 

Population size 400 Maximum of generation 50 Crossover probability 0.6 Mutation probability 0.3 Distribution index for crossover 1 Distribution index for mutation 1 

(i) *Compromisation utility function*. The utility functions of agents *b* and *s* are formulas (7) and (10), respectively. 

(ii) *Weighted average utility function*. The utility functions of agents *b* and *s* are as follows: *ub*(*x*1*,..., xn*) \=  *nj*\=1*wb,j*(1 − *x j*)*,* (23) 

*us*(*x*1*,..., xn*) \=  *nj*\=1*ws,j x j,* (24) 

where *x j* ∈ \[0*,* 1\] is a value of attribute *j*, and *wb,j* and *ws,j* are, respectively, the importance preference of agents *b* and *s* on attribute *j*, satisfying  *nj*\=1*wb,j* \= 1 and  *nj*\=1*ws,j* \= 1\. Actually, this pair of utility functions is used in classic multiattribute utility theory (Gerding, Bragt, and Poutr 2003).   
(iii) *Weighted additive square utility function*. The utility functions of agents *b* and *s* are as follows: 

*ub*(*x*1*,..., xn*) \= 1 −  *nj*\=1*wb,j x* 2*j,* (25) 

*us*(*x*1*,..., xn*) \= 1 −  *nj*\=1*ws,j*(1 − *x j*)2*,* (26) 

where the meanings of *x j* , *wb,j* , and *ws,j* are the same as those in (23) and (24). They have been used in some existing negotiation systems (e.g., Lai, Sycara, and Li 2008). 

Now, we show how the distributed NSGA-II negotiation algorithm works with each of these three types of utility functions by using four instances with different weights. Thus, there are twelve pairs of utility functions for testing, as shown in Table 4\. Every time agents *b* and *s* take one pair of utility functions from the above twelve pairs, to negotiate with each other to obtain Pareto-fronts. The experiment results for these three types of utility functions are shown in Figures 17, 18, and 19, respectively. From these figures, it can be seen that for each of these twelve pairs of utility functions, a set of Pareto-optimal offers with a good spread is obtained at each side of agents *b* and *s*. Of course, even if this set of parameters works well for all these twelve utility functions, we cannot conclude that it will work well in all the situations. However, we have done one hundred experiments for each type of utility function by randomly setting the weights of agents *b* and *s* on attributes and have shown that this set of parameters do work well in more than 98% experiments. 

In these experiments, during each Pareto-front negotiation phase, the buyer and seller need to exchange 400 × 50 × 2 \= 40*,*000 bids. It seems a large number here. However, because each negotiation attribute domain is continuous and there are infinite many points in the the solution space, 40,000 bids is still acceptable. Moreover, if we set the population size as 50 and the maximum of generation as 10, which means the number of bids is 50 × 10 × 2 \= 1*,*000, good spreads of Pareto-optimal offers are also obtained in most situations. Because we have tested various kinds of utility function proposed in current literature, we find a population size of 400 and 50 generations of NSGA-II can guarantee good spread in spite

5.2. Performance of Our Win–Win Negotiation Model 

This subsection does many experiments to examine the performance of our negotiation model.

*5.2.1. Performance of Finding Fair Win–Win Outcomes.* To check whether our model works robustly in finding fair outcomes, the negotiation is carried out between 50 pairs of agents using our utility functions (7) and (10) with both buyer’s and seller’s weights on the two attributes are equally set as 1, i.e., *w*price \= *w*period \= 1\. Tolerance parameter   is set to 0.1. From the experiment results shown in Figure 20, we can see that the buyer and seller agents’ utility values of 50 agreements are all very convergent to the utility baseline of 0.5. Thus, we can conclude that our negotiation model can find fair win–win outcomes robustly. 

*5.2.2. Performance of the Bargaining Mechanism.* This section is to show how the negotiation results vary with negotiation strategies. Because our negotiation model is a two-phase one, this section shows how the model works combining the two phases. In this experiment, the utility function of buyer agent *b* is set as formula (7) with *w b* \= (0*.*9*,* 0*.*6), and the one of seller agent *s* is set as formula (10) with *w s* \= (0*.*7*,* 0*.*8). Figure 21 shows the trade-off curves of the two utility functions. The negotiation deadlines for both are set as 20\. And the reservation utility values for both agents are set as 0.3. To check whether the dynamic negotiation process and results are varying with the two agent’s strategies, in the experiment on our model, we let buyer agent *b* and seller agent *s* take a concession strategy from one of the five strategies (as shown in Table 5\) every time, respectively. 

Figure 22 shows the dynamic negotiation processes with respect to different combinations of buyer and seller’s strategies. The offers of buyer *b* and seller *s* are depicted as circles and squares, respectively, and the final agreements are depicted as diamonds. From the figure, we can see that the offers and the final agreements vary with the strategy combination of the buyer and seller agents. For example, in Figure 22, the five sub-figures in the first row show that different agreements are reached when agent *b* bargains with agent *s* that uses one of the five concession strategies shown in Table 5\.

5.3. Comparison Experiments 

The experiments in this subsection will compare our model with four existing negotiation systems to check whether our negotiation model outperforms the others in finding win–win outcomes. 

*5.3.1. Comparison with Ito Model.* Ito, Hattori, and Klein (2007) proposed a multi attribute negotiation model. In the system, each agent generates a fixed number of samples, and then uses an optimizer based on simulated annealing (SA) to find the local optimum for its own utility functions in every sample’s neighborhood. Each agent takes the local optimal points that have larger utility than the agent’s reservation utility of *δ* as the bids, and then submits them to a mediator, along with its utilities of them. Then from all the overlapping points, the mediator selects the final agreement as the one with the highest summed bid   
 14678640, 2013, 4, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1467-8640.2012.00434.x by University Cattolica, Piacenza, Wiley Online Library on \[25/05/2026\]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License  
TWO\-STAGE NEGOTIATION:OPTIMIZATION AND CONCESSION 607 

FIGURE 22\. The negotiation process between agents *b* and *s* with respect to compromising utility function and different concession strategies. 

utility (and thus the highest social welfare). They claim that their method outperforms exist ing methods in large nonlinear spaces such as those found in real world. 

Now, we conduct experiments to check whether our model can outperform Ito model. Because Ito mode is aimed to solve negotiation problems of interdependent multiple issues, we test how it works with our compromising utility functions that model interdependent multiple issues as well. Thus, the buyer agent’s utility function is formula (7) with *w b* \= (0*.*9*,* 0*.*6); the seller agent’s is formula (10) with *w s* \= (0*.*7*,* 0*.*8). The parameter setting of SA algorithm is the same as that in Ito et al. (2007): the SA initial temperature is 50.0 and

decreased linearly to 0 over the course of 2,500 iterations. The initial contract for each SA run is randomly selected. Because the optimality of Ito model is expected to be improved by increasing the number of samples an agent takes of its own utility space when searching bids, we test the performance of Ito model with different number of samples per attribute (i.e., 200, 300, 500, 700, and 900 samples), as set in Ito et al. (2007). Because in our model, the buyer and seller agents exchange 40,000 bids during the Pareto-front negotiation phase, we also test how Ito model works with 20,000 samples per attribute, which means the buyer, seller,

and mediator agents in this model exchange 40,000 bids as well. And the reservation utility value *δ* is 0.1. From the figure, we can also see that the results are affected by the sample size of the SA algorithm. When sample size is 200 or 300, the outcome is a little far from the Pareto-front. When increasing the sample size to 500, 700, 900, and 20,000 the outcomes get closer to the Pareto-front, but still not so close as our outcomes. largest sum of two parties’ utility values from the offers proposed by both agents as the final agreement. However, in a two-objective optimization problem a Pareto-optimal solution does not always have the largest sum of two objectives. The set of outcomes found by our model as shown in Figure 25 is the collection of negotiation outcomes from experiments in subsubsection 5.2.2. From the figure we can see that our model outperforms Ito model in finding win–win outcomes. Moreover, our model can find fair win–win outcomes, which is not solved in Ito model. 

Compared with our model, another deficiency of Ito model is that a third-party mediator is needed in negotiation. However, in real-world it is really difficult for agents to trust a third party. To remedy this problem, Fujita, Ito, and Klein (2010) propose Distributed Mediator Protocol and Take-It-or-Leave-It Protocol for negotiation. Nevertheless, a mediator is still required and thus their new model is still suffering from the same problem as Ito model. 

*5.3.2. Comparison with Lai Model.* Lai et al. (2008) proposed a multiattribute ne gotiation model. By using an alternating-offer protocol, agents exchange their roles as a proposer and a responder every round. In each round, the proposer agent first calculates the utility value it desires to get in this round and then finds the corresponding indifference curve (or surface) of its utility function. Then it selects the points that have the shortest path to the best offer of the opponent in the previous round and proposes them to the responder agent. To reach an agreement easily, the agent proposes a set of offers that have the shortest paths, instead of proposing one only. The responder agent decides whether its own utility value on the offers of the proposer is greater than the utility it expects to get in the next round. If yes, it accepts the offer and the negotiation ends with this agreement; otherwise they go to next round until either agent’s negotiation deadline or an agreement is reached. Agent *i* applies the following formula to concede during the course of a negotiation: 

*dui*(*t*) \= 1 − (1 − *rui*) 

   
 *t Ti*

See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

where *dui*(*t*) is the utility that agent *i* desires to get in current time *t*, *rui* is its reservation utility, *Ti* is its deadline, and *βi* is its strategy parameter. Lai et al. claim that this model can make agents reach near Pareto-efficient agreements effectively (even if agents have general utility functions).

The experimental results are shown in Figure 26, from which we can see that the outcomes of Lai model are not so near to Pareto-fronts as ours. The outcome set of our model shown in Figure 26 is the collection of negotiation outcomes from experiments in subsection 5.2.2. So, our model outperforms Lai model in finding win–win outcomes in the case of compromising utility functions. 

Because in Lai’s paper, weighted additive square utility functions are used, we also need to test Lai model with them. More specially, we use the following utility functions of buyer 

and seller agents:   
*ub*(*x*1*, x*2) \= 1 −  0*.*7*x* 21 \+ 0*.*3*x* 22 (28) *us*(*x*1*, x*2) \= 1 − (0*.*3(1 − *x*1)2 \+ 0*.*7(1 − *x*2)2) (29)   
where *x*1*, x*2 ∈ \[0*,* 1\]. Other settings such as time-dependent strategies, negotiation deadlines, and so on are the same as those in the experiment for compromising utility function. From the experiment results shown in Figure 27, we can see that the outcomes of both our model and Lai model are convergent to Pareto-fronts in case of utility functions (28) and (29). So, our model works as well as Lai model in finding win–win outcomes in the case of weighted additive square utility functions. 

Now, we discuss how the outcomes of Lai model departure the Pareto-fronts. In Lai model, one agent’s offers are the points, on its current indifference curve (or surface), with the shortest distance to the best offer *o*  of the opponent in the previous round. It is supposed that on these points the opponent’s utilities are larger than those not near to offer *o* , and these

For example, in the above experiment, when agent *b* proposes first, as shown in Figure 26(a), in negotiation round 3, agent *s* is in turn to propose an offer. As shown in Figure 28, its current indifference curve has its utility value of 0*.*562\. After searching its current indifference curve, agent *s* finds out the points with the shortest distances to its opponent’s best offer *o*  and proposes them to its opponent. Agent *s thinks* that it has proposed the points on its current indifference curve while giving a higher utility to the buyer. However, from Figure 28 we can clearly see that the point *p* on the seller’s current indifference curve corresponds to a much higher utility for the buyer than those proposed. *5.3.3. Gerding Model.* Gerding et al. (2003) proposed a bilateral multiattribute ne gotiation model. In the model, an evolutionary algorithm is used to evolve the negotiation strategies of the agents. The system starts with two separate, randomly initialized *parental* populations of negotiation strategies, each of which is owned by an agent. Each agent uses its own strategy to negotiate with all agents using the strategies in the other populations. The average utility obtained in all negotiations is the strategy’s fitness. Offspring strategies

are created by randomly selecting an strategy in the parental populations and then mutating its chromosome. Then the fittest strategies from the parental and offspring populations are selected as the new *parents* for the next iteration. Gerding et al. claim that the agents with the evolutionary strategies can reach agreements on the Pareto-front. 

Now, we experimentally compare our model with Gerding model in finding win–win outcomes. In the experiments, the parameters of Gerding model are the same as those in their paper: (i) the population and offspring sizes are both 25; (ii) the initial standard deviation *σi* is 0*.*1; (iii) the number of negotiation rounds is 10; and (iv) all standard derivations are bigger than *εσ* \= 0*.*025\. Gerding et al. claimed that after 100 generations the agreements reached are chiefly Pareto-efficient and the percentage of disagreements is around 0*.*1% after 1*,*000 generations. Thus, we do experiments, by increasing the generation from 100 to 300, 500, 800, 1,000, 1,200, 1,500, 1,800, and 2,000, respectively. When the population size is set as 2,000, the number of bids exchanged during the whole negotiation process is 25 × 2*,*000 × 2 \= 100*,*000, which is larger than our 40,000 bids during the Pareto-front negotiation phase. 

We first examine whether our model outperforms Gerding model with our compromising utility functions (7) (with *w b* \= (0*.*9*,* 0*.*6)) and (10) (with *w s* \= (0*.*7*,* 0*.*8)). The experiment results are shown in Figure 29 and there the outcome set of our model is from the experiments in subsubsection 5.2.2. From the figure, we can see that most of the outcomes of Gerding model are on Pareto-fronts. However, these outcomes barely enter the area where the seller’s utility value is greater than the buyer’s. And some of them are very unacceptable to, for example, the seller agent in Figure 29\. It should be pointed out that in Figure 29(h) because the obtained outcomes are very convergent to each other they seem like one point in the figure. 

Now, we check whether our model outperforms Gerding model with weighted average utility function. Given *x*1*, x*2 ∈ \[0*,* 1\], by formulas (23) and (24) the utility functions for the buyer and the seller are 

*ub*(*x*1*, x*2) \= 0*.*7(1 − *x*1) \+ 0*.*3(1 − *x*2)*,*
