*5.3.4. Esmaeili Model.* Esmaeili and Mozayaninker (2010) proposed a multilateral, multiattribute negotiation model based on Multiobjective Particle Swarm Optimization (MOPSO) algorithm. In their model, an agent maintains a separate negotiation with each opponent. In every multilateral negotiation, each agent identifies the best offer among the incoming offers and an offer with maximal payoff according to its own utility function. Then, for each agent, a population of particles, representing a subset of its feasible offers, is created and the distance between these two offers is measured as part of the fitness function. Each particle is composed of as many fields as the number of issues. In each round, the proposer   
 14678640, 2013, 4, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1467-8640.2012.00434.x by University Cattolica, Piacenza, Wiley Online Library on \[25/05/2026\]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License  
TWO\-STAGE NEGOTIATION:OPTIMIZATION AND CONCESSION 617 

agent runs its MOPSO algorithm to generate an offer. The opponent finds out whether its own utility value on this offer is greater than the utility value it desires to get in the following round. If yes, it takes this offer; otherwise, it proposes a counter-offer that is generated by its own MOPSO algorithm in the next round. Two agents keep exchanging messages until an agreement is reached or the prespecified negotiation deadline is passed. The concession strategy used in Esmaeili model is the same as Lai model (i.e., specified by formula (27)). Esmaeili model is claimed to be more efficient, effective, stable, and convergent than a Pareto-optimal mechanism in real-world negotiations. 

Now, we conduct experiments to check whether our negotiation model outperforms Esmaeili model. In the experiment, Esmaeili model and our model work under the same condition: (i) the buyer agent’s utility function is formula (7) with *w b* \= (0*.*9*,* 0*.*6\) and the seller agent’s one is formula (10) with *w s* \= (0*.*7*,* 0*.*8\) (because no specific utility function is used in Esmaeili model, we just examine how it works with our compromising utility functions (7) and (10)); (ii) the negotiation deadlines for both the buyer and seller agents are 10; (iii) the reservation utility values for both the agents are 0.3; and (iv) for Esmaeili model, the buyer and seller agents’ time dependent conceding strategy factor *βb* and *βs* in formula (27) alter in {0*.*5*,* 1*,* 2} and for our model the same three strategies that are used in comparing experiments with Lai model in subsubsection 5.3.2 are used. The configuration of the MOPSO algorithm parameters for this experiment is the same as those used in Esmaeili and Mozayaninker (2010): (i) the swarm size is 60; (ii) the number of iterations is 100; (iii) acceleration coefficients are 2; (iv) inertia weight varies from 0.9 to 0.4; (v) *V*max is 0.5; and (vi) the coefficient parameters used in fitness function for buyer and seller agents are *SI*(*buyer* ) \= 0*.*7 and *SI*(*seller* ) \= 0*.*4, respectively. Agents in Esmaeili model proposes offers in turn and it is not specified who moves first. Thus, we do experiments in both the ways that the buyer moves first and the seller moves first. The experiment results are shown in Figure 31\. The circles are the negotiation outcomes of Esmaeili model when the buyers propose first, while the squares are its outcomes when sellers propose first. From the figure, we can see that the outcomes of Esmaeili model are approaching to Pareto-fronts as the outcomes of our model. Thus, Esmaeili model and our model are equally good at finding win–win outcomes. However, it can also be seen that the outcomes of Esmaeili model when buyers propose first are sometimes largely different from its outcomes when sellers propose first. Thus, Esmaeili model is not symmetric because its outcomes depends on who takes the first move in the negotiation. Our model does not suffer from this problem. 

Another problem of Esmaeili model is that an agent cannot specify its own reservation utility, which results in that the negotiation may become very unacceptable for its users of, for example, the seller agent as shown in Figure 29(a), (b) and (c). Compared with our model, one more deficiency of Esmaeili model is that it does not enable the negotiating agents to find fair win–win outcomes. 

*5.3.5. Experiment Conclusion.* In this section, with respect to the three types of utility functions we did lots of experiments to compare the four existing negotiation systems with our model. A character of our model is that the buyer and seller agents may need to exchange large number of bids during the Pareto-front negotiation phase. Thus, we also study how the Ito model and Gerding model work with the same number of bids or even larger number of bids. As to Lai model and Esmaeili model, because the number of bids exchanged in these models is only affected by the negotiation rounds, we cannot study whether and how their performance can be improved by increasing the number of bids. As summarized in Table 6, we can conclude: (*i*) only our model enables agents to find fair win–win outcomes directly; (*ii*) both our and Esmaeili models can find win–win outcomes, but Esmaeili model cannot specify reservation utility value, and more seriously sometimes their outcomes are

unacceptable and depend on which side takes the initial move in the negotiation, which is not very practical because no side wants to be at a weak position; and (*iii*) a third-party mediator is required in Ito model but our, Lai’s, Gerding’s and Esmaeili’s models. **6\. RELATED WORK** 

Against the related work, this section discusses two main contributions of this paper: (*i*) our concession strategy model, and (*ii*) our two-stage win–win multiattribute negotiation model. 

6.1. Time-Dependent Concession Strategies 

Faratin, Sierra, and Jenning (1998) presented a time-dependent concession strategy, which is employed in many negotiation models (e.g., Lau, Tang, and Wong 2004; Ros and Sierra 2006; Lai and Sycara 2008; Esmaeili and Mozayaninker 2010), as follows: 

*ua*(*t*) \= 1 − (1 − *rua*) 

   
 *t Ta* 

 1*βa,* (30) 

where *rua* is the reservation utility of agent *a*, *Ta* is its negotiation deadline, and *βa* is the factor deciding the curves of decreasing utility during the negotiation process. That is, agent *a* uses this formula to calculate the desired utility value at time *t*. The curves of decreasing utility for different values of *β* are shown in Figure 32\. From the figure, we can see the value of *β* decides the shapes of decreasing utility, reflecting a agent’s concession rate. There are mainly the following three categories: (*i*) When 0 *\<β\<* 1, the rate of utility decrease is increasing. That is, the agent concedes small at the early stage of the negotiation, but when approaching to the deadline, it does largely. In particular, when *β* is very close to 0, it concedes extreme small at the beginning; but when approaching to the deadline, it concedes to its reservation utility rapidly. (*ii*) When *β\>* 1, the rate of utility decrease is decreasing. That is, the agent makes concessions largely during the early stage, but when approaching to the deadline it does small. In particular, when *β* is extreme large, the agent decreases   
 14678640, 2013, 4, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1467-8640.2012.00434.x by University Cattolica, Piacenza, Wiley Online Library on \[25/05/2026\]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License  
620 COMPUTATIONAL INTELLIGENCE 

its desired utility value very close to its reservation utility at the beginning, and thus in the following time, there is little space for the agent to concede any more. And (*iii*) when *β* \= 1, the rate of utility decrease is constant. That is, the agent decreases its desired utility values at a constant amount of concession. 

From the above analysis, we can see that their time-dependent concession strategy cannot model that an agent wants to first make some small concessions, then stick to its current offer for a period, and at last concede again (this is a special case of our *valley* strategy (18)). This often happens in real-world negotiations, especially when a participant thinks that he is in a dominant position in the negotiation. For example, when many students compete to rent only several available rooms, the landlord can make a good deal for himself by first making small concessions and then sticking there for a while because he expects the tenant to concede gradually. However, because he always wants to make a deal, if an agreement has not been reached but his house will become available soon, he begins to make concessions again to avoid no tenants after the house is available. More specifically, suppose the landlord has a fixed rental period of four weeks, thus the tenant and landlord only need to negotiate the rent rate (between 100 and 200 pounds) per week. Thus, the landlord’s utility function can be given by *us* \= price −100   
~~100~~ and the tenant’s utility function can be given by *ub* \= 200−price   
~~100~~ . Suppose   
their reservation utility values are both 0*.*3 (i.e., the tenant’s reserved price is 170 pounds and the tenant’s reservation price is 130 pounds). The tenant is eager to make a deal with the landlord, thus he decreases his utility value by 0*.*1 to put a price for the accommodation every negotiation round and he sets his negotiation deadline at round 7\. Suppose the negotiation deadline for the landlord is round 11\. And in round 7, the tenant proposes his last offer that is best for the landlord. We call this example as *example 1*. Consider another example, in which the negotiation deadline set by the tenant is round 9\. The tenant decreases his utility value by 1−0*.*3   
~~9~~ every round. And in round 9, he proposes his last offer to the landlord. We call this example as *example 2*.   
To examine the results when an agent cannot adopt a valley strategy, we test the negoti ation outcome by setting the different values of *β* in formula (30). And we also test how a *valley* strategy models in this situation. The specific *valley* strategy *S*val we use here is given by

The negotiation paths for different values of *β* and our *valley* strategy are shown in Figure 33\. The left sub-figure shows the paths for example 1 and the right one shows the paths for example 2\. 

(i) In case of *β\>* 1, the landlord concedes larger at the early stage. In example 1, the two agents meet in or before round 6\. The agreed price is not less than 130 pounds, and at most equal to the tenant’s offer at round 6 if they come to round 6\. Thus, the landlord at last gets a price of at most 160 pounds. In example 2, the two agents meet in or before round 7\. The agreed price is not less than 130 pounds, and at most equal to the landlord’s offer at round 7 if they come to round 7\. Thus, the landlord at last gets a price of at most 170 pounds.

last gets a price of at most 170 pounds. In example 2, the two agents meet in or before round 8\. Thus, as analyzed above, the landlord at last gets a price of at most 162 (i.e., 200 − 100(1 − 1−0*.*3   
~~9~~ × 8)) pounds.   
(iii) In the case of 0 *\<β\<* 0*.*5, the landlord makes smaller concessions at the early stage. In example 1, the two agents fail to reach an agreement because when the landlord makes concession decisions, he never considers its opponent’s current offer. In example 2, if 0*.*25 *\<β\<* 0*.*5, the two agents meet in or before round 9 and the landlord gets the best profit that he could get in this example; if 0 *\<β\<* 0*.*25, the two negotiators fail to reach an agreement. 

Thus, using time-dependent concession strategy (30) with parameter *β*, the landlord can only obtain the largest profit by getting the highest price (i.e., 170 pounds) at most in one of the two examples. While using our *valley* strategy (31), he can obtain the largest profit in the both examples. That is, using our strategy, he first lowers his offer to solicit the opponent to concede, and then sticks there for a higher price offered by the tenant. From these two examples, we can see that without a proper strategy, he might lose the profit he could get, and thus obviously the human user would not delegate the negotiation task to such a computer system (Luo et al. 2006). However, the problem does not happen if the system employs our strategy instead of strategy (30). 

Among the four negotiation systems experimentally in Section 4, Lai model and Esmaeili model use time-dependent concession strategy (30), but which cannot model the valley strategy, either; while Ito model and Gerding model do not use any concession strategy. 

Besides the time factor, there are other two factors that affect upon concession as well. The first one is *eagerness*. Sim and Choi (2003) defined eagerness as an agent’s interest in coming to a deal. In their work, the value of eagerness is pre-defined, by the user, as a constant. Ren, Zhang, and Sim (2009) extended the concession factor of eagerness by considering changes in the negotiation environment. The second one is*tit-for-tat*. Faratin et al. (1998) also propose *behavior dependent tactics* to compute the next offer based on the previous attitude of the opponent: *relative tit-for-tat*, *random absolute tit-for-tat*, and *averaged tit-for-tat*. These tactics are different in types of imitation human negotiators perform. Somefun et al. (2004) presented a *tit-for-tat* strategy in which an negotiating agent simply makes the same amount

See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License

of concessions as its opponent has conceded in the last round. In addition, Lai and Lin (2004) proposed two kinds of concession strategies with respect to fuzzy constraint relaxing: the fixed concession rate strategy and the response-based concession strategy. 

The comparison of our novel hybrid concession strategies with them is shown in Table 7\. 

6.2. Win–Win Negotiation 

Many researchers studied multiattribute negotiation recently and focused on how to use *heuristics* approaches to find solutions under the incomplete knowledge about the negotiation space and their opponents. For example, Faratin et al. (2002) proposed a heuristic model of the trade-off strategy as well as an algorithm that enables agents to make trade-offs among multiple attributes. Their empirical evaluation demonstrates the algorithm’s effectiveness in generating trade-offs in a range of negotiation contexts. However, all these are based on the assumption of linear utility. Coehoorn and Jennings (2004) explore and evaluate the use of kernel density estimation (a statistical method of finding structure in data sets without the imposition of a parametric model) and apply it to Faratin’s trade-off model to make the negotiation outcome more efficient for both participants. However, their algorithm is restricted to linear utility. Our model in this paper has no this limitation. 

Luo et al. (2003) developed a fuzzy constraint based model of multiattribute negotiation. Their model can guarantee a win–win outcome if it exists, while minimized the exposure of private information to each other. In addition, by incorporating the notion of a reward into their negotiation model, the agents could sometimes reach agreements that would not be possible. However, in their model, time factor is not put into considered in negotiating, and agents cannot divide gains fairly. All these limitations are removed in our model in this paper. 

To make trade-offs in multiattribute negotiation, learning techniques are also used to help agents learn its opponent model of the attribute preferences. For example, Hindriks and Tykhonov (2008) use Bayesian learning to let an agent learn its opponent’s preferences from bid exchanges by making some assumptions about the preference structure and rationality of the bidding process. However, this Bayesian learning approach is only applicable to agents that have linearly additive utility functions. Our model works well for both linear and nonlinear utility functions.   
 14678640, 2013, 4, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1467-8640.2012.00434.x by University Cattolica, Piacenza, Wiley Online Library on \[25/05/2026\]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License  
TWO\-STAGE NEGOTIATION:OPTIMIZATION AND CONCESSION 623 

Genetic algorithms are also employed to learn opponents’ preference in automated ne gotiation. Choi, Bragt, and Poutr (2001) proposed a genetic-agent-based negotiation system, in which agents learn the opponent’s preferences by observing the counter-offers and adapt to the environment by dynamically modifying its mutation rate. Their model can maximize both the side’s payoff. However, their utility functions are assumed to be in the form of   
*u* \=  *i fi,wi* . That is, each attribute is assumed to be mutually independent. However, it is not always this case in real world. And they have not shown whether or not their model works with other utility functions.   
Fatima, Wooldridge, and Jennings (2004) present a model for multiattribute negotiation under time constraints in an incomplete information setting. The issues to be bargained over can be associated with a single good or multiple goods. In their agenda-based model, the order, in which issues are bargained over and agreements are reached, is determined endogenously, as part of the bargaining equilibrium (optimal outcome). In this context, they find the conditions under which agents had similar preferences over the implementation scheme and the conditions under which agents have conflicting preferences. Their game theoretic analysis shows the existence of equilibrium even when both players have uncer tain information about each other, and each agent’s information is its private knowledge. They also study the properties of the equilibrium solution and determine conditions under which it is unique, symmetric, and Pareto optimal. However, as other normative theory based analysis, they fail to advise the course of actions that a negotiator can follow to reach the optimal outcome in real-world negotiations. This problem does not exist in our model. 

There are also some models of one-shot negotiation that can achieve Pareto-efficient outcomes. An example of this kind is the one developed by Ragone et al. (2006), in which agents need to reveal their preferences to a third party and utility of each issue are assumed to be mutually independent. However, our model does not account on a third party during the negotiation. 

Similarly to the models of Gerding et al. (2003), Ito et al. (2007), Lai et al. (2008), and Esmaeili and Mozayaninker (2010), there are also some other win–win negotiation models. Lopez-Carmona et al. (2010) present, based on a region-based recursive bargain ing mechanism, a nonmediated bilateral multiissue negotiation model under nonmonotonic preference spaces. Their preliminary experimental evaluation shows their approach may obtain approximate Pareto-optimal results in acceptable negotiation time with a low failure rate. Unfortunately, their experiments cannot make it clear whether it can be competent in bilateral negotiations with a high quality. Snchez-Anguix et al. (2010a, 2010b) propose an evolutionary-aided negotiation model for nonmediated bilateral bargaining with complex utility functions. In their model, a genetic algorithm is used to sample one’s own utility function before negotiation. During a negotiation process, genetic operators are applied over the opponent’s and one’s own proposals to sample new proposals that are interesting for both parties. Their experiments show that their model outperforms the similarity heuristics only in sampling before the negotiation but the results obtained are similar results to the similarity heuristics that have access to all of the possible offers, as what have to be done in Lai model. However, because an agent in their model uses the shortest distance as the criterion to select offers to propose to its opponent, the same problem may appear as that in Lai model, which we have analyzed when comparing our model with Lai model in Section 5\. And the comparison experiment results have shown that our model outperforms those four models of Ito, Lai, Gerding, and Esmaeili. 

In short, based on our distributed NSGA-II, without a mediator our model can always generate win–win agreements, even fair ones; but the above existing ones cannot.   
 14678640, 2013, 4, Downloaded from https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1467-8640.2012.00434.x by University Cattolica, Piacenza, Wiley Online Library on \[25/05/2026\]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License  
624 COMPUTATIONAL INTELLIGENCE 

**7\. CONCLUSIONS AND FUTURE WORK** 

Based on evolutionary multiobjective optimization, this paper proposed a two-stage model of win–win negotiation for tackling the challenge of negotiation with multiple in terdependent attributes and thus nonlinear utility functions. In our model, agents need not to disclose their own utility functions to others nor a third-party. Our model also supports dividing the gain fairly between agents, which is addressed little in existing work. Moreover, we propose a family of time-dependent concession strategies, which can cover various con cession strategies in real-world negotiations, including many that existing work uncovered. Our experiments show firmly that our model can effectively find win–win outcomes in both linear and nonlinear domains and substantially outperforms existing work. The experimental results also show that our win–win negotiation model is stable and efficient in finding fair win–win outcomes. 

However, when consider finding fair win–win outcomes, we have not studied the relation ship of our fair outcome with the classic Nash solution (Nash 1950\) and Kalai-Smorodinsky bargaining solution (Kalai and Smorodinsky 1975). It should be an interesting further work in the future. Another direction of our future work is to extend our bilateral win–win ne gotiation model on a single object to the situation where an agent negotiates with multiple parties to acquire a bunch of objects, which are not independent to each other. For example, in grid computing, to execute a scientific workflow, a resource consumer needs to negotiate with multiple resource providers to acquire resources for executing every node in the work flow. These nodes are not independent (e.g., their execution may be sequential or parallel). A node may have to wait for other nodes to complete execution before it can start. Thus, the negotiations with multiple resource providers for the node should not happen without considering each other. So, we cannot extend our bilateral negotiation model to a multilateral one by paralleling the negotiations over multiple issues directly. We should consider how to coordinate the multiple interdependent negotiations to achieve global win–win outcomes.

DEALING WITH  DIFFICULT PEOPLEFREE REPORT 
Diagnose their motivations 

We’ve a%% met t(em: negotiators who seem to prefer competition over  collaboration, stonewalling over problem solving, and tough talk over active  listening. Perhaps it’s the boss who refuses to allow you to take time o) to help  out an ailing parent. Or the potential customer who hands you a nonnegotiable  dra\* of the deal she wants. Or the neighbor who responds with anger when you  ask whether anything can be done about his dog’s nighttime barking. 

When dealing with a counterpart who won’t give us what we want, we tend  to write him o) as di+cult or irrational. Yet negotiation scholars point out that  few people are truly irrational. Rather, there are times when each of us has  motivations that others have trouble identifying. Before you walk away from the  table—or, even worse, make a concession that you know won’t be reciprocated— consider spending some time exploring the possible motivations behind your  counterpart’s obstinance. Below, we identify three types of hard bargainers and  o)er advice on getting through to each of them.  

1\. The accidental hard bargainer. Seasoned negotiators understand that  mental shortcuts can impair our most important decisions without our awareness.  In the *Negotiation* newsletter, we show you how to reduce the ill e)ects of subtle  cognitive biases on your thinking, including overcon,dence, egocentrism, and the  tendency to escalate commitment to a chosen course of action. We also explore  how emotions a)ect our decisions—for example, carrying over from one event to  a)ect an unrelated negotiation.  

In his December 2005 *Negotiation* article “Beware Your Counterpart’s Biases,”  Harvard Business School professor Max H. Bazerman points out that when it  comes to reaching durable, value-creating agreements, recognizing our own biases  is only half the battle. We also need to be alert for biases in our counterparts. 

 1   
PROGRAM ON NEGOTIATION 

Suppose that a manager would like to take a week’s vacation to visit his  mother, who is recovering from a sudden injury. A\*er expressing his concern,  the manager’s supervisor explains with regret that he cannot grant the impromptu  vacation time because the department is in the middle of its busy season. When  the manager o)ers to work as much as he can during the trip, the supervisor  expresses doubt that he would get much done. “I’m really sorry,” he says, “but the  answer is no.”  

.e boss in this situation is making the mistake of taking a win-lose  approach to the negotiation. Instead of brainstorming possible solutions that  meet both parties’ interests, he assumes that the employee’s interests are  incompatible with those of the company. In most negotiations, of course, more  creative outcomes are within reach.  

How can you help a counterpart be less biased? First, Bazerman recommends  that you give the other party plenty of time to negotiate. When facing time  pressure, we tend to rely on /awed snap judgments. Taking a break from a heated  discussion relieves tensions and gives parties time to think more rationally.  Second, model good negotiating behavior by suggesting possible tradeo)s  that would allow each party to get what he wants. .ird, avoid inducing tough  statements from your counterpart, lest she feel backed into a corner.  

.e manager in this dilemma might model good negotiating behavior  with a statement such as “I’m con,dent there’s a solution that meets our shared  interest in performing well during this busy time while also allowing me to take  

care of my family. If you have time to meet again this a\*ernoon, I’ll be ready with  a few ideas.” .e break allows the manager to brainstorm options, such as taking  a shorter trip than planned and working overtime in the days before and a\*er  the trip. With any luck, his boss should be open to treating the request as a  negotiation rather than a unilateral decision. 

2\. The reluctant hard bargainer. Sometimes a negotiator’s tough stance can  be chalked up to constraints or interests of which you are unaware, write Deepak  Malhotra and Max H. Bazerman in their book *Negotiation Genius: How to  Overcome Obstacles and Achieve Brilliant Results at the Bargaining Table and  Beyond* (Bantam, 2007).

\-   
PROGRAM ON NEGOTIATION 

Take the case of a potential customer who hands you a long “nonnegotiable”  dra\* agreement. Trying to explain that negotiation is standard industry practice  or touting your company’s superior track record is unlikely to get you very far.  A\*er a short discussion, you might ,nd yourself walking away from the table or,  if you are desperate for business, accepting a bad deal wholesale.  

Instead, try probing the interests behind your counterpart’s excessive  demands. “I think it’s great that you have high standards because we do, too,” you  might say. “Why don’t you tell me about what is keeping you from negotiating,  and maybe we can move on from there.” 

She should then be willing to open up about some of the motivations behind  her demands. Suppose she explains that her superiors have instructed her to reach  an agreement within a week to meet upcoming production requirements. “I don’t  have time for extensive back-and-forth,” she says. “I need to wrap this up and get  someone on the job ASAP.”  

“Your deadline shouldn’t be a problem,” you assure her. “Let’s try to make  some headway this a\*ernoon. If you don’t feel we’ve accomplished enough, you  can explore other options.”  

Your responsiveness could increase your appeal as a long-term business  partner in addition to opening up a true negotiation.  

Other hidden constraints your counterpart could be facing include advice  from lawyers, the fear of setting a dangerous precedent, a tight budget, lack of  authority, and commitments to other parties, write Bazerman and Malhotra. Do  your best to show that you care about helping your counterpart negotiate within  these constraints—or, better yet, see if you can help overcome them.  

On a similar note, a hard bargainer may have hidden interests that he  has failed to disclose. He might think your opening o)er is unfair, he might  have been o)ended by something you said or did, or he might be competing  with colleagues to bring home the best deal. By asking questions designed to  investigate the range of your counterpart’s interests, you can discern if you are  capable of meeting them.

 0   
PROGRAM ON NEGOTIATION 

3\. The intentional hard bargainer. At times you will encounter negotiators  who believe that hard bargaining is the most e)ective strategy. .ey may attempt  to manipulate you by using displays of anger, hurt feelings, or even threats of  violence to get what they want, writes Northwestern University professor Leigh  .ompson in her book *\!e Mind and Heart of the Negotiator* (Pearson Prentice  Hall, 2005). .ompson tells the story of AOL’s advertising chief Myer Berlow  playing with a large knife during a 2003 negotiation with Amazon.com CEO Je) Bezos. When displeased by another AOL executive who was attending the meet ing, Berlow reportedly threatened to stab him.  

Manipulative negotiators are o\*en quite e)ective at convincing their  opponents to agree to their demands. We tend to assume that we will be strong in  the face of a tough negotiating partner, but research by Kristina A. Diekmann of  the University of Utah and Ann E. Tenbrunsel of Notre Dame University shows  that we are actually likely to back down in the heat of the moment.  

When dealing with a di+cult person, you need to tread carefully. If modeling  good negotiating behavior doesn’t work, Massachusetts Institute of Technology  professor Lawrence Susskind has several recommendations.  

First, clarify to the other party that you can be pushed only so far. “If you  can’t be more /exible, it might be time for you to explore other o)ers,” you  might say.  

Second, to get a reality check, include other members of your organization in  the negotiation and encourage your counterpart to bring members of his as well.  Finally, summarize each negotiation session and send the memo to interested  parties—a tactic that will put your partner on notice that others are monitoring  his actions and statements. If your partner still refuses to cooperate a\*er you have  taken these steps, it is probably time to give up and move on.  

**By Katherine Shonk, Editor, *Negotiation Brie\!ngs*.**  

**Adapted from “What Type of Hard Bargainer Are You Facing?”**  

**First published in the August 2011 issue of *Negotiation Brie\!ngs*.** 

1   
PROGRAM ON NEGOTIATION 

Get past no 

Ma3ty S5en7e wa9 %:;;in; :ff (i9 7:m5ute3 on Friday a\*ernoon and  eagerly looking forward to picking up his family to head to their lakeside home  for the weekend. His boss suddenly appeared and said, “Marty, I need you to  ,nish the Delcourt proposal so it’s on their desks ,rst thing Monday morning.  I’ve got to catch a plane. No problem, right? I know I can count on you.” 

Spence quickly calculated that it would take most of the weekend to ,nish  the proposal. Everyone else had already le\*, and his boss was headed for the  door. .e job would be dumped in his lap if he didn’t say something fast. He was  furious; this wasn’t the ,rst time his boss had asked him to take care of a problem  he should have handled himself. What should he do? 

As William Ury, author of *Getting Past No: Negotiating with Di"cult People* (Bantam Books, 1991), explains, we all have to negotiate at times with di+cult  people. .ey might be stubborn, arrogant, hostile, greedy, or dishonest. Even  ordinarily reasonable people can turn into opponents: A teenage daughter can  be charming one moment and hurl insults at you the next. Your boss can be  collaborative and understanding most of the time but make unreasonable  demands on a Friday a\*ernoon. 

Holding your ground. Dealing with di+cult people can be challenging, and  doing it e)ectively calls for special skills. 

In *Getting Past No*, Ury describes his ,ve-step strategy for dealing with hard  bargainers and di+cult people. He calls his method “breakthrough negotiation,”  a way to “change the game from face-to-face confrontation into side-by-side  problem-solving.” (See the sidebar “Breakthrough Negotiation.”) 

When his boss demanded his help, Marty Spence’s ,rst impulse could have  been to strike back. “You’ve had three months to work on this proposal, and I’ve  asked several times if you needed help. I’m not giving up my weekend plans to  bail you out at the eleventh hour.” If he chose this path, he would be standing up  for himself but possibly jeopardizing his relationship with his boss. Alternatively,  he could have caved in and said, “Sure, you can count on me.” .en he would have 

 2   
PROGRAM ON NEGOTIATION 

had to face his disappointed family and deal with his own anger at having been  unfairly used. 

Another option would have been to try to engage his boss in joint problem  solving. “You’ve got a plane to catch, and I’m headed out the door to pick up my  family. It’s important that I be there on time. I’d like to help you. I wish I had  known about this earlier. Let’s see what ideas we can come up with.” .is response  acknowledges the boss’s predicament—he has a plane to catch—while establishing  that Spence has his own commitment. It suggests that together they may be able  to come up with a solution (e.g., bring in someone else to help, each cut their  weekend short by a half day, submit an incomplete report, or tell the client the  report will be delivered at the end of the day on Monday). 

When you need to just say no. Sometimes, even with joint problem solving,  you need to convey a ,rm and clear “no.” No, you won’t work all weekend. No,  your household budget cannot a)ord a new Jaguar. No, your assistant can’t work  from home two days a week. No, it’s not acceptable that your supplier’s delivery  will be a month late. How do you say no while still preserving the relationship? 

In his book *\!e Power of a Positive No: How to Say No and Still Get to Yes* (Bantam, 2007), Ury suggests sandwiching the no between two “yeses.” First, say  yes to your own interests and needs. .en say no to the particular demand or  behavior. Finally, say yes as you make a proposal. 

In the case of the assistant wanting to work from home, you may learn about  her interests and still decide that they aren’t compelling enough for you to agree to  her request. You ,rst explain your *interest*: “I want to have our team here working  together and sharing ideas. I value your contribution and need you to be part of  that team.” .en comes the *no*: “I understand your concerns about the long  commute, but I’ve decided that you can’t work from home two days a week.”  Finally, a *proposal*: “We can talk about having you work from home occasionally,  and we can talk about arranging your hours di)erently so you avoid peak  commuting hours. Or we can discuss reassigning you to a di)erent job where  it’s not as important for you to be here physically.” 

Facing the challenge. It can be extremely challenging to stand up to di+cult  people who may have an arsenal of weapons, including ridicule, bullying, insults,  deception, and exaggeration. In some cases, they might attack you; in others, they 

?   
PROGRAM ON NEGOTIATION 

might avoid confrontation. Sometimes you are taken by surprise; at other times,  there might be a chronic problem you need to address. 

For example, if your ex-husband regularly belittles you in front of your  children, don’t just trade insults. Find a time when you can have a real  conversation without interruption. Let him know how his remarks make you feel.  Encourage him to talk about why he says these things. Ask questions, and make  him feel heard. .en discuss your shared interest in the children’s happiness. 

Whenever possible, prepare in advance for di+cult negotiations. First of all,  know yourself. What are your hot-button issues? What is essential to you? What is  unacceptable? Next, think about what you are likely to hear from your opponent  and plan how you might react. 

Consider the following golf analogy. Jack Nicklaus says that every golfer  should regularly take a lesson that focuses on basics such as grip and alignment,  because if your setup is sound, there’s a decent chance you’ll hit a reasonably  good shot. Similarly, every skilled negotiator should do a prenegotiation  inventory. Ask yourself, What are my goals? What is my strategy? What is my  walkaway point? Like the proper setup in golf, if you plan your negotiation with  focused preparation, you improve your chances of ending up with a good outcome. 

Build a golden bridge. Once you have brought your di+cult opponent to  the table, you may need to build a “golden bridge,” Ury’s term for letting your  opponent save face and view the outcome as at least a partial victory. Even when  your boss comes into your o+ce on Friday a\*ernoon with an inconsiderate  request, you need to say no in a way that conveys your respect for him as your  boss. And you want your assistant to feel that you appreciate her contributions,  even if you can’t agree to let her work at home. Finally, you want your ex-spouse  to know that you value his parenting, even while you ask him to stop belittling  you for the good of the children. 

So how do you help your di+cult opponent save face, while still standing  up for yourself? Ury suggests reframing the problem so that you draw your  opponents in the direction you want them to move. By way of example, he relates  a story told by ,lmmaker Steven Spielberg, who was relentlessly bullied by an  older boy when he was about 13 years old. Spielberg ,gured he couldn’t beat 

 7   
PROGRAM ON NEGOTIATION 

the bully at his game, which was to use physical force, so he *changed the game*,  inviting him to play a war hero in a movie he was making about ,ghting the  Nazis. As Spielberg describes it, “I made him the squad leader in the ,lm, with  helmet, fatigues, and backpack. A\*er that, he became my best friend.” 

.is illustrates a key concept: involve your opponent in ,nding a solution.  It’s unlikely that a di+cult person is going to accept your proposal fully, no  matter how reasonable it is. Give him some choices: Would you prefer to meet at  your o+ce or mine? I could either pay a lump sum or make payments over time;  which is better for you? 

Hostage negotiators look for ways to build rapport and let the hostage takers  save face, with the hope that the hostage takers will become more reasonable.  .e negotiators listen attentively to what the hostage takers want, whether it’s an  apology, a conversation with a loved one, a cup of co)ee, or an acknowledgment  of their grievance. .e negotiators take careful notes, hoping to ,nd something  that will give them leverage. 

Similarly, you should pay careful attention to your opponent, realizing  that some of her needs may be unstated. A business owner who won’t engage  in problem solving to help close a deal to sell her business may turn out to have  deep-seated ambivalence about selling. Realizing that, you might structure the  deal as a joint venture, with a role for her in the new company. 

Listen to learn. If there is a common denominator in virtually all successful  negotiations, it is to be an *active listener*, by which Ury means not only to hear  what the other person is saying but also to listen to what is behind the words.  Active listening is something frequently talked about but rarely done well; it is  a subtle skill that requires constant, thoughtful e)ort. A good listener will  disarm his opponent by stepping to his side, asking open-ended questions, and  encouraging him to tell you everything that is bothering him. Beyond that, Ury  says, “he needs to know that you have heard \[and understood\] what he has said.”  So sum up your understanding of what he has said and repeat it in his own words.  

Ury points out that there is a big payo) for you: “If you want him to  acknowledge your point, acknowledge his ,rst.” And you may ,nd you have little  choice but to do this—how else to avoid a stalemate?

A   
PROGRAM ON NEGOTIATION 

You don’t have to like them. Dealing with di+cult people does not mean  liking them or even agreeing with them, but it does mean acknowledging that  you understand their viewpoint. 

Lakhdar Brahimi, a United Nations special envoy to Afghanistan in the  a\*ermath of the September 11 terrorist attacks, was given the daunting task of  negotiating with warlords and others who had caused many deaths, to try to  create a stable government. He spoke of the need to negotiate with di+cult  people: “.e nice people are sitting in Paris… To stop ,ghting, you’ve got to talk  to the people who are doing the ,ghting, no matter how horrible they are… If I  don’t want to shake their hands, I shouldn’t have accepted this job.” 

Whether you’re negotiating with someone who is dangerously angry or  only mildly annoying, the same skills are helpful in getting the results you want.  Find out what your opponent wants and begin to build a case for why your  solution meets her needs. If you’re successful, you can turn your adversaries into  your partners. 

Breakthrough Negotiation 

In *Getting Past No*, William Ury outlines ,ve steps for negotiating with a  di+cult opponent, whether it’s a boss, coworker, customer, salesclerk, or spouse. 1\. Don’t react: Go to the balcony. When someone is di+cult, your natural  reaction might be to get angry—or to give in. Instead, take yourself mentally to a  place where you can look down objectively on the dispute and plan your response.  Anytime you ,nd your hot buttons getting pushed, try “going to the balcony.” 2\. Disarm them by stepping to their side. One of the most powerful steps to  take—and one of the most di+cult—is to try to understand the other person’s  point of view. Ask questions and show genuine curiosity. 

3\. Change the game: Don’t reject—reframe. You don’t have to play along with  a di+cult person’s game. Instead of locking into a battle of will or ,xed positions,  consider putting a new frame on the negotiation. 

4\. Make it easy to say yes. Build a golden bridge. Look for ways to help your  opponent save face and feel that he’s getting his way, at least in some matters. Using  objective standards of fairness can help create a bridge between your interests.

 B   
PROGRAM ON NEGOTIATION 

5\. Make it hard to say no. Bring them to their senses, not their knees. Use your  power and in/uence to help educate your opponent about the situation. If she  understands the consequences and your alternatives, she may be open to reason. 

**By Susan Hackley, Managing Director, Program on Negotiation at Harvard Law School.  Adapted from “When Life Gives You Lemons: How to Deal with Di\!cult People.”**  

**First published in the November 2004 issue of *Negotiation Brie\!ngs*.**  

Tame the hard bargainer 

Dea3 Ne;:tiati:n C:a7(: I’m about to face o) with an “old school” negotiator  whose reputation for hard bargaining precedes him. I know I’m supposed to adopt  a collaborative approach for the best results, but what about when the other side is  

being di+cult? I’m afraid that if I’m too nice, I’ll get taken to the cleaners. Robert Bordone: Although I would caution you against adopting an  unconditionally cooperative approach to negotiation, there are steps you can  take to transform a potential zero-sum competition of wills into an interaction  that is aligned toward problem solving—even with the hardest bargainer.  First, beware the common tendency to equate being collaborative with  being nice. .ere’s nothing inherently wrong with being nice, but niceness is not  the point of mutual-gains negotiation. Rather, a collaborative approach is more  of a bargaining stance than a personality style. Some negotiators view their  counterparts as competitors with whom they must spar. Others jointly identify  the issues up for discussion and work together to address them. It’s possible to be  nice or less than nice when you’re taking either approach.  

 Second, examine your assumptions about the hard bargaining you expect  to face. Consider that the other party may have a policy of acting di+cult, or he  may be unaware of the damage he’s doing. Regardless, when you try to collaborate  with him, you may feel you’re stuck between either conceding or reverting to an  old-school game of haggling. In most cases, this perceived either-or choice is a  false one. 

One e)ective antidote to troublesome behavior is active listening. Active  listening doesn’t mean waiting patiently for the other side to end a rant or  nodding and saying, “I understand, but… ”. Instead, active listening entails 

1C   
PROGRAM ON NEGOTIATION 

proactively interrupting the other party to paraphrase what he has said, asking  follow-up questions to better understand confusing assertions, and acknowledging  the highly charged emotions that may lurk below the surface. When done well,  active listening can tame the hardest bargainer—which is why it’s a central  component of hostage- and crisis-negotiation training (see cover story). 

To further move beyond the “,ght back or give in” mentality, try to change  the focus of a negotiation with a hard bargainer from positions to interests.  Consider the example of a buyer who is demanding an extended payment  schedule that the seller believes to be unreasonable. .e two parties haggle over  this single issue for months. Finally, a breakthrough: .e seller asks the buyer to  explain her desire for an extended payment schedule. .e conversation changes  the fundamental thrust of the negotiation from zero-sum to problem solving. In  the process, the parties identify a creative way to resolve the issue—by having the  seller collaborate with the buyers’ accounts payable department to streamline its  internal approval process.  

When negotiators claim they’ve “tried everything,” that usually isn’t the  case. .e next time you’re facing a di+cult negotiator, employ these strategies.  If all else fails, consult with a negotiation coach who can bring a fresh perspective  to the situation. 

**By Robert C. Bordone, "addeus R. Beal Assistant Clinical Professor of Law, Harvard Law School.  Adapted from “Dear Negotiation Coach: Taming Hard Bargainers.”**  

**First published in the September 2008 issue of *Negotiation Brie\!ngs*.**  

10 more strategies  

Je99i7a t3ieD t: 9tif%e (e3 E:y. Charlie, the majority owner of the boutique  hotel she managed in Miami, had just told her he wanted to sell the business to  her. “I’m ready for a new challenge,” he said from his usual seat at the bar, where  he had spent the evening drinking wine and doing Sudoku puzzles. “Can we make  a deal?” 

A\*er acquiring the hotel from his ex-wife during their divorce, Charlie had  put himself on salary as the marketing manager. Since he hired Jessica to manage  the hotel six years ago, its reputation and bookings had steadily climbed. Charlie 

 11   
PROGRAM ON NEGOTIATION 

was nice enough, but Jessica had grown increasingly frustrated with his habit  of blocking key improvements and neglecting his duties. He was holding the  business back, she believed. 

Jessica knew the deal was possibly a once-in-a-lifetime opportunity. Charlie  o)ered to be bought out at a bargain price, stressing that she was the only person  he’d entrust with the hotel. Nervous and excited, Jessica spent the next month  lining up an investor. Lawyers wrangled over the details for another month. 

.e night before the deal was to be inked, Charlie called Jessica over to his  bar stool. “I have a bad feeling about your investor,” he said. “I’m withdrawing  my o)er. I’m going to rededicate myself to the business. Shall we pop open  some champagne?” 

Infuriated and crushed, Jessica stopped just short of quitting on the spot. She  knew she needed to think through the situation before making any rash decisions. We’ve all faced the unpleasant task of negotiating with people we view as  irrational, incompetent, and downright aggravating. When such individuals say  no to a bene,cial deal, walking away may be the path of least resistance. Yet giving  up could mean accepting a less desirable outcome. 

What can you do when a di+cult person is the main obstacle to a  promising deal? 

.ere are a number of strategies you can use to bring a negotiation back from  the brink of failure. We present 10 suggestions here, in an order that you can alter  according to the nature of your negotiation. 

1\. Set standards of behavior. Discuss acceptable norms of behavior with a  potentially di+cult counterpart before you negotiate, advises Stanford University  professor Stephen John Stedman, who has studied “deal spoilers” in the context of  global peacemaking initiatives.  

Such norms can help you judge the legitimacy of the other party’s demands  and behaviors. If you think tempers could rise, for example, you might agree to  listen respectfully to each other and to not raise your voices. Or if you suspect  someone could get cold feet at the last moment, you might pledge to discuss ways  to save the deal before walking away from the negotiating table.

1-   
PROGRAM ON NEGOTIATION 

By suggesting to Charlie that they confer with each other regularly while  dealing individually with third parties, such as lawyers and potential investors,  Jessica might have headed o) his last-minute surprise. 

2\. Avoid dismissive labels. Too o\*en, we label anyone standing in the way of  our goals as irrational, stubborn, or worse. Such judgments can limit our options  and result in costly strategic errors. 

Even if you feel certain that someone’s behavior is foolish, destructive, or  downright crazy, acknowledge that he is acting out of very human concerns and  emotions. It’s your job to ,nd out what they are. 

A\*er Charlie rescinded his o)er, Jessica was  tempted to dismiss him as capricious and sel,sh.  But she knew Charlie to be lazy, not /ighty. She  wondered if perhaps there was more to the story. 

3\. Take the pressure off. Time pressure can  cause negotiators to say no to a deal when it would  be in their best interest to say yes. For this reason,  be sure all parties to an agreement have ample  opportunity to consider proposals and contract  dra\*s. Calling for a break gives everyone time to  make smart decisions and can head o) an escalating  war of words. 

Jessica speculated that Charlie was trying to  revoke their deal because he was nervous about    
When Someone Says No:    
A Three-Pronged Approach  

The strategies we’ve described for dealing  with a potential spoiler fall into a broader  three-part strategy: 

■ Work with the other party. Explore the obstructionist’s interests and emotions; create  and present a variety of proposals; and be  candid about your own feelings and concerns. 

■ Work around the other party. Build a  coalition that will help you influence the  deal blocker or move forward with your  plans without her assistance. 

■ Work without the other party. Pursue  relationships and opportunities outside  the current deal with the goal of creating  an appealing alternative.

signing the contract the next day. She considered the possibility that he needed  more time to get used to the idea of leaving the hotel.  

“I can’t stop you if you want to call o) the deal,” she said to him, “but  remember that decisions have consequences. How about if we meet tomorrow— just the two of us, no lawyers—and talk about what’s best for us and for the hotel.” 

.is suggestion had a calming e)ect on Charlie, who agreed to meet the next  day to discuss his sudden reversal. 

 10   
PROGRAM ON NEGOTIATION 

4\. Probe the other side’s point of view. How can you ,gure out the motives  behind someone’s seemingly stubborn position? Begin by questioning her about  the problem she is trying to solve. Deal blockers may be held back by ,nancial,  legal, personal, or other constraints you don’t know about, according to Harvard  Business School professor Deepak Malhotra. A tough stance could also  communicate a psychological need that isn’t being satis,ed. 

At their meeting the next day, Jessica asked Charlie to explain the “bad  feeling” he had about her ,nancier, an investment banker who was a longtime  customer at the hotel’s restaurant. 

As it turned out, Charlie had been o) ended by the banker’s hard-bargaining  style. “He wouldn’t return my calls, and his lawyer kept trying to lowball me,” he  said. “I refuse to do business with someone who doesn’t take me seriously.”  

Jessica knew Charlie had held lingering grudges against others for far lesser  slights. Instead of trying to persuade him to accept her investor, she decided to  examine Charlie’s change of heart about his own role in the hotel. “You told me  you were ready for a new challenge,” she said. “But now you say you’re ready to  work hard here. What’s going on?” 

Charlie hedged a bit and then admitted he was no more enthusiastic about  ful,lling his marketing duties than he had been before. “I just want to make sure  you stay in charge,” he said. “And, frankly, I’m not really sure what I want to do  next. It’s a little scary to think of leaving this place.”  

5\. Put forth multiple proposals. Jessica was gaining a clearer picture of Charlie’s  reason for canceling the sale. A\*er their meeting, she drew up three proposals: ■ Together, they could meet with her investor to talk through his and  Charlie’s di)erences in negotiating style and assess whether a deal was  still possible. 

■ Jessica could try to ,nd another investor to buy out Charlie, though he  would have to agree to some ground rules on timing and selection criteria  before she agreed to this option. 

■ Jessica would stay on at the hotel with Charlie as owner if he would agree  to let her farm out his duties to outside ,rms and give her a stake in the  business, with annual percentage increases based on performance.

11   
PROGRAM ON NEGOTIATION 

Sometimes people will block deals simply to get your attention. By developing  several proposals that meet your interests well and that also address the other side’s  needs, you convey the important message that you’ve been listening. 

6\. Be ready to walk. Note that Jessica’s package of proposals contained an  implicit threat: she would quit her job if nothing changed. She was tired of  working for someone who blocked progress.  

.reats and punishment may be necessary when you’re negotiating with  people who rigidly adhere to all their demands, according to Stanford’s Stephen  John Stedman. 

You must be prepared to follow through on your threats, of course. .at’s  why it’s crucial for you to *research your best alternative to a negotiated agreement*,  or BATNA, at the same time you’re negotiating your preferred deal. 

For Jessica, this meant quietly putting out feelers with her industry contacts  during the time she was negotiating with Charlie. She became con,dent that  she would be able to ,nd a comparable job without too much trouble, though it  would be di+cult to match the day-to-day decision-making power that Charlie  gave her. 

7\. Share your feelings. When someone calls o) a deal at the last minute,  don’t assume she’s deliberately trying to hurt you. It could very well be that she’s  preoccupied with her own interests and hasn’t thought about how her decision  will a)ect you. 

For this reason, not only do you need to give potential deal spoilers a chance  to vent, but you also need to articulate your own frustrations constructively. By  doing so, you can encourage the other party to understand your perspective and  guide her toward more collaborative behavior. 

“Your decision about the investor was a huge letdown for me,” Jessica said to  Charlie before presenting her proposals. “Running my own hotel is my dream,  as you know, and I feel as if you haven’t thought about how changing your mind  a)ects me.” 

Charlie apologized for putting Jessica through the wringer and promised to  work with her toward a mutually bene,cial solution. “I guess we’re both feeling  disrespected,” he said. “I’ll try to do better.”

 12   
PROGRAM ON NEGOTIATION 

8\. Weigh the benefits of a concession. Another option for dealing with  di+cult negotiators is to cra\* what Harvard Law School professor Robert C.  Bordone calls a “workaround”—a strategy for meeting your current goals without  the involvement or support of your adversary. 

You might be able to induce a yes with a tempting concession on a key issue,  according to Bordone. O)ering a concession can be a dangerous strategy, as it  may only encourage someone to push for more. But if a concession would allow  you to move beyond that person once and for all, it may be your best option. 

Jessica, for instance, might try to convince Charlie to get past his bad  feeling about her investor by raising more money, whether from the same investor  or other sources, and o)ering Charlie a higher price for his shares. Running  the hotel without Charlie’s interference might be worth the short-term cost of  securing extra funds. 

9\. Build a coalition. Another workaround technique is to build coalitions  that will in/uence the deal blocker in your favor. By enticing a recalcitrant party  to follow in/uential others on a particular course, coalitions exploit *patterns of  deference*, according to Harvard Business School professor James Sebenius. 

To build a coalition in support of your desired outcome, make a list of those  who have an interest in a potential deal, and consider how they might in/uence  the spoiler. Next, ,gure out the best sequence in which to approach these parties.  Finally, present your case to these key individuals. 

Jessica contacted two people who had invested in the hotel in its early days  and still owned a small stake: a cousin of Charlie’s ex-wife and the hotel’s ,rst  manager. Both were interested in selling their shares to Jessica for the right price.  Jessica hoped that showing Charlie that other buyers saw value in selling their  shares would convince him to join them.  

10\. Accept no for an answer. Badgering someone into accepting a deal is  never a good idea, even if you’re sure it would be in her best interest. Not only can  coercion be unethical and even illegal, but also a dissatis,ed counterpart could  sabotage the deal during implementation. If you’ve exhausted the strategies above  and the other party still won’t say yes, it’s time to move on.  

In the end, Charlie continued to balk at selling all his shares in the hotel to  Jessica. However, he was receptive to one of her other proposals. He agreed to 

1?   
PROGRAM ON NEGOTIATION 

give her 15% ownership of the hotel, a percentage that would increase over time  based on her job performance. He also promised to grant her even more decision making authority, to turn over his marketing duties to outside professionals, and  to cut his salary. 

A\*er talking through the o)er with several trusted advisers, Jessica decided  it was her best option at this point in her career. She and Charlie drew up a new  contract, and she began looking at him as a partner rather than an obstacle. 

**By Katherine Shonk, Editor, *Negotiation Brie\!ngs*.**  

**Adapted from “Bring Your Deal Back from the Brink.”**
